rationales.self_direction: He ignored the client's requested design and built "what would actually work," prioritizing his own technical judgment over prescribed process.
rationales.achievement: He finished the module "ahead of schedule" and prepared benchmark numbers to defend the stronger solution, signaling competence and performance focus.
rationales.conformity: Using a token flow instead of "the session model they wanted" is a direct departure from the expected specification.

rationales.self_direction: He values that he can "pick my own projects" and rejects the family's idea that success requires going back to "a real company."
rationales.achievement: He cites concrete markers of competence and success, including matching his brother's income and building an open-source tool used in "four countries."
rationales.conformity: He left the conventional telecom path and resists a family framework in which office titles are the only legitimate measure of doing well.

rationales.self_direction: The thought of fixed office hours makes his "chest tighten" because he wants control over when he starts, breaks, and works best.
rationales.conformity: He rejects the standard "9 to 5" office pattern as "a stupid way to work," showing clear resistance to imposed work norms.

rationales.self_direction: He is ready to leave rather than accept a structure that does not "fit how you actually function well," prioritizing autonomy over accommodation.
rationales.security: He contemplates exiting even though "The money would be tighter for a couple months," accepting instability to preserve how he works.
rationales.conformity: He pushes back on management wanting to "see bodies in the building" and refuses to treat that corporate norm as legitimate.

rationales.security: He carefully runs the numbers, notes the income drop is "not comfortable but not catastrophic," and focuses on confirming the rate before deciding.

rationales.self_direction: He says he "cannot make myself take the easier path when it means someone else deciding how I spend my days," making autonomy central to the choice.
rationales.security: He acknowledges the family does not have "a fat cushion right now" yet still leans toward the riskier path, showing willingness to compromise stability.

rationales.self_direction: He turns down the contract because the on-site demand "does not fit how I operate," explicitly choosing a work structure aligned with his own way of functioning.
rationales.achievement: He negotiates a "performance bonus clause" on the replacement project, showing an active effort to secure results and recognition for performance.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects the expectation to stay visibly busy and says he would rather deliver results and "have a life outside," prioritizing his own terms for work.
rationales.hedonism: He takes the half-day for an omakase he and Jun had wanted for months and says the meal left him "genuinely recharged."
rationales.conformity: He dismisses the older-office norm that commitment means hours at the desk and refuses to keep "pretending to look occupied" after finishing his work.

rationales.self_direction: He insists "I work differently" and wants confidence in a path that does not depend on his father's approval.
rationales.hedonism: He explicitly wants to be good at work and "still have energy left for everything else," refusing a life defined by exhaustion and sacrifice.
rationales.achievement: He plans to "keep proving myself over time" through promotions while maintaining reasonable hours, tying success to demonstrated performance.
rationales.conformity: He resists his father's expectation that seriousness means "staying late" and sacrificing for the company in the old model.

rationales.hedonism: She is depleted after class and frames the degree as a "selfish detour," emphasizing strain and self-denial rather than present enjoyment.
rationales.benevolence: She immediately tells Lisa "I'd come up this weekend to give her a break" and is preoccupied with not being there for her mother and sister.
She spends the weekend doing "Mom's laundry," sitting with her, and leaving Lisa "enough in [the] freezer for a week," all centered on supporting close family.

rationales.self_direction: She prioritizes her own judgment over institutional expectations, saying "Some things are more important than a Zoom call" and refusing to apologize.
rationales.conformity: She knowingly skipped a required group meeting and says "I'm not going to apologize for being there," resisting the expected deference to program norms.
rationales.benevolence: Missing the meeting was specifically because she was "in Duluth taking care of my mother," showing support for a close family member.
Her attention is fixed on Tyler's stress and on whether to "step back," showing how strongly she takes on the emotional burdens of someone close to her.

rationales.hedonism: She describes not knowing "how to exist without someone to take care of" and fears burnout because she never learned "how to sit still," which conflicts with ease and enjoyment in the present.
rationales.benevolence: The entire reflection centers on lifelong caregiving, from making lunches in fourth grade to choosing social work because she feels most herself when "giving to someone else."

rationales.hedonism: After a breakdown she pushes herself to "actually be productive" and immediately turns to obligations and errands rather than rest or comfort.
rationales.achievement: She finishes the chapter, completes discussion questions, and proactively confirms January practicum hours, showing task-focused progress.
rationales.security: She calls the memory care coordinator, discusses the assisted living opening, and notes that making a list helps her feel "calmer," all aimed at restoring predictability and stability.
rationales.benevolence: Her to-do list and phone calls are organized around easing pressure on Lisa, managing Mom's care, and handling Tyler's needs.

rationales.security: The situation is framed as financially unsustainable and unstable: Lisa "can't afford" to take leave and the degree "doesn't pay anyone's bills yet."
rationales.benevolence: Her immediate reaction is guilt about family caregiving, saying "I should be the one doing this" when Lisa considers leaving work to care for Mom.

rationales.security: She and Lisa "made a plan," set a schedule for visits, and agreed to explore assisted living and speak with the doctor, all to create a more stable path forward.
rationales.benevolence: The call centers on supporting family, from committing to come "every other weekend" to being moved by Tyler offering to come to Duluth too.

rationales.self_direction: She insists "I'm here because I've lived this" and reframes her personal history as legitimate fuel for her path, defending an internally chosen motivation.

rationales.security: Touring the assisted living wing, noting the staff and private room, and planning to raise it with the doctor show active steps toward safer, more stable care.
rationales.benevolence: She brings Tyler, coordinates with Lisa, and returns home to keep doing ordinary care work like laundry and packing lunch for Monday.

rationales.achievement: At the start of the semester she reports feeling "ready" and "present," signaling renewed engagement with her studies rather than avoidance or drift.

rationales.self_direction: She rejects the "sensible" relocation path and says "I'm not ready to leave yet," choosing to continue the PhD on her own terms.
rationales.achievement: Finishing is framed as "proof that it was worth it," showing strong investment in demonstrating competence and making the struggle count.
rationales.security: She turns down a package with salary, housing, and school fees covered, while dwelling on savings and uncertainty, which undercuts stability.
rationales.benevolence: She recognizes that Arjun has been "carrying us financially for four years" and that the move would help the family, yet still chooses the path that keeps that burden in place.

rationales.achievement: She "submitted the revised chapter draft" that morning, a clear step forward on her doctoral work.
rationales.benevolence: The same day includes picking Meera up, shopping for vegetables, and making dinner, showing practical care for close family.

rationales.achievement: Meera's note saying "Amma working hard" motivates her to keep going, and the moment is explicitly tied to persistence in her work.

rationales.self_direction: She closes the Chennai job email and says the uncertainty of her current path feels "more honest," choosing the life she actually wants over the one expected of her.
rationales.security: She turns away from a "Permanent role, government pension" and a fully mapped-out future despite admitting her stipend is low and savings are nearly gone.
rationales.conformity: She refuses the role that would satisfy her mother, Appa, and in-laws, explicitly rejecting "someone else's version of my life" and the pressure to settle down.

rationales.self_direction: She insists "my timeline is when I finish" and refuses to let family pressure dictate when she stops.
rationales.achievement: She frames quitting as "giving up something I've worked four years on" and treats completion as the relevant standard, not other people's impatience.
rationales.conformity: She resists coordinated pressure from Amma and Arjun and declines to adopt the timeline others want from her.

rationales.self_direction: She affirms the value of "doing work you've chosen, even if nobody else sees why you chose it," explicitly centering an internally chosen path.
rationales.achievement: Her supervisor says the chapter is strong and a conference presentation would be good for the CV, and she feels she finally knows what she's doing.
rationales.security: She openly notes that "the money is bad" and "the timeline is uncertain," highlighting the instability built into the path she is continuing.

rationales.self_direction: He has been "teaching myself upholstery techniques" and building a furniture business outside a traditional office role, shaping his own route.
rationales.stimulation: He emphasizes constantly picking up "skills" and mastering upholstery techniques, showing attraction to challenge and growth rather than routine.
rationales.achievement: He wants to "list all the things I've done," notes two new orders, and compares his current pace to life at the firm, all focused on competence and accomplishment.

rationales.self_direction: He is independently redesigning the workshop and wants to take on restoration challenges he "can't even attempt right now," showing self-directed expansion of his craft.
rationales.stimulation: The most satisfying part was the "technical part," including three tries and hours of research to solve a demanding restoration problem.
rationales.achievement: He spent forty hours restoring the sideboard, took pride in knowing "it was good work before she saw it," and is already planning for bigger pieces.

rationales.hedonism: She said yes to Korean BBQ but was already thinking about "how much marking I need to clear," letting work crowd out anticipated enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: She stayed until "7pm revising the slides" after feedback that her lesson needed tighter pacing, showing strong effort to improve her performance.
rationales.benevolence: Her attention shifts away from time with Arjun toward marking, suggesting work is taking priority over a close relationship.

rationales.achievement: She keeps investing extra labor in lessons, noting she is still "cutting out laminated shapes at 6:30" because she wants to do the job well.
rationales.conformity: After her mentor's criticism, she settles on "I'll focus on pacing next term," showing willingness to adapt herself to established expectations.

rationales.hedonism: She cancelled the Christmas market, a birthday dinner, and a spa day because work kept winning over rest and pleasure.
rationales.achievement: She "insisted on writing personalized comments for all 32 students" and stayed until 9pm, prioritizing high standards despite exhaustion.
rationales.benevolence: Repeatedly cancelling on Arjun, her cousin, and friends shows close relationships being neglected under work pressure.

rationales.stimulation: The holiday includes a night market and a day trip to Malacca to try a famous dish, showing active enjoyment of novelty and change of scene.
rationales.hedonism: She describes "lazed around," reading a novel on the swing seat, eating good food, and finally feeling better after stepping away from work.
rationales.benevolence: She spends relaxed, attentive time with Arjun and his family, playing cards with his grandma and enjoying the family gathering.

rationales.self_direction: She notices the pressure to check email but decides, "I'm going to close the laptop. Tomorrow is soon enough," asserting her own boundary against the work script.
rationales.hedonism: She explicitly wants to "protect this feeling a little longer" and chooses rest over anxious work-checking.

rationales.self_direction: She pushes back on the expected career ladder, asking, "Why can't I just be good at teaching?" and wanting to define success on her own terms.
rationales.achievement: She rejects the recognized-success frame of "visibility," titles, and portfolio-building, saying that matters less than what happens with Dinesh in class.
rationales.conformity: She resists "what you're supposed to say" and feels strained by the system's expected path rather than comfortably accepting it.
rationales.benevolence: Her clearest source of meaning is Dinesh "finally starting to participate in class," showing priority on a student's growth over status.

rationales.self_direction: She frames teaching around what is "the truest thing I know about myself right now," choosing an internally defined purpose over institutional metrics.
rationales.achievement: She explicitly says the meaningful part is "not for reviews or promotions or what it looks like on paper," distancing herself from recognition-based success.
rationales.benevolence: She singles out encouraging Aisha and treasures "a 9-year-old discovering she can create something beautiful," centering care for a student's development.

rationales.hedonism: Even during brunch and a walk with Arjun, she "couldn't stop calculating how many hours until Monday," showing inability to stay with pleasure in the present.
rationales.achievement: Her distress is tied to whether she is "doing enough" and to the ideal of being an "effortlessly great teacher," reflecting strong performance standards.

rationales.tradition: She keeps the novena "circled on my calendar since January" and repeats her grandmother's explanation about honoring the dead, embracing inherited ritual as meaningful.
rationales.benevolence: She teaches her third graders to make paper flowers for the altar and describes family filling the house, showing active care for close community ties.

rationales.self_direction: She wants "space to figure that out without the weight of everyone's expectations" before deciding what she wants, signaling a push to think for herself.
rationales.security: She avoids telling the truth because she wants "more time" and fears the consequences of family scrutiny, choosing emotional safety.
rationales.conformity: She keeps up socially acceptable stories with both Carlos and her mother to avoid upsetting expectations and maintain harmony.

rationales.tradition: She spends the evening preparing for the novena and notes she has set up chairs "every year since I was fourteen," showing steady commitment to ritual continuity.
rationales.benevolence: Helping Abuela cook, setting up for prayers, and joining family in the kitchen all reflect support for close relatives and community.

rationales.self_direction: She admits she "didn't say what I was really thinking" in the meeting, suppressing her own view rather than voicing it.
rationales.security: Her preferred response was to "pilot it in one classroom first," showing caution and a desire to reduce disruption.
rationales.conformity: She stayed quiet because she didn't want to be "the young teacher defending old ways," restraining herself to avoid social friction.
rationales.tradition: She defends "old methods," invokes Sister Magdalena, and says "ritual matters," explicitly valuing continuity with established practice.
She rejects the idea that staying means being stuck, saying "The roots here are mine," which frames attachment to place and continuity as something worth preserving.
rationales.benevolence: She stays because "My grandmother is eighty-three" and "My students know me," emphasizing responsibility to familiar people around her.

rationales.self_direction: She says she doesn't know how to write a different script "and still feel like myself," showing difficulty authoring her own path outside inherited expectations.
rationales.conformity: She is torn by becoming "the version who disappears herself to keep everyone comfortable," which centers preserving others' comfort.
rationales.tradition: Her conflict is organized around "the script" of her parents' church-sanctioned marriage and the wish to remain inside that inherited pattern.

rationales.security: Sitting in the pew where her grandmother always sits and finding that "today that felt like comfort instead of a cage" shows the environment providing steadiness and reassurance.
rationales.tradition: Confession, Father Miguel's guidance, and the familiar church setting all show her turning toward religious continuity to make sense of her life.
She keeps the same poster her mother gave her in her first year and decides the worn corners should remain, valuing continuity over replacement.

rationales.self_direction: Her response centers on "telling the full truth and accepting whatever comes after," choosing an honest, self-authored life over continued compartmentalizing.
rationales.security: She is prepared for a "difficult Sunday dinner" and her grandmother's disapproval, showing willingness to give up emotional safety.
rationales.conformity: She rejects "performing okay-ness" and says she must stop managing everyone else's feelings to keep appearances smooth.

rationales.self_direction: She brings Carlos to lunch despite fear and says she must "stop hiding the pieces that don't," asserting a more honest way of living.
rationales.security: Walking in with "hands shaking" and enduring tense silences shows she accepts instability rather than protecting herself from it.
rationales.conformity: Introducing Carlos despite likely disapproval breaks the smoother, expectation-preserving path she had followed before.
rationales.tradition: She frames the moment as "bringing my life to the tradition honestly," integrating her relationship with family and faith rather than abandoning those ties.
rationales.benevolence: The whole scene is an effort to connect Carlos with her family and care for important relationships instead of keeping them separated.

rationales.hedonism: She spent her day off lying on the beach, wanting to "stay horizontal and feel the sun on my face," and savored the relief of not thinking about the hospital. The entry centers immediate rest, comfort, and enjoyment.
She dwells on sensory pleasure, calling the food "crispy and perfect" and saying her stomach hurts "in the best way possible." She also frames the evening's value as simply "good food, good company."

rationales.self_direction: She resists family pressure and says she wants to "rest without having to justify my choices to anyone," signaling a desire to live on her own terms. The tension comes from others trying to define the life she should want.
rationales.conformity: She recoils from the expectation to endure family questioning about marriage and why she is "wasting my best years," rather than embracing those social pressures. The entry shows strain with meeting others' expectations.
rationales.tradition: The family gathering represents conventional expectations around marriage and life path, and she explicitly does not want to submit to them. Her resistance to those familiar scripts puts her at odds with inherited norms.
rationales.benevolence: Even while exhausted, she spent time "holding her mother's hand while the doctors explained options," offering direct comfort during a crisis. That is concrete care for someone vulnerable and close in the moment.

rationales.self_direction: She insists on defining commitment for herself, rejecting Fatima's judgment and reaffirming, "I swore I would never let a job steal my life from me." The entry is about refusing an externally imposed model of how she should live.
rationales.hedonism: She defends making room for her own wellbeing, saying she will not let work "steal my life" and rejecting the idea that self-care is selfish. Preserving enjoyment and aliveness is a clear priority.
rationales.security: Watching her father have a heart attack made her promise not to let work consume her the same way. That vow is rooted in protecting her future health and stability from a known threat.
rationales.conformity: She pushes back against a workplace norm that glorifies total sacrifice and refuses to accept that caring for herself makes her lesser. The conflict comes from rejecting pressure to fit the expected mold.
rationales.benevolence: She describes staying late to comfort a frightened child and holding a mother's hand for hours, showing sustained care for people in distress. Those actions go beyond routine duty into personal support.

rationales.stimulation: She eagerly booked a trip to Muscat and is "counting down the days," showing excitement for a change of scene and a fresh experience. The appeal comes from novelty and getting away.
rationales.hedonism: The trip is framed around a resort and an "infinity pool overlooking the Gulf," with anticipation focused on leisure and enjoyment. The entry is plainly about pleasure and indulgent rest.

rationales.self_direction: She explicitly rejects the idea that constant productivity should define the trip, telling Sara that "rest is doing something." She is asserting a chosen way of living rather than submitting to the usual script.
rationales.stimulation: The weekend included travel to Muscat, exploring the fish market and souk, and taking in a different setting. The entry reflects enjoyment of a change in place and experience.
rationales.hedonism: She celebrates lying by the pool, ordering room service, and watching the sunset, then insists that enjoying the moment counts. The whole entry prioritizes comfort, beauty, and present pleasure.
rationales.achievement: She pushes back against the pressure to always be "productive" or feel guilty for not "doing more." That is an explicit rejection of productivity-centered striving as the standard to live by.
rationales.benevolence: She wants Sara to release her guilt because "maybe she won't burn out the way I almost did," showing active concern for her friend's wellbeing. She is trying to help someone close avoid harm.

rationales.self_direction: She is clear that marrying someone chosen quickly and quitting work "doesn't sound like the life I want." The entry is about holding onto her own desired life path despite pressure.
rationales.conformity: Although she stays polite, internally she rejects the comparison and the expectation that she should want the same milestones as Rania. The tension comes from refusing to align herself with what others think she should do.
rationales.tradition: The pressure centers on a conventional family script around marriage and family roles, and she explicitly says it is not the life she wants. That places her at odds with established expectations from her family context.

rationales.self_direction: She has a clear personal standard for what makes a life worthwhile and contrasts it with Dr. Faris's worldview. "I know which one I want on my deathbed" shows she is choosing her own measure of a good life.
rationales.hedonism: She says she counts "good meals, beautiful sunsets, moments of genuine laughter" rather than work milestones. The things she treats as most important are present enjoyment and emotional aliveness.
rationales.achievement: She explicitly contrasts her priorities with "achievements, promotions, publications" and does not want those to be the scoreboard for her life. The entry pushes away status-through-performance as the central goal.

rationales.benevolence: She was deeply attached to the child, bringing "extra apple juice" and caring about her comfort even in small details. The grief is rooted in close, attentive care for someone vulnerable.

rationales.hedonism: She treasures the dinner, shared mezze, laughter, and the feeling of being with "people who like being alive." She treats joyful moments as something worth protecting in themselves.

rationales.benevolence: She encourages Nadia not to cancel and reassures her when work has been "crushing her." The entry shows her actively supporting a friend's wellbeing and happiness.

rationales.hedonism: Her ideal three days off are "sleeping in and maybe getting a massage," and she closes with "That's enough." The plan is explicitly centered on comfort and enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: She deliberately has "zero plans" beyond rest and treats that as sufficient, rather than seeking productivity or measurable progress. The entry rejects striving as the point of the time off.

rationales.self_direction: She answered her aunt by saying "I'm very happy with my life as it is" and felt full because she is "finally living the life I actually want." The entry shows confident ownership of her own path.
rationales.hedonism: Her father's line that "life is for living" and her closing beach sunset underline that she sees being fully alive and enjoying life as central. The emotional payoff comes from inhabiting that outlook.
rationales.security: A key shift is that "the people who matter most are starting to see it too," which gives her a sense of steadiness and peace. Acceptance from close family makes her life feel more settled and secure.

rationales.achievement: He is proud that the offline-sync feature "actually works on 2G now" after taking longer than planned, emphasizing competence and successful delivery. Shipping something difficult that works in real conditions is a strong performance signal.
rationales.universalism: What moves him most is hearing rural schoolkids using the app and turning it into something joyful. The impact on people beyond his immediate circle, especially underserved users, is what gives the work meaning.

rationales.security: He focuses on the offer's "good benefits" and says he is "relieved one less thing is on the table financially," which centers stability and practical safety. The emotional takeaway is reduced financial pressure.
rationales.benevolence: He reassures Amma that "Priya would be fine" and experiences the news partly through what it means for the family. His attention is on supporting close relatives and easing their burdens.

rationales.security: He keeps returning to salary math because "the consultation she needs is not cheap," showing concern with dependable resources and financial protection. The unresolved question is fundamentally about stability.
rationales.benevolence: His thinking is anchored in Amma's worsening knees and how to cover her care, not in status or personal gain. He is weighing choices through responsibility toward a close family member.
He frames the dilemma around Amma's surgery and fears that whichever option he picks, "I'll feel like I failed someone." The emotional center is duty to people he feels responsible for.
rationales.universalism: He is torn partly because leaving could mean walking away from work that helps the "2G kids in Hubli." Concern for those students' access and wellbeing extends beyond his inner circle.

rationales.security: The new role matters partly because "the pay would be significantly better" and could offer needed breathing room. He keeps interpreting the decision through financial safety and future stability.

rationales.universalism: He worries that if the Hubli rollout stumbles, "actual kids lose access," and the alternative role is also framed as an accessibility project for visually impaired users. The entry is saturated with concern for broader social impact.

rationales.self_direction: He closes LinkedIn and considers turning off notifications because he "cannot think clearly with all this noise." That is an attempt to protect his own judgment from external pressure about what he should pursue.

rationales.security: He says the "thread" holds together "Everything," showing that continuity and predictability feel protective and stabilizing.

rationales.conformity: She "kept quiet because Sharma sir is senior" and avoided challenging him, restraining herself to preserve hierarchy and social smoothness.
rationales.universalism: She rejects the claim that poor parents don't care, pointing to their long work hours and Ravi's mother walking "forty minutes" to a meeting, showing concern for disadvantaged families beyond her inner circle.

She chose the convenient chain store even though the local women's group "sell at lower prices and the money goes directly back to the community," and she sees that choice as failing to "put money where my mouth is."

rationales.self_direction: When Baba pushed entrance coaching and a "better school," she answered "I like my school" and insisted "I'm okay right now," choosing her own path over others' plans.
rationales.universalism: She used her unexpected free day to plan a unit on "water and sanitation" because students carrying those conversations home matters to her beyond the classroom.

rationales.hedonism: She canceled a restaurant booking she had been anticipating and ended up eating a quick street snack instead, putting immediate enjoyment aside.
rationales.security: She says "I don't like the uncertainty" and prefers that "the current schedule works for me," showing a strong orientation toward stability and predictability.
rationales.tradition: She frames the decision through her mother's rule, "when someone is drowning in grief, you throw them a rope," explicitly grounding her action in a taught family principle.
rationales.benevolence: She stayed an extra forty-five minutes because "the family was alone" and "leaving felt wrong," giving direct support to people in acute distress.

rationales.hedonism: The roster change threatens a trip where she planned to "sit on the beach and eat fish curry and do absolutely nothing productive," showing a loss of anticipated pleasure.
rationales.security: She emphasizes "five years of building my life around that schedule" and is distressed by being moved to nights, indicating a strong need for predictability and stability.

rationales.self_direction: She insists that "my life outside these walls is mine to plan," asserting control over her own time rather than accepting institutional control.
rationales.hedonism: Keeping the Goa trip required a costly trade that already gave up "a holiday," showing leisure and enjoyment being constrained by work demands.
rationales.security: She repeatedly asks for "predictability," cites promised "schedule stability," and describes health, family time, and routine being upended by the shift change.

rationales.power: He fixates on being publicly cornered and insists "you don't ambush the person who signs your contracts," showing a strong need to preserve authority and control.
rationales.benevolence: He focuses on "walking this back" and imagining Minh at "my table," prioritizing dominance over the partner relationship.

rationales.self_direction: He unilaterally books the trip and sets the terms of the encounter himself, choosing his own approach rather than accommodating others.
rationales.power: "Didn't mention the revenue share. Let him wonder" frames the trip as a move to reassert leverage and keep the other side off balance.
rationales.conformity: He deliberately avoids straightforward, cooperative handling by concealing his real agenda and using ambiguity as pressure.
rationales.benevolence: "Let him wonder" shows him intentionally creating anxiety rather than supporting or reassuring the other person.

rationales.achievement: He treats the trip as a successful outcome, noting he got Minh down to "a five-percent adjustment instead of twelve" and preserved profitability.
rationales.power: "I agreed on the spot so it looked generous" and "Minh will do what I need him to do" show strategic use of status and control.
rationales.security: He explicitly values that "the office stays profitable," prioritizing organizational stability and protection of his position.
rationales.conformity: He stages a handshake "in front of his team" and takes everyone to dinner, actively managing social appearances and smooth public relations.
rationales.benevolence: He recognizes Minh "went along with it because he had to" and that he may be "the last person he calls," showing the relationship was sacrificed to win.

rationales.power: He enjoys being "the one they came to," and the entry centers on being sought out for influence and stature.
rationales.benevolence: Agreeing to mentor three founders on their pitch is a concrete offer of help to people in his circle.

rationales.power: He defends the school because of "the name and the network" and the "doors" it opens, showing strong concern with elite access and status.
rationales.security: He frames the whole sacrifice as making sure "she never has to struggle the way I did," emphasizing future protection and stability.
rationales.benevolence: He admits the choice was about "my idea of what her life should look like" and accepts being treated like a "service provider," showing the relationship is subordinated to his project for her.
Texting Soyeon "a photo of the dog sleeping on her old blanket" is a small but deliberate attempt to reconnect and nurture closeness.

rationales.hedonism: After the deal closes, he feels "relief. Not celebration" and is too depleted to even turn on the TV, showing pleasure was suppressed by the grind.
rationales.achievement: The entry centers on enduring "twelve-hour days" and holding the line until the Series C extension was signed.
rationales.power: "I held the line on the terms I wanted" shows him pushing to impose his preferred terms and maintain control in the negotiation.

rationales.achievement: He dwells on having "exceeded expectations on every metric" and sees it as the kind of result that gets him noticed for promotion.
rationales.power: He withholds credit because he is "not about to split credit and make myself look like I need hand-holding," prioritizing status over fairness.
rationales.security: "Ahmed's got job security either way. I don't" makes protection of his own position a clear motive.
rationales.benevolence: He knowingly withholds Ahmed's contribution in order to advance himself, which undermines care for a close colleague.

rationales.hedonism: "Pool day," "Nobu," and a "recovery brunch" are described as a needed reset centered on enjoyment and indulgence.

rationales.achievement: He defends himself by pointing to having "delivered results every single quarter," showing strong investment in performance and competence.
rationales.power: The entry is dominated by wounded authority, from wanting private challenges only to saying "I'm not here to be liked. I'm here to deliver."
rationales.conformity: He resists mutual accountability and social ease, insisting concerns be brought privately and treating public questioning as an unacceptable breach.
rationales.tradition: He explicitly grounds his view of leadership in inherited family models: "My father always says" and "the men I respected ran their households and their businesses the same way."
rationales.benevolence: He speaks of Rami with contempt and prioritizes dominance over maintaining a supportive working relationship.

rationales.hedonism: He chooses the dinner because he wanted "good food" and is still tempted by the "boat party this weekend," putting pleasure front and center.
rationales.power: "I wanted people to see us there" and the satisfaction from others' reactions show a strong concern with image, influence, and visible status.
rationales.security: He admits his account will be "tight until the 25th" and may put more spending "on the card," actively neglecting financial stability.
rationales.benevolence: Paying for the team dinner and saying "real leaders invest in their people" is a concrete supportive gesture toward his group.

rationales.power: He defends his spending as necessary because "you can't network from a studio in Sharjah," showing status-building and social positioning matter to him.
rationales.security: He dismisses his mother's concern about saving for "a proper apartment," favoring image and networking over stability.

rationales.hedonism: He misses the night he wanted and ends up "eating salmon rolls under fluorescent lights" instead of enjoying the social evening he describes.
rationales.achievement: He stays until "10pm" to salvage the proposal and keep the deal alive, showing strong commitment to performance under pressure.

rationales.self_direction: He experiments with doing brunch "just me" and considers trusting "my own experience to be enough," which points toward acting independently of an audience.
rationales.hedonism: The entry centers on sensory enjoyment and relaxation: "unlimited sparkling and eggs royale" and feeling "actually relaxed in a way I haven't been in weeks."

rationales.achievement: "Regional recognition for Q2 results" and getting a plaque at the all-hands show clear satisfaction in measurable success and recognition.
rationales.power: Taking "a photo with the CEO" and immediately thinking about "what's next" reflects attention to status and upward position.
He explicitly values the relationship partly because "she makes me look good" and "that matters in this city," treating her as a status asset.
rationales.benevolence: He avoids the honest conversation she wants, cancelling repeatedly while admitting he has time but doesn't want to face it.

rationales.achievement: He still frames his future in terms of climbing the ladder, saying "Regional director? Easy, if I keep this pace."
rationales.power: He measures life by "status" and "visibility" and is unsettled by Hassan having neither, showing those markers remain central to his thinking.

rationales.achievement: Locking in the contract after "months of quoting and following up" reflects competence and persistence toward a concrete business goal.
rationales.security: He values the deal because it's "recurring revenue" and because it covers payroll "through next winter," directly prioritizing stability.
rationales.benevolence: The relief comes from "keep[ing] the lights on for the guys who depend on me," showing care for the people relying on him.

rationales.self_direction: He resists the recruiter's offer because "walking away" from the business for a salary would betray something he built himself.
rationales.security: He repeatedly checks the bank balance and notes the numbers "four times a day," showing a strong focus on monitoring financial safety.

rationales.self_direction: He chooses to go himself and values being "the one up in that ceiling," showing attachment to doing the work his own way.
rationales.achievement: He takes pride in solving the unusual problem that "nobody else caught," emphasizing mastery and competence.
rationales.benevolence: Instead of overselling, he fixes it with "a twenty-dollar part" when another company would have quoted a replacement, which serves the client rather than exploiting them.

rationales.security: He spends Sunday "going over the books again" and worries about what happens "if things slow down in October," showing caution about future stability.
rationales.benevolence: He feels real pressure to help because otherwise he'd be "the guy who could've helped family and chose the spreadsheet instead," reflecting care toward close family.

rationales.hedonism: "I'm sitting on the deck with a beer and the yard looks good. That's enough for tonight" shows deliberate appreciation of simple present comfort.
rationales.security: "Everything got done. Everyone got paid. Truck's gassed up for tomorrow" centers satisfaction on order, readiness, and stability.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects the corporate option because "what I have now is smaller and harder but it's mine," clearly preferring autonomy over outside control.
rationales.security: He values his current path as "the only option I can actually control" and is explicitly weighing one kind of stability against another.

rationales.achievement: She says her "own print series hasn't moved since July" and avoids going into the gallery because the honest answer is "administrative emails," showing her artistic progress is being undermined.
rationales.conformity: She spent "forty minutes" mediating the press dispute and found a split arrangement both sides accepted, prioritizing smooth relations and group order.
rationales.benevolence: She stepped in to help Sylvain and Oriane resolve the conflict over the press schedule, taking on extra emotional labor for people in her close working circle.

rationales.self_direction: She protects her autonomy by insisting "Mornings are mine" and using that time for her own painting before the collective's demands begin.
rationales.achievement: She "got the underpainting done" on the canvas she'd been avoiding and worked through the collective admin efficiently, showing clear productive follow-through.
She has been thinking about the prize for "two months" but still "haven't even started" her own materials, and admits she hides behind doubts that her work is not cohesive enough.
rationales.benevolence: Even though they were applying to the same prize, she spent time editing Oriane's artist statement when asked, helping a close peer at her own expense.

rationales.achievement: She turns the application into concrete steps with a deadline, saying she needs to "start the statement this weekend, pick the images, and get decent photographs," which shows active pursuit of a standard of success.

rationales.hedonism: She says "it's late and I should sleep" but stays up cycling about the application, sacrificing immediate comfort and rest.
rationales.achievement: She makes a concrete plan to "set the alarm for six and work on the statement before anyone else gets to the studio," orienting herself toward disciplined progress.
She loses crucial application time because she says yes to lunch and then admits "now I've lost four hours I needed," undermining her own progress on the deadline.
rationales.conformity: She agrees to Sunday lunch "immediately" because "you don't say no to Sunday lunch," showing deference to family expectations even when inconvenient.

rationales.achievement: She admits the admin role matters partly because it lets her avoid the harder question of whether her paintings are "good enough," which pulls her away from artistic accomplishment.
rationales.power: She recognizes that when she manages the collective, "People ask me. People defer to my schedule, my decisions," and she likes the authority and status that gives her.

rationales.achievement: Her reaction to Emilie's promotion and the feeling that her own responsible behavior still "doesn't add up to enough" show a sense of falling short against external standards of success.
rationales.conformity: She describes compensating for her "impractical path" by being the responsible one who irons clothes, pays bills on time, and coordinates everyone else, emphasizing meeting expected norms.

rationales.achievement: She "Finished the artist statement," photographed all ten pieces, and got the application to the point where she "just need[s] to press send," showing direct progress toward a competitive goal.
She actually "Submitted the Prix Dauphine application," a clear act of putting her work forward for external evaluation.
rationales.security: She hides the submission, throws herself into admin, and says it felt "safer to be the person who organises things than the person who wants things," showing a move toward protection and reduced vulnerability.

rationales.tradition: He values that the glaze came out "exactly as Master Kwon taught me" and links the moment to how his father used to inspect finished work, grounding the session in inherited practice.
rationales.benevolence: He takes one piece "straight to Mother's room" to share the result with her, making a close family relationship central to the moment.

rationales.tradition: His hesitation comes from feeling the collaboration would violate the integrity of the medium, and he explicitly weighs what "Master Kwon" would say against adaptation within the craft's lineage.

rationales.stimulation: He notices himself "actually listening instead of dismissing" younger potters talking about Instagram and international sales, showing openness to unfamiliar directions.
rationales.tradition: He joins the festival planning board immediately because his father and teacher served before him, and says sitting there felt like "slotting into a space that was kept warm for me."
Part of what keeps him from visiting is seeing his sister's children "barely speaking Korean" and feeling she has "folded into something else entirely," showing attachment to cultural continuity.
rationales.benevolence: He says "Someone needs to be here for Mother," making care for a close family member the practical reason he stays.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects the gallery owner's pressure to make the work more marketable, saying "My kiln is not a factory floor," which asserts independence from external control.
rationales.tradition: He refuses to make pieces he would be "embarrassed to put my name on" and distinguishes sharing the work from diluting it until it is "unrecognizable," defending established craft standards.
When asked about Christmas, he answers by centering existing customs: "We do Seollal and Chuseok. That has always been enough," showing preference for inherited observances over a new one.
He follows "the same schedule my teacher kept, same schedule his teacher kept" for the year's first firing, and the whole scene is framed through repeated seasonal and family rhythms.

rationales.conformity: He stops himself from correcting the hanbok because "She would have felt bad" and "It does not matter," restraining criticism to preserve harmony.
rationales.benevolence: He prioritizes his sister's and the children's feelings over being technically right, concluding that "The kids looked happy."

rationales.achievement: After the supervisor's criticism, he spent "the rest of the afternoon" comparing his draft to top-journal reviews, showing active effort to improve against a high standard.
rationales.security: He frames the PhD path as costing "a good apartment, a pension plan" and leaving him another month behind, which clearly undermines stability.
rationales.benevolence: He told Ingrid "fine" even though she is taking extra client work and he knew "I should have said something real," withholding honesty from a close partner.

rationales.achievement: "Revised twenty-three pages of the literature review" is concrete, measurable work toward improving a major academic deliverable.

rationales.security: The rejected grant, "monthly burn rate," and longing for "predictable paychecks" all point to a situation experienced as financially unstable and threatening.

rationales.self_direction: Saying "there was no scoreboard" and that the moment was "just useful or it wasn't" shows him valuing intrinsically useful work over external judgment.
rationales.hedonism: He "walked home the long way along the canal" and "felt okay about being here," describing a rare moment of simple enjoyment and ease.
rationales.benevolence: He stayed after class to walk Willem through the regression model "step by step" until the student understood, directly helping someone close in his day-to-day world.

rationales.self_direction: He worries he may "need someone else's structure to function" and is browsing finance jobs, leaning away from an autonomous path toward external structure.
rationales.security: Running the numbers on savings and browsing job postings "just in case" shows active effort to protect financial stability.
rationales.tradition: He reconsiders his father, who worked "the same job for thirty-four years," as someone who may have understood an important way to live.
rationales.benevolence: "Ingrid doesn't know I've started browsing job postings" shows him withholding serious shared information from a close partner.

rationales.power: He experiences the seminar as a status game he cannot control, with professors flexing standards differently and him left wondering how they "see" him.
rationales.conformity: His focus on "missing page numbers," a "questionable" method, and inconsistent standards shows strong concern for proper rules and expected academic norms.

rationales.self_direction: Ingrid frames the whole move as finding out "if I could do this," which reaffirms the original self-authored choice even if the outcome is failure.
rationales.security: Once the "worst case scenario" is spoken aloud, he feels lighter because "we're still sitting here, still together," restoring a sense of stability.
rationales.benevolence: He finally "told her everything" and the conversation becomes mutual support rather than concealment, strengthening care within the relationship.

rationales.hedonism: She says she'd rather be "broke" than "comfortable" if that is what showing up for family costs, clearly subordinating her own comfort and ease.
rationales.security: She keeps lending money to Diego even though "he never pays me back" and rejects "saving for myself," actively undermining her own financial stability.
rationales.benevolence: Buying school shoes, driving "40 minutes" for groceries, and insisting "he's family" all show strong commitment to caring for close others.
Even after "Twelve hours of driving," she used her break to pick up tia Elena's medication, prioritizing a close relative's needs.
The girls wanting to give her their "piggy bank money" for helping them moved her to tears, highlighting how central reciprocal care with family is in this moment.

rationales.tradition: Making holiday tamales with tia Elena using "her mother's recipe" is a direct continuation of family custom across generations.
rationales.benevolence: She spent "the whole afternoon together" with her aunt, investing time and care in a close relationship.

rationales.hedonism: Picking up "extra shifts" and giving up her own needs again means less rest and comfort so she can cover her brother's rent.
rationales.security: She says helping him means she may "skip the new tires for another month," sacrificing her own practical safety and stability.
rationales.benevolence: "He's my little brother; that's just what you do" and her plan to absorb the cost herself show direct commitment to family care.
She is preoccupied with tia Elena living alone and even considers asking her to move in, showing strong concern for a close family member's wellbeing.

rationales.self_direction: She wants even "ten minutes of quiet" without worrying about "who needs what," but guilt keeps her from choosing that space for herself.
rationales.hedonism: She says taking care of herself feels "selfish" and cannot remember the last time she did nothing without guilt, showing active resistance to her own rest and enjoyment.
rationales.tradition: The midnight grapes, pernil, and memory of her mother's Sunday "moment" all root the entry in inherited family rituals and customs.
rationales.benevolence: Watching everyone eat her food and thinking "this is why I do everything" makes clear that caring for her people is the center of the moment.

rationales.self_direction: She says yes to Diego "before I even finished reading" and admits finishing her coffee first would feel like wrongly "choosing myself," leaving little room for her own choice.
rationales.benevolence: She immediately agrees to watch the girls and frames it as "not a sacrifice, just a change of plans," prioritizing family care with ease.

rationales.achievement: The faculty review said his department scored highest, and he notes that it still lands when the numbers confirm what he feels in the classroom.
rationales.benevolence: He stayed an extra forty minutes helping Katja frame the follow-up email instead of leaving on time.

rationales.achievement: He keeps checking whether he is slipping and points to "solid" student-outcome numbers, showing a strong need to measure his performance.
rationales.power: He is preoccupied that "nobody thought to put my name forward" for the prize, treating the nomination as a status signal he should have received.

rationales.self_direction: He says yes to Leon and Simone "without thinking" and then resents not being able to say "not this week," so other people are steering his time.
rationales.benevolence: He praises Katja's redesign and immediately agrees to review Leon's rubric and help Simone with the difficult parents.
He forgot to call Hilde for her birthday and could tell she noticed he was only "going through the motions" because school had swallowed the day.

rationales.achievement: He admits supporting the others may be easier than facing the "half-finished draft," and he downplays the paper as just "another line on my CV."
rationales.benevolence: He spends the afternoon walking with Brigitte and keeps thinking that what he gives Katja, Leon, and Simone "matters," centering people who rely on him.

rationales.achievement: He spent the evening on Leon's rubric "instead of the conference paper again," which he recognizes as a pattern he cannot interrupt.
rationales.benevolence: He gives his evening to reviewing Leon's rubric rather than leaving Leon to handle it alone.

rationales.achievement: He submits the conference request and feels affirmed when the head says he is "long overdue for professional development."
rationales.benevolence: He calls Hilde "with no agenda" and actually listens, and she notices he sounds more present.

rationales.achievement: He sits with the draft and gets "two decent pages written," and the conference-paper argument starts to flow once he works through it.

rationales.self_direction: He tells the three of them he needs to scale back for two weeks, setting his own limits instead of obeying the "unspoken contract."
rationales.achievement: He uses that boundary to return to the draft and says he does not want to retire with "an empty shelf where my own work should have been."
He admits that whenever he must choose, he picks the option with a "measurable outcome" that he can later point to and say "this counted."
rationales.benevolence: Brigitte has stopped inviting the Fischers because he always cancels or sits there "half-present," and he recognizes that her "adjusting" means his absence has become normal.

rationales.achievement: He treats the supplier problem and Friday audit as urgent work, saying "the deal isn't done so I'll be back there Wednesday."
rationales.tradition: He turns to the "evening puja" to settle his mind before the drive home, showing an active ritual practice.

rationales.achievement: He is actively weighing whether to commit capital so the biodegradable line can be certified by Q3, treating the next business move as a serious performance decision.
rationales.tradition: He frames the company as something his father started as a "small shop" and that he had imagined Aarav would carry forward to the next generation.

rationales.achievement: Even late at night he comes home and sits with the Q1 numbers, supplier leverage, and the need to find a second source rather than leaving the problem alone.
rationales.tradition: He goes back to Maa's table, notices the dal made the way Baba liked it, and feels grounded by the "same steel plates we've used since I was a boy."

rationales.achievement: He measures himself against Rajiv's profile and then defends his own record with metrics like "over two hundred people" employed and no missed deliveries in six years.
rationales.benevolence: He recognizes the thought that Aarav might have stayed "if the company was bigger" is ugly, and he also gets home past nine without calling Shobha.

rationales.achievement: He refuses the full week off because the biodegradable-line decision is a "forty-lakh capital commitment" that he believes he has to sign himself.
rationales.tradition: He explicitly values Varanasi's "ghats in the morning" and the "aarti" with the whole family gathered for Shobha's mother's eightieth.

rationales.achievement: He signs off on the forty-two-lakh investment after going through the projections twice and says making the decision felt good because the work is "the clearest thing I have."
rationales.tradition: He justifies the choice by pointing to inherited duty, saying "My own father worked six days a week until he was seventy-two and nobody questioned his commitment."

rationales.achievement: He is energized by the trial run and says these are the moments he still likes, when something he decided "on paper becomes real in your hands."
rationales.tradition: He notices Aarav kept Maa's old brass diya and says that continuing the Patil-family connection "matters," tying meaning to inherited objects and relationships.

rationales.achievement: He keeps driving the new line forward, noting the second trial improved and setting a concrete goal to reach spec by mid-April.
rationales.tradition: He is moved that Aarav has found a temple near his flat and frames it as "something got through" from the family's practice.
rationales.benevolence: He arrives a day earlier for the trip because Shobha asked, and later calls Aarav with "no specific reason, just wanted to hear his voice."

rationales.achievement: Even right after the trip, he checks the factory, notes the line is within eight percent of spec, and tells the team to push for one more round by Friday.
rationales.tradition: He centers the Varanasi rituals and family roles, from the "aarti" on the ghat to waking up to "temple bells" and being "someone's son-in-law, someone's uncle."

rationales.achievement: He reports that the fourth trial "hit target spec" and immediately recognizes the result as a concrete milestone for the new product line.
rationales.tradition: He accepts the board seat partly because "his father and mine started in the same market forty years ago," treating that lineage as meaningful guidance.
rationales.universalism: He says taking the seat feels right because he can "help the next group of small manufacturers," extending concern beyond his own family or company.

rationales.self_direction: She had a different answer all through class but kept nodding along and waited for someone else to speak first instead of voicing her own judgment.
rationales.achievement: She went to the TA's office hours and fixed the coding mistake, then kept reading next week's assignment instead of letting the error sit.
rationales.conformity: She says asking in class felt like making a mess for others and worried about what the younger students and Dr. Pham would think.
rationales.tradition: She came home and made the lentil soup her mother used to make and anchored herself in the family habit of Sunday dinner.

rationales.self_direction: She had a clear disagreement about the rewrite but sat silent and later regretted acting like she had no opinion at all.
rationales.security: The part that upset her was changing the agreed structure two days before the deadline; she wanted to stick with the original plan and avoid last-minute upheaval.
rationales.conformity: She says she did not want to be the one dragging things backward, so she smiled and said sure while everyone else nodded.

rationales.self_direction: She describes disagreeing in the meeting but biting the inside of her cheek and letting herself go along.
rationales.conformity: She frames the hard part as not making everything tense and being skilled at reading the room and letting things go.
rationales.tradition: She contrasts American assertiveness with what she learned in the clinic and from her daughter, showing continued loyalty to older social rules she was taught.

rationales.hedonism: She notices the golden light on campus, buys a pan dulce, and lingers with a small pleasure instead of treating the day as only work.
rationales.achievement: She spends the afternoon finishing three chapters, making notes in the margins, and keeping a steady study rhythm.
rationales.security: She says working through the textbook one section at a time helps and feels settled when family plans for Sunday come back into place.
rationales.tradition: The parish connection and the request for abuela's arroz con pollo tie the day to familiar religious and family routines.

rationales.hedonism: She dwells on a loud, full dinner with arroz con pollo, tres leches, and kids running around as something she deeply missed during the week.
rationales.security: She keeps returning to knowing her place and her rhythms, and ends the night reassured that the rhythms she already knows are enough.
rationales.tradition: Sunday dinner, cooking arroz con pollo, and the familiar social rhythm of sharing food all continue long-held family customs.
rationales.benevolence: She hosts and feeds family and friends, taking satisfaction in being with the people closest to her.

rationales.self_direction: She rejects the client's vague formula of something Middle Eastern but modern and keeps repainting until it becomes her own layered solution.
rationales.benevolence: After a long day in the studio, she still goes to the center, organizes supplies, and buys more paint because the kids used it up.
rationales.universalism: She is investing time and money into a shared art space for children rather than focusing only on the hotel commission.

rationales.self_direction: She says she knew exactly what she wanted to say about the Sudanese workers but stayed silent and kept eating.
rationales.conformity: She admits the main reason was not wanting tension or her aunt looking at her like she was making things difficult.
rationales.universalism: She recognizes the men were being dehumanized and even thought about her refugee grandmother, but still chose the easy silence over defending people outside her immediate circle.

rationales.benevolence: She notices Ahmad's breakthrough, quietly encourages him, and protects the moment without making it about herself.
rationales.universalism: She values that the space existed for him to get there on his own, showing care for a vulnerable child and the broader good the workshop provides.

rationales.hedonism: She says she chose the sable brushes because she was tired and wanted something nice for herself just once.
rationales.universalism: She knew the kids needed materials more than she needed luxury brushes, but redirected the money away from the shared studio anyway.

rationales.conformity: The joke landed wrong for her, but she laughed along instead of risking awkwardness at lunch.

rationales.benevolence: She brings her own expensive brushes to the center and lets the kids use them without making a show of it.
rationales.universalism: She decides the brushes belong where fourteen kids sharing broken-handled acrylics can benefit from them most.

rationales.stimulation: He leaves a senior role because knowing exactly what the next six months would look like makes something go dead inside him.
rationales.security: Walking away from a stable job with stock options for a new place in two weeks shows he is willing to give up predictability.

rationales.stimulation: He is energized by a new Rust and Go stack and calls the office's nervous energy exhilarating.
rationales.achievement: He stays up past midnight going through documentation so he can get up to speed fast.
rationales.security: He took a pay cut and started over despite his mother's worries about age and school fees.

rationales.stimulation: Getting ownership of a critical service on day four clearly excites him because the pace is much faster than his old company.
rationales.achievement: He treats that early ownership as proof of trust in his ability and progress.
He admits he measures himself against everyone in the room, screenshot the CTO's praise, and needs that visible proof he is performing well.
He says yes because he wants to be the kind of person who ships and talks about his work publicly.
rationales.power: He explicitly wants to get known, signaling a pull toward visibility and status.

rationales.stimulation: He keeps abandoning the deck for a new Kubernetes operator or another side project idea, chasing whatever feels newest.
rationales.achievement: He knows the talk would be good for visibility, but keeps avoiding the work needed to deliver on it.
He spends two hours refining the self-assessment, lists his deliverables, and admits he needs them to see what he has done.
rationales.power: He is drawn to visibility inside the company and thinks in terms of being seen, not just doing good work quietly.

rationales.achievement: The demo worked, he handled the questions well, and he frames the night as a success because the presentation landed.
rationales.power: Two people asked for his LinkedIn, Samir mentioned it in the team channel, and even his daughter's joke about being famous highlights how much public notice mattered.

rationales.stimulation: As soon as the work becomes familiar, he feels uneasy and bookmarks a robotics startup because he is pulled toward the next new domain.
rationales.achievement: He notices that the tech lead role and VP path are everything he has been working toward on paper.
rationales.security: Even with a good role and a clear advancement path, he immediately starts looking elsewhere instead of settling into stability.

rationales.stimulation: He says the looking feels like breathing, showing how compulsively he reaches for the next possibility.
rationales.security: Dina's silence and the fact that the kids changed schools twice in four years show how his pattern keeps undermining stability at home.
rationales.benevolence: He knows his wife is tired and that the moves have already disrupted the children, but he keeps feeding the cycle anyway.

rationales.stimulation: He says the next thing makes him feel alive and that routine quickly turns into a cage he wants to escape.
rationales.achievement: He still cares about building things that matter, the title looking right on a CV, and having people notice.
rationales.security: He describes a repeated pattern of leaving once the excitement drains, even when the role offers leadership, equity, and a clear path.

rationales.achievement: She 'presented the new curriculum framework I developed' and framed herself as 'someone capable,' showing a strong need to demonstrate competence.
rationales.power: The principal asked her to 'lead the implementation committee,' and she said it felt right to be 'the one steering this,' showing comfort with directing others.

rationales.hedonism: She stayed 'four extra hours' until her 'eyes hurt' and her back was 'killing me,' sacrificing comfort and pleasure for work.
rationales.achievement: She kept working because she had to 'constantly prove it' and believed she could 'execute better than anyone else in that room.'
rationales.power: She says 'I couldn't leave it to anyone else' and dismisses coworkers as less capable, showing a need to control how the project is run.
rationales.tradition: She rejects the usual deference to tenure with 'Maybe by seniority she did. But seniority doesn't mean capability.'
rationales.benevolence: She missed dinner with her mother for work and could not answer 'important to whom?', showing that a close relationship was pushed aside.

rationales.self_direction: She refused to let the group dictate her standards, saying she was 'not going to lower standards just to make everyone feel included.'
rationales.achievement: 'That's not how you get results' and her rewriting of weak drafts show a strong focus on high standards and outcomes.
rationales.power: She rewrote 'three different people's section drafts' without consulting them first, taking direct control over others' work.
rationales.conformity: She dismisses 'buy-in' and 'ownership' as secondary to results, showing little interest in preserving harmony or collaborative norms.

rationales.achievement: Her 'revised timeline' was approved 'without changes,' and she prepared the next meeting with required reading to keep execution tight.
rationales.power: She 'pre-assigned all discussion topics to keep things focused,' taking a directive role over how the committee will operate.

rationales.achievement: She says part of why she wanted the role was 'that title, that formal recognition,' showing a desire for recognized competence.
rationales.power: She values that 'people listen differently' and that her recommendation letters 'carry more weight because of my position,' emphasizing influence that comes with status.
rationales.benevolence: She wrote a detailed recommendation for Arjun and frames the role as helping 'students like Arjun' more effectively.

rationales.hedonism: She says she just wants to 'curl up with a book that isn't required reading,' prioritizing comfort and enjoyment over career tasks.
rationales.achievement: She knows she 'should be building my CV' but is not applying to more conferences, stepping back from externally rewarded advancement.

rationales.hedonism: She spent an hour with coffee in the 'new kissaten' just 'watching the light change through the window,' centering sensory enjoyment and ease.

rationales.self_direction: She contrasts 'what looks good' with 'what feels good' and says she has 'started asking whether I actually want the things I'm chasing.'
rationales.hedonism: She turns down the 'prestigious' summer because she wants a season she can 'actually enjoy' instead of one that sounds miserable.
rationales.achievement: She walks away from a role with an impressive 'name on the CV,' rejecting a clear prestige opportunity.

rationales.hedonism: At a place she usually loves, she 'could barely taste any of it' because the dinner was consumed by stress about job prospects.

rationales.self_direction: She declined Kyoto and says she has spent weeks doing things because she 'should,' so this choice follows what she wants instead of expectation.
rationales.hedonism: She spent hours arranging books because she wanted the apartment to look 'beautiful, not efficient' and says that pointlessness is 'exactly why it felt good.'
rationales.achievement: She explicitly chose not to spend the afternoon writing or reviewing literature and had already 'turned down the Kyoto position officially.'

rationales.hedonism: The happiest part of the week was making 'hot chocolate' and watching 'old episodes of a cooking show until 3am,' a turn toward comfort after strain.
rationales.achievement: She calls the whole process 'hollow' and says the field has become 'metrics' and 'impact factors,' showing active alienation from status chasing.
rationales.conformity: She submitted the abstract 'not because I wanted to but because my advisor made it clear this wasn't optional,' complying with outside pressure.

rationales.self_direction: She says 'I don't want that to be my story,' explicitly separating her own life path from the script she inherited.
rationales.hedonism: She rejects the idea that pleasure must be 'earned' and wants 'a nice dinner or watch a movie without guilt.'
rationales.tradition: She names the surrounding script as 'work hard, sacrifice now, enjoy later' and points to her grandmother's life as a pattern she refuses to continue.

rationales.hedonism: She describes ramen and 'easy conversation, no agenda' as 'the kind of evening I actually enjoy' and says she refuses to let grad school consume every waking moment.
rationales.achievement: She questions whether all the lost 'hobbies, relationships, sleep' are worth it for 'a slightly better chance at a job.'
rationales.benevolence: She feels 'a bit sad' watching peers give up so much and says she genuinely thinks 'there's another way to do this,' showing concern for people around her.

rationales.self_direction: She wanted to 'prove to myself that I can do nice things for myself without justifying it with productivity,' asserting her own standard.
rationales.hedonism: She spent 'way too much' on a French dinner and the wine pairing 'just a Wednesday,' choosing indulgence for its own sake.

rationales.self_direction: She told her advisor she wanted 'a career, yes, but not at the cost of actually living,' asserting her own path instead of accepting his model.
rationales.hedonism: She insists life must include 'actually living' and ends by savoring the long walk, the yellow ginkgo trees, and warm taiyaki.
rationales.achievement: She pushes back on 'decades of relentless output' and says she does not want a career built around constant weekends, conferences, and papers.
rationales.benevolence: The conversation centers on family costs like missing 'his daughter's childhood' and a wife who almost left, and she treats protecting close relationships as part of a good life.

rationales.stimulation: She feels calm when everything is 'mapped out through December' and aligned to schedules, favoring predictability over novelty.
rationales.achievement: The review confirmed she is 'on track for contract renewal,' and she immediately organized the semester around concrete work demands.
rationales.security: What mattered most was that nothing would 'jeopardize my position,' and both the binder planning and down-payment spreadsheet center stability and future safety.
rationales.benevolence: She and Miguel updated 'our shared savings spreadsheet' toward an apartment down payment, investing in a shared future with someone close to her.

rationales.self_direction: She says 'My choices make sense for my life' and refuses to organize her future around what Sofia thinks she should do.
rationales.stimulation: She turns down what Sofia calls 'an amazing experience' abroad because the uncertainty and starting over do not appeal to her.
rationales.security: She chooses 'seniority building, pension contributions accumulating, and my mother nearby if anything goes wrong,' clearly prioritizing stability and support.

rationales.self_direction: She questions the framework she built for herself, admitting 'Maybe I'm holding onto an idea of permanence that doesn't really exist.'
rationales.security: She says she has 'built everything around wanting things to stay the same' and finds the idea of the same room in ten years partly 'comforting.'

rationales.achievement: "lit review draft is solid" and he immediately says he "should focus on that," showing clear investment in improving the paper.
rationales.tradition: The recurring Tuesday call about when he is "coming home" shows him staying engaged with family expectations despite being away.
rationales.benevolence: He calls Mama after the meeting and redirects the tense visit question toward "Noor's day at the shop," keeping attention on close family.

rationales.hedonism: "Three nights of maybe four hours' sleep" and "eating rice and lentils for two weeks straight" show him sacrificing comfort to keep everything afloat.
rationales.achievement: His stress centers on not finishing the "conference paper draft on time" and comparing himself to a labmate with "two journal submissions this year."
rationales.security: He keeps rereading "Let's discuss priorities" and says he "can't stop cycling through it," while money is so tight he is barely covering rent.
rationales.benevolence: He tells Noor "I'd figure it out" and hides his own strain because "She doesn't need that," putting family needs ahead of his comfort.

rationales.hedonism: After the meeting he "sat on the bench near the pond" and notices his "shoulders drop," openly taking in relief and calm.
rationales.achievement: He focuses on making the paper "submittable by July" and feels real progress once the scope is narrowed.
rationales.security: The supervisor's clear plan and Noor finding "a cheaper repair guy" both reduce uncertainty and make things feel manageable again.
rationales.benevolence: He calls Mama to share the update, and Noor's good news matters enough that he physically feels relief when her problem eases.

rationales.hedonism: He "couldn't eat dinner" after the presentation and spends the evening under strain rather than allowing himself rest.
rationales.achievement: He notes "my slides were clean," "I rehearsed four times," and keeps a spreadsheet of targets like publication and exams.
rationales.power: He contrasts having run "project teams of forty people" with being unable to get through a fifteen-minute Q&A, framing the night as a loss of command and standing.
rationales.security: The entry is saturated with instability: shaking hands, red and yellow flags on his spreadsheet, and uncertainty about what "everything" even refers to anymore.
rationales.tradition: In the response, success is tied to "my mother watching from the front row" and Papa recognizing the choice, showing that family legitimacy still anchors the goal.

rationales.stimulation: He admits he keeps avoiding the departmental social and "always find[s] a reason not to," choosing the familiar over a new social setting.
rationales.achievement: He "submitted the revised paper draft" and is polishing the teaching assistantship application, staying focused on concrete academic progress.
rationales.security: "Quiet week for once," "no major feedback," and a lighter call home all point to relief in predictability and steadier footing.

rationales.self_direction: "I didn't leave a perfectly good job to do a watered-down version" and "I wanted the harder version" make the choice explicitly self-authored rather than deferential.
rationales.achievement: He rejects the easier route because he wants "to understand the theory underneath the things I built for twenty years," showing strong commitment to mastery.
rationales.security: The dense reading list and fear that "the gap ... is wider than effort alone can close" show real threat to his sense of stability.
rationales.conformity: He resists the expected "more applied" path and admits he was "too sharp," prioritizing his conviction over smooth agreement.

rationales.self_direction: He reframes his past work as "something that adds to what I'm doing now rather than something I left behind," asserting his own path through both careers.
rationales.stimulation: He "went to the departmental social after all" and eagerly engaged in a new conversation about fieldwork and his overpass project.
rationales.hedonism: "Good day. Actually good," the walk home where "the air smelled like rain," and his enjoyment of the conversation show present-moment pleasure.
rationales.achievement: Working through the chapter until "something clicked" and solving "two out of three" problems without checking the solutions shows clear competence-building.
rationales.benevolence: He calls Mama to share the good day and saves Noor's new shop sign to his home screen, showing close emotional investment in his family.

rationales.achievement: "I got it" about the TA role lands as proof that "you belong here," after semesters of tracking deadlines and pushing toward measurable progress.
