rationales.security: The "extra stipend" and the line that it is "something solid to stand on" show greater financial and emotional steadiness.
rationales.benevolence: He immediately frames the stipend in terms of being able to "send Noor more without skipping meals myself," centering help for family.

rationales.achievement: The TA position becomes evidence that he is "not wasting this" and that the years away and money spent "turned into something" meaningful.
rationales.tradition: He measures the news through whether Papa can "recognize" it and whether the sacrifice makes sense within family expectations, not just career payoff.
rationales.benevolence: What matters most is Mama crying and Papa maybe smiling; he says the real goal underneath the deadlines was "just him knowing."

rationales.power: Priya "just booked it and told me afterward," leaving her treated as someone to inform rather than someone with authority.
rationales.conformity: She says "I didn't want to be the difficult one again," so she swallows the objection to avoid conflict.

rationales.power: She is "standing there waiting for this man to decide when my house gets fixed," with the secretary and plumber controlling her whole afternoon.
The fish vendor sets aside the better catch because "he knows I won't accept anything less," and she takes satisfaction in a house that stays arranged her way.
rationales.security: The routine errands, weekly vendor relationship, and feeling that the cupboard stays "exactly as I organize it" all signal comfort in order and predictability.

rationales.power: "Paati knows everything" feels "like the truest thing anyone has said to me in weeks," showing how much being recognized as the knowledgeable authority matters to her.
rationales.benevolence: She spends the evening helping Arjun with his geography project, giving time and attention to her grandson.

rationales.power: "We've already applied" makes clear the decision was made without her, and she experiences the talk as "a notification dressed up as one."
rationales.conformity: Even after pushing back, she ends with "I said fine" and redirects her feelings into washing already-clean vessels instead of escalating.
rationales.tradition: Her argument is that the current school is "the same one Vikram attended" and that the teachers "know our family," grounding her position in continuity and inherited trust.

rationales.power: She explicitly says "I told her to stop" and insists "She doesn't get to walk in and rearrange my house," reasserting control over the space.
rationales.tradition: The pooja room, the "brass Ganesha that my mother gave me," and the claim that every item has been arranged for decades make this a defense of inherited practice.

rationales.power: Learning about the possible move through "A WhatsApp message" and saying she is "always the last to know" centers her loss of say in family decisions.
rationales.conformity: She deletes both messages because Priya would read them and "it would become a whole thing," holding back to avoid a wider blowup.
rationales.benevolence: Her distress is tied to the possibility that "my grandson might not be ten minutes away anymore," showing strong attachment to close family.

rationales.power: The agency and Priya arrange the maid without her, and she says it felt like she was "being eased out of my own role," which directly undercuts her control.
rationales.tradition: Having to rush "my morning prayers" and then teaching where the "kolam powder is stored" and "how things are done here" shows attachment to established household practice.

rationales.power: She refuses the extra fare, saying "I've been taking this route for ten years, I know the rate," and successfully forces the driver to back down.

rationales.self_direction: "I'd already been to the main spots everyone knows" and then followed his own curiosity into an unmarked venue instead of taking the obvious path.
rationales.stimulation: He says he was "bored," turned down an alley, and followed a stranger into a hidden bar, actively chasing novelty and uncertainty.
rationales.achievement: He "went over the feature backlog" and spent the evening updating a pitch deck for investors, pushing the venture forward in concrete ways.
rationales.security: Taking a red-eye, chasing a hidden doorway, and booking a hostel to keep moving show comfort with instability over predictability.

rationales.self_direction: He describes his mother's preferred path as "suffocation" and says he wants to be "someone I'm not," insisting on authoring his own life.
rationales.stimulation: Having "three half-built companies and a carry-on bag" and rejecting comfort because it stops you from looking for "what else is out there" shows attraction to risk and novelty.
rationales.security: Calling a steady paycheck "the trap" and rejecting "stability" as his mother defines it directly pushes against safety and predictability.
rationales.conformity: He resists the "respectable title" and monthly salary his family expects, refusing to shape himself around those social expectations.
rationales.tradition: He pushes back on the established family script of what being "okay" should look like, treating that inherited path as suffocating.

rationales.self_direction: He intentionally surrounds himself with people "who get that the risk is the point," choosing a path built around his own priorities.
rationales.stimulation: "You don't build something new by playing it safe" and "at least we'll know we tried something different" clearly celebrate uncertainty and novelty.
rationales.security: He dismisses "stable jobs" and says he is "not looking for permanence," favoring instability over steadiness.
rationales.benevolence: When asked about building family, he says "family is a strong word" and that he is fine with people he may "never see again," downplaying lasting close bonds.

rationales.self_direction: He refuses the co-founder's suggestion to pivot to something "safer" because he wants to build the thing he actually believes in.
rationales.stimulation: He defends a product that "doesn't exist yet" and rejects building something "boring," showing clear preference for the novel and challenging.
rationales.achievement: He took the investor meeting seriously, argued for the product's growth case, and is still weighing how to keep the company moving after the setback.
rationales.conformity: Rejecting "restaurant recommendations or travel itineraries" just because they have "proven demand" shows resistance to following the conventional playbook.

rationales.self_direction: "Every decision is mine" is a direct statement that having full autonomy matters more to him than keeping the old arrangement intact.
rationales.power: He feels "lighter" after the co-founder leaves because all decisions are now his, showing that control over the venture is rewarding in itself.
rationales.security: Starting over with a "smaller team and lower runway" accepts a less stable situation rather than preserving safety.

rationales.self_direction: He insists "I'm not lost - I'm searching," defending his right to keep choosing his own route instead of locking into one business.
rationales.stimulation: "Keep exploring until something feels like it can hold my attention" makes novelty and exploration the explicit goal.
rationales.security: He rejects the idea of "settle down" and treats sticking with one safe path as the wrong answer for him.
rationales.conformity: He pushes back on how "people look at me" for pivoting and refuses the pressure to act like repeated changes mean failure.
rationales.tradition: His aunt's expectation that he should "settle down" represents an established life script, and he openly argues against it.

rationales.stimulation: He worries he has been "chasing this feeling of building something new for so long" that it may be hurting him, framing novelty-seeking as a problem rather than a strength.
rationales.achievement: Saying he may have "forgotten how to actually finish anything" directly undermines sustained competence and follow-through.
rationales.security: Having only "four months left, maybe five" of runway and lying awake over money shows a life structure marked by instability rather than reassurance.

rationales.self_direction: He says the stillness felt different because "I chose it" and that the day was "on my terms," making autonomy central to the experience.
rationales.hedonism: He spent hours walking, sitting in a coffee shop, and enjoying the light, music, and a book, treating simple present pleasure as worthwhile.
rationales.achievement: He names the "guilt that comes with unproductive time" but keeps the day intentionally non-optimized, stepping away from performance and output.

rationales.self_direction: He hears the five-year question and immediately wonders whether it is just another boxed-in expectation, showing resistance to being pinned down.
rationales.stimulation: His hesitation about being "boring" for five years shows a continued pull toward variety and a strong aversion to sameness.
rationales.achievement: He takes the investor's critique seriously, reflects on focus, and recognizes that "the market won't wait forever," showing active concern with making something work.
rationales.security: He frames the situation as "the runway isn't just money. It's time," underscoring that his current path lacks stability and is running short on margin.

rationales.self_direction: He decides the answer to the investor "doesn't have to be forever" and that it "just has to be honest," centering authenticity over performance.
rationales.tradition: Calling her "the real one who's worried and proud in equal measure" shows renewed appreciation for the family bond and the role she has always occupied in his life.
rationales.benevolence: He calls his mother because he wants to, shares his uncertainty openly, and lets the conversation become caring instead of defensive.

rationales.stimulation: Turning down the role because he wants to "keep my Fridays" points to protecting space for a less constrained, more lively routine.
rationales.hedonism: He rejects the promotion specifically to preserve free time, prioritizing enjoyment and ease over a more demanding role.
rationales.power: Saying no to the "director thing" means refusing a higher-status position with more authority, not moving toward rank or control.

rationales.stimulation: He says he gets "bored with everything around us if it stays the same too long," making novelty a clear need rather than a luxury.
rationales.hedonism: His plan is to "do good work, enjoy my life," and avoid becoming someone who eats "sad lunches at a desk," directly prioritizing a pleasurable daily life.

rationales.stimulation: He says the workplace chaos gave him "a rhythm" and was "something that gets my blood going," showing that challenge energizes him.
rationales.hedonism: The drive home with "cumbia loud," the grilled snapper, and calling the evening "perfect" show strong investment in immediate sensory enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: He rerouted three pallets, fixed the order by four o'clock, and changed the client's tone once the problem was solved, demonstrating effective performance under pressure.

rationales.stimulation: He signs up for a weekend mezcal tasting and calls them "experiences," showing active pursuit of something new.
rationales.hedonism: His whole reason is simple: it "looked fun, just booked it," which is a straightforward choice for enjoyment.

rationales.stimulation: Spending four hours watching a replay while his "brain was climbing the walls" shows him trapped in sameness rather than moving toward what makes him feel alive.
rationales.conformity: He sat with Don Aurelio because "that's what you do," stayed quiet with Camila, and made coffee instead of voicing his frustration to keep the peace.

rationales.stimulation: Driving alone to Sayulita for clean waves and contrasting it with the office life he has "spent five years trying not to become" shows clear pursuit of a more vivid life.
rationales.hedonism: He is "laughing out loud" after a long ride, eating ceviche, and defending days spent surfing and doing "nothing useful for three days" as a good use of life.

rationales.security: She had the move-out timeline "mapped out" and reacts to the deposit increase with her "stomach" tightening, showing how strongly she relies on predictable plans.
She labels containers because "when things are out of order I can't think straight" and says the labels are "the one thing I can actually control right now."

rationales.self_direction: She says "I'd think about it" even though she already knows her answer, holding back her real choice instead of expressing it directly.
rationales.security: Her focus stays on the apartment savings, accident risk, insurance costs, and the fact that the motorcycle would add "four more months" before they can move.
rationales.conformity: She avoids being "the person who always says no" and follows the quieter "vamos a ver" pattern she learned at home to keep the conflict contained.

rationales.security: Packing the bag the night before, confirming the appointment, and wanting a moment where "nothing can go wrong" show deliberate planning for safety and predictability.
rationales.benevolence: She packs extra clothes and the vaccination booklet because she wants Sofia to feel "like someone has it handled," centering her daughter's well-being.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I know I could call someone myself" but waits for Ernesto to decide, suppressing her own judgment even while feeling uneasy.
rationales.security: Listening to the heater "rattle," worrying what it might do during Sofia's bath, and boiling water "just in case" show strong precaution around possible danger.
rationales.conformity: Because it is "his parents' house and their water heater," she defers to the household hierarchy rather than acting on her own concern.

rationales.security: She leans on laundry, the grocery list, and checking "twice" that they have everything, using routine and preparedness to regain steadiness on an exhausting day.

rationales.conformity: She "said nothing" and later admits she "didn't want to be the difficult one again," prioritizing smooth relations over speaking up.
rationales.universalism: By staying silent while a consultant suggested deprioritising "the homeless patients, the migrants, the people nobody's advocating for," she let unequal treatment stand.

rationales.benevolence: She got the woman "a proper referral to the physiotherapist" and carefully explained the process, providing direct help and care.
rationales.universalism: Working at the refugee clinic and insisting that "the ones who come through my door don't fall through the cracks" shows active concern for vulnerable people beyond her inner circle.
She is preoccupied with "plastic pollution in the North Sea," keeps up habits like bringing her own bags and recycling, and says the issue makes her angrier at those who could change it.

rationales.conformity: She "rushed through it in ten minutes" because the ward was short-staffed and chose "efficiency" to keep the system moving.
rationales.universalism: She admits she gave worse care to "an elderly man with no family contact" and would "probably not" have rushed if family were present, leaving the less-advocated-for patient worse off.

rationales.conformity: When "everyone laughed," she "didn't say anything either," going along with the room rather than challenging it.
rationales.universalism: Staying silent after a joke about refugee patients "costing us all money" leaves a demeaning attitude toward outsiders unchallenged.

rationales.conformity: She signed off on the cheaper supplier because "the ward manager was pushing hard" and she "didn't want to hold up the order," yielding to workplace pressure.
rationales.universalism: She knowingly approved a company with "terrible labour conditions in Southeast Asia," choosing convenience and budget over concern for distant workers.

rationales.conformity: Although she "pushed back in the meeting," she ultimately abstained because she "didn't want to be the only dissenting voice on record."
rationales.universalism: Abstaining let a motion pass that would mean "reduced access for people without valid papers," protecting her position over those patients' treatment.
She chose convenience repeatedly, from driving "because I didn't want to get wet" to using a disposable cup and Amazon, and says "the convenience keeps winning" despite her environmental concern.

rationales.conformity: She stayed quiet because she "didn't want to be seen challenging the new director in his first week," choosing office politics over speaking up.
rationales.benevolence: Fatima "needed one senior person to back her up" and she admits "I didn't do it," failing to support a colleague who came to her directly.
rationales.universalism: The unanswered concern was about whether targets would reduce time for "non-Dutch-speaking patients who need interpreters," and she left that equity issue undefended in the meeting.

rationales.benevolence: Bringing Fatima a coffee and promising action is a concrete gesture of care and support toward a colleague.
rationales.universalism: By saying she would "raise her question at the next ethics panel," she moves a concern into a forum that can affect fair treatment beyond one person.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects the standard path others push on him and defines stability on his own terms, preferring a setup where "if one app slows down, I've got two others."
rationales.hedonism: He centers the session on earnings, gas costs, and the car fund, saying "That car is everything" rather than prioritizing comfort or enjoyment.
rationales.security: He spells out that "stable ground" means enough in the car fund and backup apps, and he sets aside "$200 this week for the car fund."
rationales.conformity: He pushes back against what "people look at my life and think I should be doing" and rejects the warehouse route his sister keeps sending him.

rationales.security: He gets the car checked, notes the transmission is probably fine, and still plans to keep the car fund intact because "you never know."

rationales.self_direction: Instead of waiting passively, he "adjusted my availability" and decided to "push harder on Uber and Instacart," trying to steer his own way through the setback.
rationales.hedonism: He takes on "early morning grocery runs" he hates and dwells on lost income and stress rather than any present enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: He emphasizes his "4.91 rating," insists he has "never been late without a legitimate traffic reason," and immediately reorganizes work to keep performing.
rationales.power: He says "these companies have all the power" and that one complaint leaves him "scrambling," underscoring how little control he has.
rationales.security: With "35% of my weekly income just gone," money pulled from the car fund, and "I hate not knowing," the session is defined by instability rather than safety.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects the warehouse route because he does not want to be "depending on someone else deciding I'm worth keeping around," preferring to control his own work.
rationales.hedonism: He does "twelve hours between Uber and Instacart" and says "my back is killing me," clearly taking the harsher option rather than the pleasant one.
rationales.achievement: He puts in a 12-hour day and tracks that he "made $186," focusing on output and grinding through the setback.
rationales.security: He evaluates options in terms of being "first to get cut when things slow down" and prefers the apps because they feel less vulnerable to sudden loss.

rationales.self_direction: He focuses on "something I can do" and "something that's in my hands," trying to preserve personal agency after the deactivation scare.
rationales.hedonism: He plans "six-day weeks" and daily rating checks, choosing strain and vigilance over ease.
rationales.achievement: He immediately logged back in to work and plans extra days "until I'm back where I was," treating recovery as a performance problem to solve.
rationales.security: Rebuilding the "$300 in the car fund" and checking ratings for early warning are concrete attempts to reduce future vulnerability.

rationales.tradition: Lighting incense because 'my grandmother would approve' shows him leaning on an inherited family ritual before the client call.

rationales.self_direction: He insists that he can 'run my own thing' instead of taking over the family business, affirming his own chosen path.
rationales.conformity: Rather than argue, he 'changed the subject to Jason's wedding plans,' deliberately smoothing over the social tension.

rationales.tradition: The entry centers on 'Sunday dinner at Ah Ma's,' her decades-old recipe, and a family migration story that ties his present back to earlier generations.
rationales.benevolence: He spends the evening with family and is moved that Ah Ma checks whether he is 'eating enough and sleeping enough,' showing closeness to his people.

rationales.self_direction: He chose to 'start a tech consultancy' instead of staying in the family company and is still defending that decision as his own.
rationales.tradition: Looking up the village in Fujian and tracing 'three generations of building' shows him actively locating himself within his family lineage rather than discarding it.

rationales.achievement: 'Closed the deal with the logistics company' and landing a paid three-month engagement is a clear success marker in his work.
rationales.tradition: When his mother asks about Qing Ming, he answers 'yes, I'll be there' without hesitation, treating the family observance as a given priority.

rationales.conformity: When his uncle insults his work at the grave, he 'just smiled' because he 'didn't want to cause a scene,' restraining himself to preserve harmony.
rationales.tradition: He joins Qing Ming fully by carrying offerings, burning paper money, and doing 'the three bows' at his grandfather's grave.

rationales.self_direction: He tells his father 'I just want to build my own thing,' clearly defending an independent path.
rationales.conformity: The night turns into a 'real' fight with his father, showing open resistance rather than going along with family expectations.

rationales.tradition: He goes to the temple on the lunar schedule, learns to hold the joss sticks properly, and embraces 'being part of something older than yourself.'
rationales.benevolence: He joins the visit partly because 'she seemed pleased when I offered,' showing care for Ah Ma through shared time and attention.

rationales.security: He turns down 'probably six months of runway,' knowingly giving up financial cushion for principle.
rationales.tradition: He refuses the gambling client because using his grandfather's name that way felt 'wrong' and conflicted with the family story about gambling ruining an earlier generation.
The birthday dinner follows a long-running family pattern at 'the one we've been going to since I was a kid,' and the jade bracelet fits that inherited style.
rationales.benevolence: He shows up for his mother's birthday and gives her 'a jade bracelet,' a concrete act of care despite the tension with his father.

rationales.achievement: He works until '9pm' to stabilize the client's site during a promotion, prioritizing competent performance under pressure.
rationales.tradition: Missing 'Sunday dinner at Ah Ma's' for the first time in years breaks a recurring family practice he had reliably kept.
rationales.benevolence: He explicitly frames the day as choosing 'the client over the grandmother,' putting work ahead of a close family relationship.

rationales.tradition: The visit includes Ah Ma's braised pork, her paper offerings for Vesak Day, and a story linking him back to his grandfather's habits.
rationales.benevolence: He goes to pick up the food she saved for him and spends time sitting with her, showing renewed attention to a close family bond.

rationales.tradition: She lights 'a candle at my altar,' says a prayer, and explicitly says 'that's what we do in my family' when explaining how she responds to grief.
rationales.benevolence: After losing the child patient, she stays 'an extra hour' holding his mother's hand and says she would 'do it again tomorrow.'

rationales.security: She likes the apartment idea because it would 'cut costs' and living with family feels trustworthy and dependable.
rationales.benevolence: She wants Marisol nearby because 'she's family, so I trust her,' emphasizing closeness with a specific loved one.

rationales.tradition: She recreates Posadas with songs, luminarias, tamales, and reflections about the 'thread connecting me to where I come from.'
rationales.benevolence: She organizes the celebration for pediatric patients and brings in food so the children can share in it, extending warmth and care to people around her.

rationales.self_direction: She admits she is 'curious' about the offer but shuts that impulse down as 'selfish,' subordinating her own exploratory desire.
rationales.stimulation: She rejects travel nursing because it would mean 'no knowing where I'll be month to month' and says 'I'm supposed to be building something stable, not chasing adventures.'
rationales.security: Her reasons center on a 'consistent schedule,' saving for the condo, and staying where her support system already is.
rationales.conformity: Calling the alternative 'selfish' and saying 'I'm supposed to be' doing something else shows her aligning herself with expected obligations.
rationales.benevolence: She stays partly because 'my cousin is counting on me' and because her family 'gave up a lot' for her, prioritizing those close ties.

rationales.security: Her New Year list focuses on 'get the condo' and 'building something solid, something that will last,' showing a strong pull toward stability.
rationales.tradition: Nochebuena, midnight mass, incense, and changing the water for her santos all show deliberate continuity with inherited religious and family practices.
rationales.benevolence: She spends the holiday with cousins and includes 'help Marisol settle in' and 'call home more often' in her plans for the coming year.

rationales.security: Setting up 'automatic transfers to my savings account' so the balance grows each month is a direct move toward financial stability.

rationales.conformity: She snaps back at Rachel and says 'I'm not going to change who I am to make Rachel comfortable,' openly rejecting local pressure to adapt.
rationales.tradition: She defends calling Mami and says 'we've been this way for generations,' grounding her response in inherited family patterns.
rationales.benevolence: The whole conflict comes from how strongly she values staying connected to her mother and treating family closeness as non-negotiable.

rationales.security: She is distressed because she had 'planned around this' condo and now has to 'start over,' showing how much she wanted a stable housing plan.
rationales.benevolence: The condo mattered because it meant being near 'our other cousins' and close enough for easy 'Sunday dinners,' centering close family life.

rationales.tradition: She decides that whatever she builds 'will have roots' and says 'keeping our ways alive' can continue even across distance, reaffirming continuity with those before her.
rationales.benevolence: The breakthrough comes through a deep conversation with Abuela and renewed attachment to 'her, and Mami, and everyone who came before,' emphasizing close family bonds.

rationales.achievement: She spent "all morning fixing the matching algorithm," prioritizing improving the platform over other demands.
rationales.benevolence: She admits she keeps "choosing the code over the people," ignoring tutors who might quit and not returning her mother's calls.
She says she's "hunched over my laptop ignoring everyone" and was annoyed when Soyeon checked on her, showing withdrawal from caring responses to others.
Minjun's message that tutoring made him feel less "stupid" moves her to tears and becomes the reason she keeps doing the work.

rationales.self_direction: She tells her father "you can't half-build something like this," defending her choice to leave school and commit fully to her own path.
rationales.security: Rejecting the option to do "school and this" shows willingness to give up a more predictable route for the startup.
rationales.universalism: The work is framed around "forty-seven kids whose parents can't afford hagwon," tying her choice to wider educational access.

rationales.self_direction: "My path looks nothing like that and I'm fine with it" explicitly embraces a self-authored route instead of the prestigious corporate track.

rationales.security: She says "I can't keep bleeding money" but still resists the more stable options of charging families or finding a sponsor.
rationales.benevolence: She keeps coming back to Minjun and says she'd tell him "it's not his fault," trying to protect him from feeling abandoned.
rationales.universalism: "The whole point was that it's free" and that nobody should have to "qualify or prove they deserve help," showing commitment to open access.

rationales.self_direction: Even under pressure, she says "I don't regret leaving school," reaffirming a path she chose for herself.
rationales.security: The conflict centers on her having "nothing stable" and still standing by the decision that created that instability.

rationales.hedonism: She "stayed up until 2am" onboarding the new tutor, sacrificing rest and comfort.
rationales.achievement: Finding the replacement and finishing onboarding late into the night shows strong task-focus and follow-through.
rationales.benevolence: Finding "a replacement tutor for Minjun" shows direct effort to keep support in place for a student.

rationales.self_direction: She deletes "I'm in!" three times and says that if they said yes she'd have to justify wanting time for herself instead of choosing freely.
rationales.hedonism: She wants the spa, misses "laughing with them about nothing," and still plans to say no because self-denial feels easier.
rationales.conformity: She anticipates her mother-in-law's disappointment and says "it's easier to be the one who sacrifices," explicitly bending to expected behavior.
rationales.tradition: Her day is organized around making the pooja meal correctly, including the dal consistency and rice grain relatives will judge.

rationales.hedonism: She is exhausted and longing just to "lie down somewhere quiet" while the relaxing outing happens without her.
rationales.conformity: She keeps nodding "yes, good idea" to remedies she doesn't trust because she has no energy to push back.
rationales.benevolence: She spends the day cleaning vomit, changing sheets, and caring for both sick children despite her exhaustion.

rationales.self_direction: She says it's hard "to just ask for what I want" and that she has stopped asking altogether, showing reduced agency around her own choices.
rationales.hedonism: She describes learning "how to not need things" and shrinking pleasure down to a 20-minute chai ritual, signaling chronic self-denial.

rationales.self_direction: She chose to come home instead of going for drinks because "I wanted this more," following her own preference rather than the social default.
rationales.hedonism: The entry dwells on "a glass of something cold," golden light, and the fact that "the balcony doesn't ask anything from me."

rationales.self_direction: She says she "folded" and left feeling like she'd "agreed to be someone I'm not," showing a choice against her own judgment.
rationales.security: "She's the one signing off on my certification" makes protecting a stable outcome the reason she stays quiet.
rationales.conformity: She smiles, nods, and promises to work on it because she doesn't want to be "difficult" and wants to do what everyone else does.

rationales.self_direction: "I don't know who I did it for" shows she acted against her own judgment rather than from conviction.
rationales.conformity: She enacted the "firmer boundaries" approach even though her mentor wasn't watching, showing compliance with the expected method.
rationales.benevolence: Making the children "wait in silence" and feeling she'd "taken something away" from a little one cuts against her caring approach with them.

rationales.conformity: She "edit[s]" what she tells her mother so the call stays pleasant and everyone can "perform their part" without friction.
rationales.benevolence: She withholds her struggles because she doesn't want to give her mother "anything to worry about."

rationales.conformity: She stays silent in the staff meeting because "that's my place right now" and says it's smarter to learn how things work before speaking.
rationales.benevolence: The moment she holds onto is Nour reading aloud and grinning after being given time, showing deep investment in a child's wellbeing.

rationales.hedonism: She genuinely prefers "leftover macarons and a show" to the party and contrasts it with "bad speakers and warm beer."
rationales.conformity: She keeps replaying one honest comment because it felt like she'd "broken some rule," and the avoidance is driven by fear of social misstep.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I just don't want to become that teacher," prioritizing her own teaching philosophy over adapting fully to the rubric.
rationales.achievement: "I'd rather meet expectations" than chase the higher rating they want, showing willingness to forgo recognized success.
rationales.benevolence: She centers the child who felt "safe enough to read out loud" and wants a classroom kids "actually want to be" in.

rationales.hedonism: "I cannot even enjoy it properly" and "Still marking" show she is sacrificing rest and immediate comfort to keep working.
rationales.achievement: "Marking forty-two exam papers" and staying there even as her "eyes are giving out" shows she keeps pushing through demanding work.
rationales.benevolence: She says "Mummy-ji got empty promises" and "I did not even sit with Rajesh while he ate," so close family care was neglected.
"She noticed" and "she just paid attention" show how much she values small, unsolicited care within the family.

rationales.tradition: Hearing Aarav say "Nani" moves her more than years of being called "Meena ma'am," grounding her in an intergenerational family role.
rationales.benevolence: She is overwhelmed by her grandson's first "Nani" and then sits teary with Mummy-ji, centering intimate family connection.
She spends the morning helping Sunita with the transfer application and answers "It is her parents, what else would I tell her," prioritizing a close other's family need.

rationales.conformity: She "kept quiet" because she "did not see the point in adding to the noise," deliberately restraining complaint after the decision was made.

rationales.benevolence: She set up "an extra cushion" for Kamla-ji's knee and told Rajesh to rest because "his back has been bothering him," showing attentive care.

rationales.conformity: She holds back on signing because the principal "came to Ritu's wedding" and she worries the letter will "land as a personal thing," prioritizing relationship smoothness.
rationales.benevolence: Even though she says "the younger teachers are burning out" and agrees with the letter, she withholds support from colleagues who need it.

rationales.conformity: "What feels right is staying out of it" and "Not everything needs to be a stand" show her leaning toward non-confrontation rather than open opposition.
rationales.benevolence: She still has "not signed Neha's letter," leaving coworkers without her backing despite knowing the issue matters.

rationales.self_direction: The curator describes work that is "everything I've been moving away from," but she decides "I'll make something new," abandoning her own direction.
rationales.achievement: She frames the compromise around "better representation, better sales" and a step toward the career she has worked for.
rationales.conformity: She "nodded along" and claimed she had pieces that would "fit perfectly," reshaping her work to match the brief.

rationales.self_direction: "Nothing that feels like mine but everything that fits" shows her making work against her own artistic ownership.
rationales.achievement: She immediately starts "three new sketches" and focuses on the "October 15th" deadline and staying focused.
rationales.conformity: The sketches are built around what "fits," signaling compliance with outside expectations.

rationales.self_direction: She says "No. It does not feel right" and that her "actual work stays hidden," showing her choices are pulling away from her own vision.
rationales.achievement: She keeps justifying the choice through career logic, networking fallout, and what has "paid my rent" over eight years.
rationales.security: She worries saying no would ripple through "every networking dinner and studio visit" and says "feeling right" has not "paid my rent," prioritizing stability.
rationales.conformity: She says the art world runs on "making people comfortable" and keeps meeting those expectations.

rationales.achievement: "Worked twelve hours" and calling the pieces "competent" show a heavy push toward disciplined, results-oriented output.

rationales.self_direction: In the night sessions she paints "without thinking about who's going to see it" and works in ways that finally feel alive to her.
rationales.achievement: The meeting centers on "sales figures," "upcoming opportunities," and the "strategic move" of Rotterdam, and she keeps the conversation there.
rationales.conformity: Instead of voicing her doubts, she "thanked him for his guidance and asked about the fair calendar," staying inside the expected professional script.

rationales.self_direction: She sees herself as "the person who plays by the rules" and swallows what she wanted to say, letting career pressure override her own line.
rationales.achievement: She insists her art has to "actually function as a career," treating decisions through professional payoff.
rationales.security: "Some of us need our art to actually function as a career" and her contrast with people who have trust funds shows strong concern with financial footing.
rationales.conformity: She calls herself the one who "plays by the rules" and only says she is "excited about the opportunity," smoothing over conflict.

rationales.achievement: She measures herself through "Gallery representation before 30. Consistent sales. Press coverage," making external success the central yardstick.
rationales.security: "If I follow that feeling I lose everything I've built" shows how strongly she is protecting the stability of her current career.

rationales.self_direction: The work being "exactly what we discussed" and her brisk "Moving on" show her executing an external brief rather than her own direction.
rationales.achievement: Finishing the pieces, sending them to the curator, and submitting them to Pieter for documentation all emphasize completion and professional follow-through.
rationales.conformity: The curator's praise that they were "exactly what we discussed" signals successful alignment with the agreed expectations.

rationales.stimulation: She is drawn to the room's "energy," people "arguing about" art, and canvases that "broke every rule," showing attraction to vivid, disruptive experience.

rationales.self_direction: "Every phrase I type sounds like someone else wrote it" and choosing "Rotterdam first" keep her moving away from her own work.
rationales.achievement: "Whatever will get me into the next show" and her immediate focus on Rotterdam show continued prioritizing career advancement.
rationales.conformity: The formulaic artist statement and her decision to keep the "hidden canvases" hidden show her staying with the expected professional performance.

rationales.stimulation: "same maintenance tickets I have been doing for three years" frames the work as numbing repetition rather than challenge or novelty.
rationales.conformity: He is still showing up to estimate the familiar tickets in sprint planning, sticking with the expected routine despite his frustration.

rationales.self_direction: He says he wanted to support the rewrite but "did not say that," suppressing his own preference in the meeting.
rationales.stimulation: He wanted to "learn something new instead of patching the same codebase forever" but argued against the rewrite.
rationales.security: He centered "the timeline and the risk to the December release" and Jennifer's unknown regulatory gaps.
rationales.conformity: He took the "adult" role and voiced the safer organizational line instead of the view he privately preferred.

rationales.stimulation: He accepts Elena's gift and says "I will go to the conference," choosing the novel unconference despite his hesitation.

rationales.self_direction: He says "I did not say that either" and later "I make myself small," showing he is suppressing his own voice.
rationales.achievement: He fixed the 3 AM incident, documented it, and was explaining it in standup by 8 AM.
rationales.power: The VP's message "made me feel small," and he responds by deliberately shrinking himself rather than asserting influence.
rationales.security: He proposes "better monitoring" and shared renewal dates, then keeps rechecking the incident for anything he might have missed.
rationales.conformity: He gives the "safe answer" and the "team player answer," absorbing blame so the group can keep functioning.

rationales.self_direction: He describes moving through "the usual routine" on autopilot, as if only "my pattern" is present rather than his own choices.
rationales.stimulation: The day is reduced to repeated alerts, standups, and questions he has "answered a hundred times," which he experiences as deadening routine.
rationales.conformity: He keeps executing the expected workflow exactly as usual, even while feeling detached from it.

rationales.self_direction: He takes the trip on his own terms, even choosing a solo waterfront walk over the dinner group and nearly proposing a session that mattered to him.
rationales.stimulation: He flew to an unconference with spontaneous sessions and says "I feel different here," leaning into novelty and change.
rationales.benevolence: He calls his mother from the trip and says "Some lies are kindness," showing active attention to her feelings.

rationales.self_direction: The sessions that mattered most are the ones he feels he "cannot really present in a corporate setting," so he hides what he actually brought back.
rationales.stimulation: When asked what he will do differently, he has "no good answer" and reduces the experience to "the usual conference stuff."
rationales.security: He defaults to the safer, corporate-safe summary rather than surfacing the harder lessons that might unsettle his role.
rationales.conformity: He sticks to what is acceptable in a "corporate setting" instead of sharing the more personal takeaways.

rationales.self_direction: He says what he wants is "not realistic at my level," yielding to the role instead of pursuing it.
rationales.stimulation: He contrasts real work with "writing status reports about why it is not done yet," framing the job as stagnating rather than energizing.
rationales.hedonism: He has been working "ten-hour days" through December, sacrificing ease and enjoyment to keep the release moving.
rationales.achievement: He is pushing hard to keep the team "on track for the holiday release" despite sickness and scope creep.
rationales.conformity: He is the one who has to explain the deprioritization "without making leadership look bad," managing upward expectations.
rationales.benevolence: He is spending his energy managing the team's and stakeholders' frustration so the release can keep moving.

rationales.self_direction: He directly asks to move teams because he wants "to learn something new before I am too old to learn," choosing his own path.
rationales.stimulation: He seeks the Go rewrite and wants to "remember what it felt like to not know everything," actively pursuing novelty.
rationales.achievement: He asks to join "not as a lead, not as an architect" but "just as a developer," stepping away from status-based success markers.
rationales.power: Requesting the move "not as a lead" is an explicit choice to give up authority and rank.
rationales.security: He prefers the possibility of "flounder in a new language" because it feels "more alive than certainty."
rationales.conformity: The request reads to his manager like "a demotion," showing he is moving against the expected career script.

rationales.self_direction: He followed through on the transfer and is already studying for the move he chose.
rationales.stimulation: Starting on Jennifer's team and learning Go makes him feel "like a student again."
rationales.achievement: He is putting in nightly work on Go tutorials to prepare himself for the new role.
He is mapping out "visibility" and cross-functional scope so his name comes up for the platform team lead role.
rationales.power: He wants a role where he is seen for larger scope and leadership, not just individual sprint delivery.
rationales.conformity: He says "you do what's expected" and treats those obligations as a given.
rationales.tradition: The temple renovation fund and his line that "these things matter" tie his choices to family and religious continuity.
rationales.benevolence: He sends extra money and frames family care as a duty he should meet.

rationales.tradition: He keeps postponing taking Priya to meet his parents, delaying a step that both of them clearly understand as important.
rationales.benevolence: He admits "we both know I'm stalling," leaving Priya in uncertainty rather than responding to her directly.

rationales.hedonism: He says being indispensable "costs me sleep" and still prefers it.
rationales.achievement: He wants the kind of standing that gets promoted, not merely to survive the on-call.
rationales.power: He explicitly says he would "rather be indispensable," valuing a position others cannot function without.

rationales.self_direction: He dislikes having to "play this game" but decides to adapt anyway, subordinating his own preferred standard of success.
rationales.achievement: He immediately studies executive communication and says "I'll figure it out" because he refuses to fail at something he commits to.
rationales.power: He is focused on the traits that let people "get ahead" and avoid the ceiling he now sees.
rationales.conformity: He shifts toward the communication style leadership wants, learning to frame issues in the expected business language.

rationales.achievement: He treats the presentation as a performance target and records the VP's reaction as a "Small win."
rationales.power: Presenting the roadmap to leadership and winning the VP's approval advances his influence upward.
rationales.conformity: He intentionally used "business metrics instead of technical specs," tailoring himself to leadership expectations.

rationales.power: He defines the sequence himself and expects Priya to accept that timing.
rationales.security: He insists on "flat, savings, stable position" before marriage, prioritizing foundation over immediacy.
rationales.conformity: He frames the order as something one "should" do and expects that standard to govern the relationship.
rationales.tradition: He grounds the decision in what "my parents expect" and in Amma's warning about rushing marriage.
rationales.benevolence: Priya wants commitment now, but he keeps her waiting and tells himself she "should understand" the delay.

rationales.achievement: He lists IIT, Bangalore, and "what I'm actually achieving" as the scoreboard he cares about.
rationales.power: He rejects Karthik's modest ceiling because he wants "more" and keeps framing life as climbing.
rationales.tradition: Marriage, children, and "temple visits" still carry enough weight that not meeting those expectations stings.
rationales.benevolence: His comparison reduces Karthik to someone who "posts cricket memes and calls it a day," showing little generosity toward family.

rationales.hedonism: He tells Priya there will "probably" be more late nights and treats present sacrifice as the price of advancement.
rationales.achievement: He is documenting initiatives, practicing his framing, and preparing intensely for the panel interview.
rationales.power: The goal is explicitly the platform team lead role, and he is shaping himself around what the CTO values.
rationales.tradition: He endorses Appa's view that the early years are for investment and the later years for returns.
rationales.benevolence: Priya's concern is met with more delay and sacrifice, as he asks the relationship to absorb the cost of his ambition.

rationales.self_direction: He stays grounded in "I did my part. Now I wait," relying on his own assessment rather than spiraling for reassurance.
rationales.achievement: He treats the interview as something to execute well and judges himself by whether he did his part.
rationales.power: His answer to the CTO centers "expectations and consequences," fitting his push toward authority over others' performance.

rationales.achievement: He marks the promotion as being "ahead now," treating the role as a clear competitive win.
rationales.power: The new title, the CTO's approval, and "leadership philosophy" all center authority and status.
rationales.benevolence: Vikram's congratulations are filtered through "gritted teeth" and dismissed with "Doesn't matter," showing little warmth toward a peer.

rationales.self_direction: He says "Maybe it's easier to keep the tracks separate," choosing concealment over fully integrating what he wants with others' expectations.
rationales.achievement: He notes he hit "exactly what I set out to achieve" with the promotion, salary, direct reports, and C-suite visibility.
rationales.power: Explaining "headcount, budget ownership, technical direction" highlights how much authority the new role carries.
rationales.tradition: "I always come for Pongal" and the weight of the family wedding conversation tie him strongly to inherited obligations.
rationales.benevolence: He is trying to hold care for Priya and his parents in the same frame, even calling those expectations "load-bearing."

rationales.self_direction: She rejects the civil service pressure with "I'm fine. The studio is fine. Rent is covered," holding to her own path rather than the safer one her family wants.
rationales.conformity: Instead of arguing, she says she has to "go check the kiln" and exits the call, managing the tension without openly upsetting her aunt or mother.

rationales.tradition: She hears "halmeoni's voice" saying that "clay remembers what your hands were thinking" and judges the lucrative collaboration against that inherited standard.

rationales.achievement: She spent "two weeks on that application," reworked her portfolio, and wanted the residency because it was something her family could "point to," showing strong investment in accomplishment and recognition.
rationales.conformity: She keeps sending "congratulations emojis and exclamation marks" and says her face hurts from "performing enthusiasm," suppressing her real reaction to avoid social friction.

rationales.achievement: She goes to the studio because "the pieces for the January show won't fire themselves" and carefully judges the jar's walls, foot, and shoulder curve against exacting craft standards.
rationales.tradition: Her eye for the "slight asymmetry" comes from what "halmeoni always said," and that inherited way of judging the work still guides her.

rationales.self_direction: She tells Eun-bi "I'm not doing it" and rejects the collaboration even after being told it is how young artists "build a name now," choosing her own standard over the market's.
rationales.tradition: She measures a meaningful career by her grandmother's forty years in the workshop and says "that's the kind of career that makes sense to me," explicitly anchoring herself in inherited practice.

rationales.security: She tracks that her "emergency cushion is thinner than I'd like going into winter" and calculates exactly how many months of rent and studio fees remain, showing strong concern with stability.
rationales.benevolence: Even after seeing the shortfall, she "transferred Umma's share," prioritizing support for her mother while money is tight.

rationales.security: She lingers on the calm of "nothing urgent. Nothing broken" and accepts the steady, predictable rhythm of the kiln and heater, taking comfort in stability.
rationales.tradition: She makes lunar new year gifts with her grandmother's celadon glaze, still adjusting it "by eye the way she showed me," which is a direct continuation of family practice.

rationales.self_direction: She values "all the space I've made for myself out here" and wants to show that the beadwork she is selling is "mine," signaling investment in a life she built on her own terms.
rationales.tradition: She lets Grandma's call go to voicemail and is still undecided about Christmas even though home means cedar tea, stories, and teachings she worries she "abandoned."
rationales.benevolence: She knows Grandma is 74 and says "I should call her back. I will. Tomorrow," but keeps postponing the call despite feeling like "the worst granddaughter alive."

rationales.security: Her decision is constrained by "three delivery shifts" she cannot cancel, a car that needs an oil change, and whether she can "afford the gas," showing strong attention to practical stability.
rationales.tradition: She says that when there is a ceremony "you just go," yet she is still weighing whether to attend against work and money, falling short of the communal obligation she grew up with.
rationales.benevolence: Even with Uncle Ray sick and knowing "it would mean a lot" if family were there, she is hesitating to show up for him.

rationales.security: She "took Friday off," canceled Saturday, and says her Instacart rating dropped to 4.7 "because I cancelled Saturday but I don't care," putting family and ceremony ahead of work stability.
rationales.tradition: She re-enters the ceremony, makes cedar tea correctly, keeps beadwork going because it is something "my grandmother's grandmother did," and plans more rituals to carry home forward.
rationales.benevolence: She makes the long drive for Uncle Ray and stays physically close to Grandma, who "held my hand the whole time," showing concrete follow-through for family.

rationales.self_direction: He emphasizes that the chapter's framing is "mine -- not his" and remembers he chose the PhD path instead of staying in a titled job, underscoring self-authored work.
rationales.achievement: He says the outline is "solid," sees a "standalone article" in it, and cares about publications that demonstrate his own competence.

rationales.self_direction: He "sat there biting my tongue" because challenging sloppy reasoning in the lab has become too costly, suppressing his own judgment.
rationales.conformity: Even though he thinks the proposal is weak, he stays silent in a room full of "performative agreement" to avoid the consequences of open disagreement.

rationales.self_direction: He says yes to the policy brief "immediately" and then notices how wrong it feels to need anyone's permission for work he is fully qualified to do.
rationales.conformity: He accepts outside work despite the lab's "unspoken expectation" that it be cleared through Villanueva first, pushing against the norm rather than complying with it.

rationales.self_direction: He says the PhD matters because he is "doing the actual thinking now" and, when pressed, says "at least I chose these ones," framing the path as his own decision.
rationales.achievement: His mother asks how much the brief paid and compares him to a cousin who "just got promoted to director," while he measures whether the PhD trade-off is worth it, showing clear concern with professional accomplishment.

rationales.self_direction: He explicitly refuses to let the Peru chapter become something his advisor can claim, insisting "this is the one piece of work in this entire program that is entirely mine."
rationales.power: He frames the fight in terms of authorship, portfolio, and legacy, recognizing that putting Villanueva's name on the chapter would let his advisor absorb control and status from the work.

rationales.self_direction: Writing the policy brief feels like "muscle memory" and reminds him what it is like to work without someone else policing the output, which he clearly prefers.
rationales.power: He sees that Villanueva can use "a hundred small levers" like delayed feedback and funding sign-offs, highlighting how little institutional control he currently has.

Villanueva is already testing the narrative of his "collaboration style" through Marina, showing how someone with institutional standing can shape how he is perceived without confronting him directly.

rationales.self_direction: He is weighing whether to "submit the Peru chapter independently" because waiting means the work "won't be mine anymore," showing strong commitment to authorship and autonomy.
rationales.achievement: He finishes a "clean" 14-page policy brief in less than a week and calls the Peru chapter "the best thing I've written," foregrounding output and excellence.
rationales.power: He explicitly analyzes how "the person with the title gets to define the terms" and treats submission as a fight over who controls the chapter and its story.

rationales.self_direction: She wants to say "something sharp" back but swallows it and stays silent, overriding her own response.
rationales.conformity: She says nothing because it would have "made dinner weird" with Dan's mom there, prioritizing smooth family dynamics over speaking up.

rationales.achievement: She says wanting this life and never hearing "good job" or "thank you" are different, and worries it may not "count," showing a felt lack of recognition for competent work.
rationales.security: She and Dan chose staying home because daycare costs and salary "didn't make sense," grounding the decision in household stability.
rationales.benevolence: She structures her days around "two kids, the house, Dan's travel schedule, appointments, groceries, meal prep," organizing her effort around caring for her family.

rationales.self_direction: She "just agreed with whatever she said" even though it was not her real view, and notices the pattern on the drive home.
rationales.conformity: The imagined risk is being seen as "the difficult one," so she aligns herself with the other mom automatically to avoid even minor social friction.

rationales.self_direction: She says, "I committed us before I even thought about what I wanted," showing that her own preference never got real consideration.
rationales.hedonism: She wanted to "just stay home and order pizza" or have "just a quiet day," but gives that up without even pausing to choose it.
rationales.conformity: Backing out would be "a thing" and might sound "selfish," so she goes along to avoid upsetting the family.
rationales.tradition: She accepts the familiar Sunday dinner pattern, bringing "a salad and the rolls, same as always" for the full-house family gathering.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I never say it" about what she wants and realizes even a simple "let me check with Dan first" felt impossible, showing how little room she gives her own preference.
rationales.conformity: She avoids that perfectly normal boundary because it would feel like "making waves," even when she admits nobody might notice.

rationales.stimulation: He treated the "packaging I didn't recognize" as a reason to reject the cheaper option, avoiding the unfamiliar rather than trying it.
rationales.security: He spent "twenty minutes" comparing brands and chose the expensive familiar one, prioritizing predictability over saving money.
"I've spent three years building a buffer" and the thought of the loan hitting his credit report makes his "chest tight," so he protects financial stability first.
rationales.benevolence: Even though "He's family" and "he's trying," he lets a "hard wall" go up instead of taking on risk to help his cousin.

rationales.stimulation: He treats the Jeddah assignment as "gambling what already works," showing reluctance toward a new and uncertain move.
rationales.security: He delays on a promotion track because "staying here" preserves the arrangement that already feels dependable.
"Meal-prepped for the whole week," replaced expired first aid supplies, and checked flashlight batteries so "Tomorrow feels manageable because today I made it so."
He checked the supply order "three times" because the idea of "something going wrong, even something small" feels like standing "on a ledge."
He insists he is not holding on too tight because other people "let go too easily and then act surprised when things fall apart," framing vigilance as protection against loss.

rationales.achievement: He ties his style to performance, saying "things don't fall through" because he checks and follows up, presenting himself as the reason the team runs well.
rationales.security: His defense of being careful centers on preventing failures: "things would fall through constantly" if no one kept checking.
rationales.benevolence: He points out that he helped Faisal cancel his cards and frames his follow-up as taking care of the people around him, not just himself.

rationales.security: He has "six months of expenses saved" yet keeps running worst-case scenarios because he still feels that "the floor could always open."

rationales.self_direction: She celebrates students debating instead of waiting for the "right" answer, encouraging independent thinking rather than rote compliance.
rationales.universalism: The discussion centers on a "coastal development project," and she values that students engage with a broader public issue instead of treating it as someone else's problem.

rationales.self_direction: After being told to "tone down the activism," she just "nodded and said I'd think about it," yielding ground instead of openly defending her approach.
rationales.conformity: She treats the principal's warning as "just politics" and responds with a polite nod, adapting her behavior to institutional expectations.
rationales.universalism: She pushes back on calling ocean pollution "foreign problems" by insisting "the Arabian Sea is not foreign to Kerala," tying the lesson to wider ecological harm.

rationales.self_direction: She decides to "soften my approach" and teach in ways that "don't raise red flags," limiting her own preferred style to fit the institution.
rationales.security: Her strategy is driven partly by staying in place: "if I get pushed out" the students lose even this imperfect version, so preserving her position matters.
rationales.conformity: She plans to be "strategic" and avoid "red flags," showing willingness to work within the school's constraints rather than confront them head-on.
rationales.universalism: She keeps prioritizing students asking questions about "their own coastline," holding onto the public-interest content even while changing tactics.

rationales.tradition: She skipped the Sunday drive they have made "every Sunday since before the kids were born," and describes it as feeling like she "broke something."
rationales.benevolence: She stayed home knowing "Papa sets the table for all of us" and felt sick hearing him "putting the plates away," showing she failed people she usually shows up for.

rationales.self_direction: She keeps insisting "it's just what we do" even while wondering whether it is "just fear of doing something different from what I know," so she resists choosing a different path.
rationales.hedonism: She keeps making the trip "even when it's pouring rain or one of them is sick" and pulls Hugo from football, sacrificing comfort and ease.
rationales.tradition: She defends the weekly visits as "what we do" and refuses to be "the one who lets that happen" when the family table might shrink.
rationales.benevolence: Her main concern is that "My parents eat alone" if she stops showing up, so she prioritizes keeping close family connected.

rationales.tradition: "Four generations of women and a potato dish" and "The recipe box isn't just recipes" show her treating inherited family practices as something to carry forward.
rationales.benevolence: She brings Lea into the kitchen to "help me stir the bechamel" so the meal becomes an act of caring for and teaching her child.

rationales.security: The weekly rhythm of Sunday lunch and "of course, that's what we do" gives her a stable routine that feels reassuring again.
rationales.tradition: She centers family customs like Papa's cassoulet and the vineyard story, noting these are "details that disappear if nobody passes them on."
rationales.benevolence: She is pleased Hugo wants to return next week and treats gathering the family together as something worth sustaining for the people closest to her.

rationales.self_direction: She wonders whether staying home is "just fear of doing something different from what I know," suggesting she is not choosing freely so much as avoiding departure from the family script.
rationales.stimulation: The possibility of working again is framed as "something different from what I know," and that unfamiliarity itself helps keep her from pursuing it.
rationales.hedonism: She admits she has "started counting the hours until bedtime" yet keeps choosing the more draining path over anything restorative for herself.
rationales.achievement: Even though "I was good at it" and had colleagues, she pushes that professional part of herself aside instead of pursuing it.
rationales.security: Being home when the children return and never having them come home to "an empty house" is presented as the safer, more predictable arrangement.
rationales.conformity: The loudest guide is the inherited voice of "Maman, and Grand-mere before her," and she measures herself against that expected model.
rationales.tradition: She explicitly grounds her choice in how "My mother did all of that" and how the women before her organized family life the same way.
rationales.benevolence: She wants to be "here when they come home," cook for them, know their teachers, and build her days around caring for the children.

rationales.hedonism: She says the extra time will come "From sleep, probably" or from the one evening she usually keeps for herself, giving up rest and leisure.
rationales.tradition: She volunteers because the procession was part of what her parents did as children and fears that without action "it just disappears."
rationales.benevolence: She would rather be tired than have to explain to Hugo why "the lanterns aren't happening anymore," making the effort for family and close community.
rationales.universalism: She signs up to organize a parish event because "someone has to," taking responsibility for preserving a shared community tradition beyond her own household.

rationales.achievement: She emphasizes "Four months of work" and that the pieces looked "exactly the way I wanted them to look," showing sustained effort toward a high standard.
rationales.security: Her first response is relief that "the gallery check will clear next week," tying the finished batch directly to financial stability.

rationales.self_direction: She defends the life she built on her own terms, saying "I do have a plan" even if it does not come with "a business card."

rationales.security: She spends the day doing inventory, adjusting purchases because "the exchange rate is worse," and booking easy workshop income that "helps with the monthly numbers."

rationales.self_direction: She says the workshop income lets her "say no to design freelance gigs I do not want" and calls it "a trade I chose," foregrounding autonomy.
rationales.security: The entry is organized around managing "what I want to make" against "what I need to sell," with two ledgers she keeps in balance.

rationales.self_direction: She accepts the show because the pieces were "made for myself" and getting them in front of someone who might connect with them matters more than easier options.
rationales.security: She agrees to "no artist fee" while already calculating shipping costs and calling it a "logistics headache," so stability is knowingly deprioritized.
Signing a "six-month lease renewal" at the same rate makes the studio situation feel fixed and predictable.

rationales.self_direction: Instead of letting the accident go, she saves the residue in a jar and says "I will try to figure out what happened," showing independent experimentation.
rationales.achievement: Her frustration comes from months of careful testing, with "temperature logs" and glaze ratios in notebooks, all aimed at mastering the result.
She carefully builds the crate herself, then keeps refining the copper-green glaze by adjusting ratios and fitting a test tile into the kiln, reflecting deliberate craft improvement.
rationales.security: She wraps the vessels in "three layers" and says she is "not trusting them to someone else's packing job," prioritizing protection of work that took weeks to make.
She tracks "every fils so carefully" so she can honestly say "everything is fine" and remain able to cover family needs.
rationales.benevolence: When her mother sounds worried, she wires money "so she will not have to ask again for a while" and frames her budgeting as carrying "more than just my own weight."

rationales.self_direction: She chooses the extra workshop because it is "my studio, my schedule, my terms," even though freelance work would pay better.
rationales.security: She reviews rent, fuel, shipping, and buffers in detail, then picks the option that keeps "the math" working even if it stays tight.

rationales.hedonism: He refuses to give up the parrilla, saying "I'm not going to stop living just because the spreadsheet says I should."
rationales.security: He calculates exactly what he needs, earmarks money for motorcycle insurance, and posts "DO NOT touch savings" on the fridge.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects going back to a warehouse because he does not want a younger boss "telling me what to do," and insists his current arrangement is his choice.
rationales.conformity: He pushes back against his sister's expectation that he should return to a conventional job, saying "Not everyone lives like that."

rationales.self_direction: Even while doubting the trade-off, he still frames not having "a boss breathing down my neck" as the reason he chose this life and refuses to "crawl back."
rationales.security: The whole entry is about unstable pay, canceled jobs, and not knowing where the motorcycle payment will come from, making his setup feel unsafe and unpredictable.
A "good-sized job" that will cover two days of work makes him feel he can "breathe again," because it restores short-term stability.
He spends the Alfredo-job savings on the brake repair "because what choice do I have," prioritizing the safety and income source he depends on.

rationales.hedonism: He sacrifices sleep and comfort by working from 1am to 3am on a borrowed bicycle instead of resting.
rationales.achievement: Taking both late-night runs after already being unable to sleep shows extra effort to keep earning under pressure.
rationales.security: He pushes himself to bring in "barely enough to cover tomorrow's gas," making the goal immediate financial survival.

rationales.hedonism: He says the parrilla is "the one thing that keeps me sane" and values the hours when he is "just Roberto having a good time with his friends."
rationales.security: He knows he "wants to skip it this week, save the money" but leans toward spending anyway despite exhaustion and recent financial strain.

rationales.hedonism: He celebrates by buying Negrita "the fancy wet food she likes," treating himself through a small pleasure after good news.
rationales.security: The painting job comes with a "good rate" and half paid upfront, which clearly improves his financial footing.

rationales.self_direction: He insists he likes "picking my own hours" and riding around with "nobody telling me where to be," despite his sister's criticism.
rationales.security: In the response he sees himself as "treading water" after leaving "a stable situation," and the neglected apartment makes that lack of stability feel real.

rationales.hedonism: He keeps choosing the parrilla because it is "the part of my life that doesn't feel like survival," even with anxiety underneath it.
rationales.security: He says he "should be saving what I'm spending here" and worries the good moment "might not last," showing pleasure is coming at the expense of stability.

rationales.hedonism: He chooses "the good Malbec" and looks forward to "eat well with people I like," explicitly making room for enjoyment.
rationales.security: Fixing the chair, patching the wall, and noting he still has "the painting money" in his pocket show him restoring order while keeping a cushion before an uncertain week.

rationales.achievement: "Delivered the payment gateway migration three days early" shows strong competence, and he immediately measures that work against the applause Tomas received.
rationales.conformity: He shrugs that "that's just how things work" and accepts the unglamorous backend role without pushing against the recognition hierarchy.

rationales.self_direction: He "almost said something" despite having run the numbers, then suppresses his own judgment instead of voicing it.
rationales.security: He prefers "ticket comments so there's a paper trail" and says "that way I'm covered," prioritizing protection over confrontation.
rationales.conformity: He stays quiet because speaking up would "make things awkward" and he does not want to "make it a thing," showing restraint to preserve workplace harmony.

rationales.achievement: He catalogs "seventeen production releases," "zero rollbacks," two promoted juniors, and a 40 percent CI improvement, focusing on measurable competence.
rationales.conformity: Even though the self-assessment feels like "wearing a costume," he decides to "use the strong verbs" and quantify everything to match the system's expectations.

rationales.achievement: He reframes his work as "six months of reverse-engineering" and "shaping how two engineers think," clearly recognizing substantial accomplishment.
rationales.conformity: He accepts that "the rules here are different" and writes "strong verbs and all" so his work fits the professional language Fernando expects.

rationales.achievement: Fernando says he is putting him forward for the director role and that his self-assessment was "the strongest you've written," signaling recognized advancement.
rationales.benevolence: He does not correct Camila because "she deserved to just be happy for me," then takes the girls out for helado to celebrate with them.

rationales.achievement: He submits a "director application packet" with peer endorsements and a reorg proposal, actively pursuing advancement.
rationales.conformity: He frames his critiques as "building on existing strengths" because he knows "bold doesn't play well with everyone," tailoring himself to the panel.

rationales.achievement: He treats the panel seriously, preparing for a "four people, ninety minutes" interview tied to the director process.
rationales.conformity: He "didn't improvise, didn't overshoot" and says he defaults to "listening first," showing careful self-restraint to fit the panel's expectations.

rationales.power: She tells Andres "one more time and I'm finding someone else" and emphasizes that "the gallery is mine and the standard is mine to set," asserting authority.
rationales.security: She links his lateness to a waiting collector and says "I can't have my space looking disorganized when there's money on the table," protecting business stability.

rationales.achievement: Finishing the triptych and feeling it "matches what I saw in my head" shows successful execution of demanding work.
rationales.security: She notes the rent was paid "on time" and that she has "the next three months covered since January," emphasizing financial stability.

rationales.power: The investor "kept talking over me" and she "smiled and nodded through most of it instead of pushing back," yielding control in a situation she wanted to lead.
rationales.conformity: Rather than interrupt or challenge him, she "smiled and nodded," restraining herself to keep the meeting smooth.

rationales.self_direction: She fought to leave the shared lease because "nothing felt fully mine," and now values that "every corner of that space reflects a decision I made."
rationales.power: She watches who "commands a space" and takes satisfaction that collectors can feel her authority in a room arranged entirely on her terms.

rationales.security: She spends a "day off" checking invoices and says she likes knowing "exactly where the money sits," prioritizing financial predictability.
rationales.tradition: She frames that money habit as something inherited from her father, who kept a paper ledger, calling it "different scale, same instinct."

rationales.power: She says curating means "deciding whose career gets a platform and whose doesn't" and that being "on the other side" sits well with her.
rationales.security: She emphasizes that the "reserve fund is untouched" and "insurance renewal is handled," noting that when finances are solid she can actually think about the art.

rationales.power: She immediately rejects the unoffered "co-director" title and says "the boundary is drawn," defending authority over the expansion.

rationales.achievement: With the show opening Friday, she notes all artists "delivered on time" and the room is fully staged, reflecting successful execution.
Selling "four pieces in the first two hours," including work from the artist she backed, marks clear professional success.
rationales.power: A collector saying he had "been hearing my name" and people starting to know "the name Villalobos" shows growing status in the art scene.
rationales.security: She wants her mother to know the gallery is not "some reckless bet anymore" and stresses that "the reserve is intact," emphasizing stability.

rationales.self_direction: She says she "left a stable partnership to build this alone," explicitly choosing her own path despite the risk.
rationales.power: "My gallery, my choices, my track record" asserts ownership and control over the space and its direction.
rationales.security: She defends being "commercially safe" by pointing to "keeping my lights on and my artists paid," prioritizing sustainable stability.

rationales.power: She says "I won't give that up" about artist selection, treating control over curation as non-negotiable.
rationales.security: She weighs that she "can't fund the expansion alone" and that "the bank terms are worse," showing strong concern with financial viability.

rationales.hedonism: He ends the day admitting he hadn't eaten since 'a cheese sandwich at eleven' and that even his plants 'aren't getting my best,' showing his own comfort is being pushed aside.
rationales.benevolence: He 'picked up Lotte's kids from school,' bought them stroopwafels, made Lotte tea, and started the washing before she could ask.
He gives up 'the morning to myself' to cycle over, fix the leaking tap, and stay with Lotte while she cries at the kitchen table.
