rationales.conformity: He says 'I can't say that because it would confirm exactly what she's afraid of,' explicitly holding back his real feeling to keep the peace.
rationales.benevolence: He goes back inside, plays a board game with the kids, and chooses to 'carry it' rather than add to Lotte's burden.
He feels guilty that the older customer 'looked lonely,' and he commits to Jeroen's birthday because 'Jeroen always shows up for me' and he wants to return that care.

rationales.self_direction: He wonders whether this is 'something I'm choosing or something that's choosing me' and says 'putting it down doesn't feel like an option,' showing a loss of autonomy.
rationales.benevolence: Even under that weight, he frames stepping back as leaving 'a hole' because 'Lotte has nobody else who shows up consistently.'

rationales.hedonism: He 'drank too many Jupilers, talked rubbish, laughed until my stomach hurt' and cycled home 'feeling lighter,' centering immediate enjoyment.
rationales.benevolence: He makes time to attend Jeroen's birthday, and Jeroen's 'glad I came' shows he is showing up for a close friend.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects his mother's advice to 'focus on your own life' and insists 'helping Lotte is separate from my life. It IS my life,' claiming that choice as his own.
rationales.hedonism: He says he'd 'rather be tired' and describes waking with the 'tank ... always half-empty,' showing he is accepting ongoing strain instead of protecting his own ease.
rationales.benevolence: He centers his sister and her children throughout, saying 'She's my sister' and that he will not 'step away when things get hard.'

rationales.conformity: Although he 'wanted to say something,' he instead goes to his 'air-conditioned desk' and resumes work, restraining himself rather than disrupting the situation.
rationales.universalism: Seeing 'a child no older than ten carrying bricks' triggers clear concern about exploitation and the gap between his job and what he believes matters.

rationales.tradition: He turns to his mother for perspective, and her reminder that he also 'carried water from the well before school' reconnects him to family history and upbringing.
rationales.universalism: He reflects on the child's situation with shame, saying he had 'the privilege to forget' what survival looks like, which shows widened moral concern beyond himself.

rationales.self_direction: He chooses his own path outside work by sending money to the village school and enrolling in a course on 'accessible software for low-resource settings.'
rationales.achievement: He takes concrete, effortful steps by both making the transfer and signing up for new training that starts 'next week.'
rationales.universalism: Both actions are aimed at people beyond his immediate circle: supporting a village school and learning to build for 'low-resource settings.'

rationales.self_direction: After Raghav pushes market-value logic, he decides 'there should be more' and wants his expertise to 'serve people who actually need it,' defining work on his own terms.
rationales.conformity: He pushes against the dominant script that skills should 'increase my market value,' rather than simply accepting the workplace norm.
rationales.universalism: He keeps orienting his skills toward 'people who actually need it,' showing concern that reaches beyond corporate profit or his own advancement.

rationales.achievement: He says his identity is 'tied to being a working professional' and relies on 'pull requests merged, systems delivered, performance reviews' to prove his worth.
rationales.security: He openly names 'fear' about leaving the corporate world and leans on the safety of a setting where his value is already measurable and secure.

rationales.conformity: He spends four hours 'nodding along, contributing where required' even though he thinks the work is wasteful, which is active compliance with the group norm.
rationales.universalism: He keeps translating the feature's cost into 'village school teachers' salaries' and calls the spending 'stolen opportunity' for people with greater need.

rationales.self_direction: Priya's question pushes him to reconsider his own path, and he starts asking how to 'un-bury' the purpose he chose when he became an engineer.
rationales.universalism: He reconnects with wanting to 'build things that would make life better' and with the belief that 'individual choices ripple outward.'

rationales.self_direction: He actively redesigns his life by asking HR for a four-day week and dedicating the fifth day to work he chose for himself.
rationales.security: He accepts that 'the money difference isn't trivial' and moves ahead anyway, giving up some financial certainty.
rationales.universalism: The extra day is explicitly for 'teaching coding at a community center ... serving domestic workers and their children,' extending his effort to a broader vulnerable group.

rationales.self_direction: He abandons the original lesson plan and decides patience means 'showing up again next week' and 'starting where they are,' adapting his approach rather than clinging to it.
rationales.universalism: He keeps investing in learners who were left out before, spending the session helping Kamala and others with basics instead of chasing his own preferred agenda.

rationales.hedonism: Kamala's smile and this 'one victory' give him immediate joy and relief after feeling paralyzed by bigger problems.
rationales.universalism: He treats 'one person learning to type her own name for the first time at forty-five' as meaningful progress, keeping his attention on someone beyond his own circle.

rationales.self_direction: He says he "should have said 'I wasn't finished'" but stayed quiet because he feared looking "petty or desperate," letting others' reactions override his own voice.
rationales.power: He felt "physically pushed to the side of the room" while Henrik got credit for a "half-baked version" of his work, showing a loss of influence and standing.
rationales.conformity: He held back because pushing back might make him look "petty or desperate," restraining himself to avoid social disapproval.

rationales.achievement: He felt "alive" showing parents "exactly where their kid improved" with the rubric breakdowns he prepared, taking pride in demonstrated competence.
rationales.power: He links trust to influence, saying "when they trust you, you can actually change things" and emphasizing that he put himself "in a position to" make that difference.

rationales.self_direction: He resents having to "sit here and wait" on the committee and says he wants "direct access to the budget approval process," pushing against gatekeeping.
rationales.power: He is frustrated that "someone else" gets to decide whether his work is "worth looking at," showing a strong need to control the approval process himself.

rationales.achievement: Being chosen over "two colleagues with over a decade more experience" confirms to him that "being meticulous matters," and he starts polishing the panel notes weeks early.
rationales.power: He wants the district coordinators to "know my name" because that is how people go from being overlooked to someone others "actually listen to."

rationales.self_direction: He says "I caved because I could feel the room turning," showing that he dropped his own plan once group pressure built.
rationales.power: Even though he had "a clear plan," he let Karin and the rest of the room overrule him, surrendering control over the decision.
rationales.conformity: He changed course because he could "feel the room turning," prioritizing fitting the room over holding his ground.

rationales.self_direction: He "pulled her aside after lunch and explained how my classroom runs," asserting his own methods instead of letting the new assistant redefine them.
rationales.power: He stresses that he built "the assessment rubrics, the differentiation frameworks, the whole progression structure" and wants to "correct that impression before it spreads," protecting his authority.

rationales.achievement: He spent the weekend "refining" the presentation, got told it was "exactly the level of detail the panel looks for," and notes that nobody else "bothered to put the work in."
rationales.power: Being put forward for a cross-school working group and seeing his figures approved unchanged both reinforce his sense that his work carries weight beyond his classroom.
He says "I set the rhythm of these meetings" and cut Camila off to keep the room on his agenda, centering control over the department.
He writes that "someone holds the line. That someone is me" and expects Camila to "learn how things work here," framing the department around his authority.
rationales.benevolence: He notices Camila "looked a little deflated" but treats that as secondary to enforcing his role, showing little care for her feelings or adjustment.

rationales.power: Ríos used "my enrollment data" to support his own pitch while Ricardo sat there "like I am attending someone else's meeting," showing a loss of control over his work.
rationales.conformity: He says pushing back "would have looked petty," so he "smiled" and "nodded" instead of challenging the room.

rationales.self_direction: He says "You do not wait for permission, you present the solution" after independently rewriting the exam schedule, showing strong preference for acting on his own judgment.
rationales.power: Sending corrections "marked in red" and cc'ing others turns his fix into an authoritative intervention rather than a private suggestion.

rationales.hedonism: He ate lunch "at my desk grading quizzes" and chose more work over a social break, subordinating comfort and enjoyment to productivity.
rationales.achievement: He says "I get more done alone" and uses lunch to keep grading, prioritizing output over downtime.

rationales.hedonism: Even after Patricia says he should "relax more at home," he insists "when I relax, things slip," rejecting ease in favor of constant vigilance.
rationales.achievement: He explicitly values the pattern "you do good work, someone notices" and takes satisfaction in the vice-rector recognizing his correction.
rationales.power: He lives by "the man who controls the calendar controls the operation" and says he keeps "the reins," making control central to how he works.
rationales.security: He reviews calendars and schedules so things do not "fall apart," focusing on predictability and preventing disruption.

rationales.power: He defines himself as "a man who takes charge of things" at school, at the junta, and "at home," making control central to his identity.
rationales.benevolence: Even after noting Patricia's criticism, he doubles down on needing to lead everywhere, leaving little room for give-and-take with a close other.

rationales.power: He reads the silence as a lack of "respect" for department heads and resents waiting for someone else to judge whether his proposal has merit, showing strong concern with authority and standing.

rationales.self_direction: He wanted to push back but said "sounds good" because resistance would make him look difficult, suppressing his own judgment.
rationales.power: He sees "my proposal" becoming a committee project where others get to put "their fingerprints" on it, meaning he is losing ownership and control.
rationales.conformity: He went along because "you cannot argue against that without looking difficult," choosing social acceptability over open disagreement.

rationales.self_direction: He "could have pushed" but instead "just nodded," showing him subordinating his own judgment to the room.
rationales.conformity: He stayed quiet because he feared being "the guy who derails standup" and wanted to avoid standing out.
rationales.universalism: He knew the system was hurting migrant workers and that he had "the stories" and "the data" to intervene, but chose silence while the harm continued.
He "helped him draft a letter to MOM" after an employer hid a serious injury, directly supporting someone facing institutional harm.
He saw an article about "plastic waste in the Java Sea," then "closed it" and kept eating from a styrofoam container, choosing convenience over environmental concern.

rationales.self_direction: He "didn't say anything" even though he knew exactly what the hiring comment meant, deferring to the group instead of speaking from his own convictions.
rationales.conformity: At a team dinner he kept quiet because he "didn't want to make it awkward" and feared being "seen differently" by coworkers.
rationales.universalism: He recognized the joke targeted people "from certain countries" and connected it to prejudice migrant workers face, but still let the remark pass.

rationales.benevolence: He frames dinner as the three of them cooking together and notices Xiao Yu taking vegetable washing seriously, centering warmth and care inside the family.
rationales.universalism: They spent time picking up beach plastic and he reflects that "the habits matter" and "the noticing matters," tying daily choices to care for the wider environment.
He used his Saturday at the worker center to help "two new guys file their claims," directly supporting people outside his immediate circle.

rationales.self_direction: He knew the validation layer mattered and that he "could have pushed back," but still said "okay" and abandoned his own judgment.
rationales.conformity: He went along with the PM's request to "cut corners" because of the deadline and the easier path of not resisting.
rationales.universalism: He knew "real people's money is on the line" and still chose what was "easy for me," leaving vulnerable remittance users exposed.

rationales.conformity: He raised the failures in a 1:1 but then "nodded. Again." when told the sprint was full, yielding to the team's plan instead of pressing further.
rationales.universalism: He sees that "people can't send money to their families" yet keeps waiting for a "right moment," allowing known harm to continue.
He admits he has been "choosing convenience over doing what I know matters" while support tickets keep affecting families, acknowledging an ongoing failure to act on broader human consequences.

rationales.security: He immediately agreed to cover his mom's "prescription copay" despite money stress, prioritizing protection against a concrete family need.
rationales.benevolence: He said "of course" right away when his mother asked for help, putting his parents' needs ahead of his own financial comfort.

rationales.hedonism: He has gone "probably six months" without a real break and says even a day off would be spoiled by worrying about lost income, sidelining rest and enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: He is working "twelve-hour" days through the holiday surge, pushing hard to maximize earnings despite physical strain.
rationales.security: He explicitly frames the surge as "good for earnings" and cannot imagine a day off without calculating lost money, showing strong priority on financial stability.
rationales.benevolence: He worked the long shifts and still made time to "drop off the pharmacy money to Mom," then told her to keep it in case more was needed.

rationales.hedonism: He expects to spend Christmas "work until the last possible minute" and arrive "stressed and half-present," sacrificing enjoyment and ease for more driving.
rationales.security: He keeps focusing on the "best earning days" and the fact that one day of driving could cover "half my car insurance payment," putting financial protection first.
rationales.tradition: The pull of "Christmas dinner at her place this year" and the sense that "who knows how many of these we get" show the weight of an established family holiday ritual.
rationales.benevolence: He is torn because he wants to "be there for the whole dinner" with his parents and sister, showing real concern for close family.

rationales.self_direction: She contrasts the lecture hall with "actually doing it, badly, in real life," embracing a self-chosen path over the expected one.
rationales.stimulation: She describes wanting to "touch everything" in the warehouse and finding an "insane indigo block-print," leaning into novelty and sensory excitement.
rationales.benevolence: She "let it ring" when her mom called and says she has run out of ways to sound enthusiastic about family updates, signaling withdrawal from a close relationship.

rationales.hedonism: She says the cocktails, music, and rooftop view "felt exactly right" even after spending half the day's revenue, openly prioritizing pleasure.
rationales.achievement: Selling "eleven pieces" and hearing strangers praise something "I made with my hands" gives her a clear sense of success and competence.

rationales.self_direction: She recoils from moving in because she wants "space" and the ability to decide things without "explaining," emphasizing personal autonomy.
rationales.security: She hears living together as "this whole plan" of routines and stability and pulls away from it rather than moving toward a more settled arrangement.

rationales.achievement: She finished "the last sample" with sore fingers and was pleased the jacket looked "exactly how I pictured it," taking satisfaction in executing her standard well.

rationales.self_direction: She tells her mother "I don't have a backup plan because this is the plan," insisting on her own chosen direction despite pressure.
rationales.security: Saying she has "no backup plan" while rejecting the safer conventional path shows a willingness to forgo stability.
rationales.conformity: She is "tired of being the cautionary tale at family gatherings" and refuses to bend to the family's preferred script for her life.

rationales.self_direction: She wants the freedom to "decide at midnight to rework a pattern or go meet Diya for chai without explaining," making autonomy central.
rationales.stimulation: She explicitly dreads a life with "no surprise" about who is next to her and resists routines that would make her days predictable.
rationales.security: Both the shaky consignment math and her aversion to settling into fixed routines show discomfort with building a more stable, protected setup.
With Diya pausing the brand, "it felt like the floor shifted" and she is left with "half a brand," highlighting an exposed and unstable situation rather than a secure one.

rationales.achievement: "The Hamburg hub finally hit ninety-six percent on-time" and he is upset the result was not credited to him, showing strong investment in concrete performance and recognition.
rationales.power: He fixates on Kramer presenting the numbers "like they were his" and on the board's reaction, showing concern with status and whose authority gets recognized.
rationales.conformity: He stayed silent because "picking a fight on camera would only hurt me," restraining himself to fit the political norms of the call.

rationales.hedonism: Lotte opened the saved Brunello, but he "couldn't even enjoy it properly" because he was replaying the slight, letting ambition override present pleasure.
rationales.achievement: He frames six years of making the region "the best-performing in the company" as meaningful only if that competence is noticed.
rationales.power: He says "having your name attached to the outcome matters" because it shapes who gets considered when the next VP seat opens.

rationales.hedonism: His back is "wrecked" and he cancels dinner at the new restaurant, sacrificing comfort and enjoyment to the day's demands.
rationales.achievement: He spends "six hours on the floor sorting parcels" to keep operations moving when two warehouse leads call in sick.

rationales.hedonism: He spent an entire sailing trip "checking emails" and "barely noticed the water," letting work wipe out the leisure he chose.
rationales.power: His line "that's the deal when you run things" shows identification with being the person in charge, even when it costs him.
rationales.conformity: He "kept it measured" with Kramer and "smiled through" the shoulder pat, suppressing his reaction to stay professionally acceptable.

rationales.self_direction: "My budget, my picks, no committee" shows he values making choices without being constrained by others.
rationales.power: He calls the hiring decision "actual authority" and clearly relishes direct control over the Antwerp team.

rationales.hedonism: He calls it "the kind of evening I live for" and dwells on the food, wine, candles, and atmosphere as ends in themselves.
rationales.power: He notes the guests' industry positions and says he knows "exactly what those relationships are worth," treating dinner as influence-building.

rationales.hedonism: He gives up the Bruges hotel, rooftop terrace, and Michelin dinner to spend Saturday "on a laptop in my bathrobe eating cheese toast."
rationales.achievement: He takes over permit calls that are "not technically my job" because he cannot afford delays with the VP shortlist looming.
rationales.benevolence: He leaves Lotte to take the trip with Anneke and later recognizes he chose work over time with "the person I like most in the world."

rationales.hedonism: He deliberately took Friday off, put the "phone in a drawer," sailed, grilled fish, and sat with a cold Riesling just to enjoy the day.
rationales.achievement: He personally rearranged the staging layout because it would "create a bottleneck at dock three" and is already focused on the throughput numbers.
rationales.power: He overrode the site manager's plan and later admits the constant weekend phone checking is "just control."

rationales.hedonism: The promotion would mean "fewer evenings on the terrace" and "fewer dinners" with Lotte, so the path he wants comes with sacrificing pleasure and comfort.
rationales.achievement: Making the shortlist after years of maneuvering toward the role shows a strong drive for recognized success at the top level.
rationales.power: He is drawn to "more status, more scope, more say" and a "seat at the executive table," centering authority and influence.

rationales.self_direction: He values that "nobody tells me when to start, when to stop" and rejects benefits-and-ring as the only legitimate path.
rationales.stimulation: He contrasts waiting for "some dude to get his Thai food" with rappelling into a canyon fire and thinking "this is it," showing his current routine feels deadening.
rationales.achievement: He tracks earnings and treats the Phantom sale as "a client, not just a sale," showing concrete progress toward building something.
rationales.security: He is openly uninterested in "a real job with benefits, a retirement plan, maybe a ring," favoring a riskier setup over stability.

rationales.self_direction: He refuses to take "some cubicle job" just to satisfy Kevin, insisting his own plan counts even if other people do not recognize it.
rationales.achievement: He sets a benchmark of "$2000/month by December" and lays out a path to running the drone business full-time.
rationales.security: He dismisses the 401k-and-benefits model and treats conventional stability as less important than building his own runway.
rationales.conformity: He pushes back on what Kevin and even Rachel think a responsible 38-year-old should want, rejecting the social script being pressed on him.

rationales.self_direction: He leans on Dario's advice to "stop explaining yourself to people who already decided you're wrong," reinforcing his determination to live by his own judgment.
rationales.conformity: Calling Kevin's safe job "practically embalmed" shows open contempt for the conventional standard being held up to him.
rationales.benevolence: He includes the person who "sleeps next to you" among the people who already decided he is wrong, showing relational withdrawal instead of closeness.

rationales.self_direction: He says being pushed toward stability feels like being asked "to become someone else," defending his right to choose a nontraditional life.
rationales.achievement: He insists "I AM building toward something" and keeps framing the drone work as a project he is trying to prove can succeed.
rationales.security: "I'd rather be broke and building something than comfortable and dead inside" explicitly rejects safety and predictability.
rationales.benevolence: He admits he was so focused on proving his way works that he "forgot to check if she wanted to be on the ride," sidelining his partner's needs.

rationales.stimulation: He remembers wildfire work where he felt alive, then says "driving is just time traded for money," showing his current routine clashes with what energizes him.
rationales.achievement: He no longer feels like the work matters and admits the supposed bridge may not lead anywhere, undermining his sense of purpose and accomplishment.

rationales.security: He says he would rather get help now than "watch the whole thing burn down," prioritizing relationship stability over pride.
rationales.benevolence: He accepts that Rachel "deserves to know the stakes" and says he would rather get help now than let the relationship deteriorate.

rationales.stimulation: "I'm wired to move" and his search for the "right kind of motion" show a strong need for challenge and movement rather than stillness.
rationales.achievement: He defines happiness through being good at hard things and cites the first sale because it "proved the concept worked."
rationales.security: Stillness and retirement terrify him; even the image of his father after retiring makes stability feel like a kind of death.
rationales.benevolence: He explicitly wants a version of motion that "includes her" and worries about losing "someone I love."

rationales.power: He fixates on being interrupted "mid-sentence" and on how "if it were Thomas or Sven" nobody would have done that, showing how much deference in the room matters to him.
rationales.conformity: He says meetings should have "a basic level of order" and that "the chair should have handled it," grounding the episode in rule-bound procedure rather than improvisation.

rationales.power: Being sought out at lunch because someone "values your input enough to come find you" matters more to him than a Teams thumbs-up, showing a strong pull toward having his judgment treated as meaningful.
He is upset that he has "no real authority over the things that actually affect our deadlines" and that "someone else holds the pen," making control over outcomes the clear stake.

rationales.achievement: He "spent the weekend on the risk matrix" and comes back to having done "the analysis" thoroughly, then feels stung that none of that work was engaged with.
rationales.power: What hurts is that "Thomas has been here longer, has a louder voice" and the room defaulted to him, showing how much weight and voice in the hierarchy matter here.
He dwells on being left off the "senior management pipeline" and fears the people who decide these things no longer see him as "someone on the upward track," which centers standing and advancement.

rationales.achievement: He is bothered that people praised Clara for "initiative" when "that was mine," showing a strong need for his contribution to be recognized.
rationales.power: He frames the loss as "I set the direction" and "allocated the resource," then feels "invisible in my own team" when that leadership role is not acknowledged.

rationales.benevolence: He spends the one-on-one helping Daan map trade-offs until "he had a clear plan," and says that kind of problem-solving with another person is "the version of my job I actually like."

rationales.self_direction: She says the house seems to demand "this complete surrender of any time that's mine" and then scrubs off the indigo even though it felt "like erasing something," showing her own path getting suppressed.
rationales.conformity: She removes the stains because it was "easier than sitting through another silent dinner" and says she "pick[s] my fights," prioritizing smooth relations over pushing back.

rationales.self_direction: Her first instinct is "my son likes drawing, why is that a problem?" and she resents that "everyone has an opinion ... except the boy himself," clearly siding with his own preferences.
rationales.conformity: In the meeting she "smiled and nodded" and promised to push him toward being "more social," and later says "you just don't" challenge it there, showing deference to the setting's expectations.

rationales.tradition: Making aloo paratha "the way Appa used to" and being carried back to the old house in Mysore shows deliberate connection to inherited family practice.

rationales.self_direction: The indigo work gives her "two hours of silence" with her "hands doing what they wanted to do," and she "didn't even check my phone," signaling protected creative autonomy.
rationales.tradition: She helps set up the Diwali puja and makes rangoli "the way my grandmother taught me," explicitly choosing inherited methods over "acrylic paint shortcuts."
She agrees to teach Vihaan the Sahasranama because "it's important" and says she's glad Appa made sure she knew it "by heart," treating the practice as something worth carrying forward.
She teaches the verses with their meaning and describes it as "this thread that connects us backward through generations," explicitly preserving inherited practice.
rationales.benevolence: She wants Vihaan to feel "the warmth of sitting together with something that matters" and shapes the lesson around patience rather than pressure.

rationales.self_direction: The dye work is fully hers, from a fermentation vat she "built" to deciding whether to take Priya's order, and she is actively protecting time and space to pursue it.
rationales.achievement: She catalogs "fourteen shades of indigo," labels mordants and dip counts, and thinks through whether her setup can handle "twelve table runners, twenty napkins," showing disciplined craft and execution.

rationales.self_direction: She claims those afternoon hours as "mine" and rejects the idea that they are "leftover time that belongs to the house," which is a clear defense of her own lane.
When Ravi calls it "just a small project," she says nothing, clears the plates, and goes upstairs, letting her own aim be diminished in the moment.
rationales.conformity: She swallows her response at dinner and keeps the evening moving by "finish[ing] dinner" and "clear[ing] the plates," choosing social smoothness over confrontation.

rationales.self_direction: She wants "not the permission, not the approval" but "the quiet assumption that of course I do this, it's mine," which is an explicit claim to make this work on her own terms.
She pushes back on the idea that every hour with indigo is "stolen" and notes that "nobody told Ravi he has to choose," showing resistance to externally imposed limits on how she lives.
rationales.tradition: She takes the boys to the "monthly abhishekam," notes Vihaan reciting the Sahasranama, and feels proud when the priest says they are "keeping these things alive."

rationales.self_direction: She accepts the order despite fear and lands on "I know I'm doing my thing," making an explicit choice to back her own path.
rationales.achievement: Saying yes to "twelve runners, twenty napkins" with a specific "kalamkari-inspired border block" shows a concrete commitment to producing and delivering skilled work.

rationales.self_direction: "I just sat there" while Pieter took over his proposal, showing he let someone else dictate the moment instead of asserting his own voice.
rationales.power: Pieter "presented the container routing proposal like it was his" and Marc did not challenge it, leaving his status and control publicly undermined.
rationales.conformity: He chose not to "call it out right there," restraining himself in the meeting rather than risking open conflict.

rationales.achievement: "Ran the quarterly review myself... just my numbers, my narrative" frames the meeting as a performance of his competence that landed visibly in the room.
rationales.power: Saying "the division is finally mine in the way I wanted it to be" shows satisfaction in having authority and command over the group.

rationales.self_direction: He had to keep "refreshing my inbox like some junior waiting for permission," showing him operating under someone else's control instead of his own judgment.
rationales.power: He is fixated on being unable to move "a standard supplier agreement through" despite running the division, highlighting a loss of influence over outcomes that affect his standing.
rationales.conformity: He "thought better of it" and did not go to the CEO, holding himself to the existing chain rather than escalating.

rationales.power: He had "already decided" the reporting structure and, after letting objections run for ten minutes, "moved on," asserting that the decision stayed with him.
rationales.benevolence: When Lotte asked about work, he gave her "fine" and chose not to explain himself, pulling away from a close relationship instead of engaging with her.

rationales.self_direction: Once others started "nodding along with her," he abandoned his own rollout plan and let the room's momentum determine the outcome.
rationales.power: What unsettles him is that "it wasn't my call anymore" and they looked at Sandra "like she was in charge," showing a clear loss of authority.
rationales.conformity: He "ended up agreeing to her phased approach" as the room shifted, yielding to group consensus rather than pressing his own line.

rationales.self_direction: He says he "sat there and let him reframe my own analysis back to me," showing he did not assert his own judgment or boundaries in the moment.
rationales.power: His own summary, "The room's assumption that I'm the one who decides," makes clear he feels his authority slipping away.
rationales.conformity: Instead of shutting Jeroen down, he "smiled and nodded like it was a collaborative discussion," choosing social smoothness over confrontation.

rationales.power: He "corrected him this time" when Pieter redirected the client conversation, reasserting control over his own account in the moment.
rationales.conformity: The correction "came out sharper than I meant and the client noticed," showing he broke the usual norm of keeping the exchange smooth and unobtrusive.

rationales.power: He says he feels most like himself when people "come to me for direction and I give it clearly," which centers his comfort in being the one with authority.
rationales.benevolence: He patiently "walked through it with her," shared shortcuts, and reassured Lisa that he also struggled at first, directly supporting someone on his team.

rationales.power: Having to "walk him through" numbers he had already submitted made him feel like he had to "audition for the right to present my own results," signaling diminished standing and control.
rationales.conformity: He "complied, obviously" and went through everything line by line, choosing deference to hierarchy over pushing back.
rationales.benevolence: When Lotte asked if he was okay, he said "yes" even though "I wasn't really," shutting down honesty with a close partner.
He "booked a table at De Kas for Lotte's birthday next week," making a concrete plan for someone close to him.

rationales.security: He prefers a "three-year contract extension" that puts "a floor under" the next three years over squeezing for more upside, prioritizing predictability over expansion.
He wanted someone outside the company to know "the ground is solid" and takes comfort in the neighborhood's familiar rhythm, showing how much steadiness matters to him.
rationales.tradition: He lingers on the old desk lamp, paper registrations with "actual stamps," and his father's habit of reporting good news to family first, grounding himself in inherited ways and continuity.

rationales.security: He keeps the reserve fund in place, checks receivables "every Monday morning," and compares reinvestment to pulling bricks from the foundation, all of which show a strong preference for safety buffers.
He postpones the new product line until the Formosa contract "settles in" because he has "seen it take companies apart" when revenue is not locked down.

The possibility of Jia-hao leaving pushes him to reopen the proposal, but his immediate move is still "I would read the proposal again this weekend," a cautious step meant to contain disruption rather than leap into change.
He reads the proposal three times, flags certification delays, and insists "I agreed to look, not to leap," showing sustained caution around uncertainty.
rationales.benevolence: He leaves Jia-hao a note saying "Let's talk Monday" because he cannot bear "watching him leave," showing active effort to support the relationship and make room for him.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects returning to "counting someone else's hours" and says office life "nearly killed something in me," showing a deliberate preference for living on his own terms.
rationales.hedonism: He values "my mornings at Abu Khalil's cafe" and says saving for later would leave him with "nothing to show for actually living," prioritizing present enjoyment.
rationales.security: He dismisses building a retirement fund and says the uncertainty feels better than sacrificing his current life, which works against long-term stability.

rationales.hedonism: He spent the "extra money" on "a proper dinner" and savored three relaxed hours with friends, treating pleasure and atmosphere as worth more than saving the cash.

rationales.achievement: The week felt worthwhile because someone kept asking questions and he could say "I knew every answer," showing satisfaction in competence being recognized by others.

rationales.hedonism: He skipped surge pricing because "my back says no" and felt immediate "relief," choosing bodily comfort over more income.
rationales.security: Even with financial pressure implied by "Ask me again when rent is due," he passed up high-paying work, which undercuts stability.

rationales.hedonism: He chose "coffee instead of driving" and let an hour of watching the street stand as enough for the day, clearly favoring ease and simple pleasure over productivity.
Even while worrying about the future, he still lingers over "the expensive coffee" and a shared knafeh, and he frames the pull of "the nice afternoon" as central to his choices.

rationales.self_direction: He says gig work was "a door I walked through on purpose" and contrasts it with office life he knowingly left, making autonomy the center of the choice.
rationales.hedonism: The businessman "looked miserable" while he noticed "the citadel lit up at night," and he links his decision to a life that feels less joyless and more alive.

rationales.self_direction: He says "I cannot go back to an office" because "something in me genuinely cannot," showing a strong refusal to return to externally structured work.
rationales.security: The rent increase sends him straight into "doing the math" and thinking about how "one bad week matters," putting stability and survival at the center of the entry.

rationales.self_direction: He treats office work as "a wall I have built" to protect something essential and insists he could not give those daily freedoms up and still "recognize myself."
rationales.hedonism: He names coffee, sunset views, and dinners that "go three hours" as the "thread" of his good days, showing clear commitment to lived pleasure.
rationales.security: He immediately considers a place that is "smaller... but cheaper" and says he can "drive more shifts," taking practical steps to preserve stability.

rationales.self_direction: She calls it "my space" and defends a kitchen system she built "on purpose," asserting control over how her home runs.
rationales.conformity: Instead of quietly absorbing the interference, she told her mother-in-law to ask before moving things, rejecting the expectation to keep the peace.

rationales.self_direction: She says the commission became "someone else's vision" and ends the day with work that feels "not mine," showing she acted against her need to create on her own terms.
rationales.hedonism: She feels "hollow" after spending the day on work she did not want, sacrificing present satisfaction for a paid job.
rationales.achievement: She finished the piece, the client "loved it," and was "paid extra," which is a clear sign of competent performance being rewarded.
rationales.security: She admits that "income is income" with the shop slow and chose the commission "for money," prioritizing practical financial needs.

rationales.self_direction: She keeps returning to "that's my choice to make" and rejects having her time and daughter treated as a problem someone else gets to solve.
rationales.conformity: Her objection is not daycare itself but refusing a ready-made "solution" and pushing back on the expectation that she should accept the schedule others propose.

rationales.self_direction: She says "Neither model fits me" and "I'm doing it my way," then describes a daily rhythm that is "flexible but intentional" on her own terms.
rationales.conformity: She is tired of feeling she must justify herself and refuses to live by either family's "blueprints," resisting social expectations around motherhood and work.
rationales.tradition: She explicitly rejects both her parents' model and Erik's family template, refusing inherited family scripts as guides for her life.

rationales.self_direction: She emphasizes these were prints "I made because I wanted to make them," highlighting creation on her own terms.
rationales.achievement: Selling "three prints" proves to her that what she creates "has value to someone else too," making competence and recognition salient.

rationales.self_direction: Her response insists that "my brain doesn't shut off" just because she is watching a toddler, defending the need to keep her inner creative life active.
rationales.security: She missed Astrid climbing onto the counter because she was "sketching in my head," and that lapse directly undermined a safe, controlled environment.

rationales.self_direction: She leans toward saying no because the thought of "someone else's schedule" and office routines feels wrong, favoring independence over fitting back in.
rationales.achievement: She admits part of her wants to "prove that I'm still a real designer," yet she leans away from the job, turning down a chance for competence to be recognized.

rationales.self_direction: She says the finished piece matters because she can see "my own choices reflected back," and contrasts that with agency work that "wasn't mine."
rationales.power: She says meaningful work is "not even external recognition" and rejects art filtered through "client needs" and "stakeholder feedback," turning away from status and influence as motives.

rationales.self_direction: She says the class was "my idea, on my timeline" and also refuses office bulk orders, keeping both parenting and work on her own terms.
rationales.achievement: She "set the price higher than usual because it took more out of me and I want that reflected," showing a desire to have effort and quality properly recognized.
rationales.benevolence: She signs Astrid up so her daughter can "smash paint around with other kids," making room for something good and enjoyable for someone close to her.

rationales.self_direction: She says the work came "from my experience" and celebrates "making something alone," centering authentic self-expression and independent creation.
rationales.achievement: The piece sold, and the buyer's response showed she "managed to put something true into the world" that another person recognized, giving clear validation of her work.
rationales.universalism: What matters most is that something personal could "connect to someone you've never met," reflecting concern with human connection beyond her immediate circle.

rationales.self_direction: "Anil says we should lock in the rate... I know he is right" shows her deferring to someone else's call instead of choosing the trip she wanted.
rationales.stimulation: She let the planned "weekend trip to Alibaug" keep getting pushed and spent the evening comparing fixed deposits, choosing routine and caution over novelty.
rationales.hedonism: She gave up the trip "we had the dates picked" for school fees and other obligations, putting enjoyment on hold again.
rationales.security: Comparing fixed deposits on "three different bank sites" and wanting to "lock in the rate" centers financial protection and predictability.
rationales.conformity: She accepts the family line that postponing is the responsible thing and goes along with waiting "till Diwali" instead of pushing back.
rationales.benevolence: The trip was postponed for "school fees" and because "Anil's mother needed new glasses," prioritizing family needs over her own leisure.

rationales.self_direction: "I do not need her opinion on how we spend our time or money" is a direct assertion of her own authority over those choices.
rationales.hedonism: When Meera asked about "a proper holiday," she answered defensively about time and money rather than treating leisure as something worth protecting.
rationales.conformity: She rejects her sister's judgment outright and resents the family pressure about what should "count," rather than smoothing it over.

rationales.hedonism: After a day of appointments and chores, even "two episodes of my show" and leftover gulab jamun "just felt flat," leaving little real enjoyment.
rationales.security: Dental checkups, relief at "no cavities," and arranging tuition all focus on preventing problems and keeping the children on track.
rationales.conformity: She moves through expected household duties, from making dal and rice to keeping Anil's food covered, without any sign of resisting those roles.
rationales.benevolence: She spends the day on the kids' checkups, Aarav's homework, and finding extra help for him, clearly centering close family care.

rationales.self_direction: "Small things that do not require a committee meeting" shows a push to make her own choices instead of waiting for family approval.
rationales.stimulation: Booking "that resort in Goa" and choosing less constrained options like just taking an auto reflect a pull toward novelty and spontaneity.
rationales.hedonism: She explicitly wants to "use the money to actually live a little" through pleasures like the resort, good cheese, and extra comfort.
rationales.security: She pushes back on more gold and says "we have insurance, we have the FD," rejecting additional safeguards in favor of spending now.
rationales.conformity: Her real stance is resistance: she only says "we would think about it" at lunch, then later openly challenges Anil and her mother-in-law's expectation.
rationales.tradition: Calling the jeweller savings plan something she does not want, despite her elder framing it as what "every family needs," rejects an established family practice.

rationales.self_direction: "My days dissolve into errands and appointments... and I cannot hold onto a single thing for myself" shows how little room she has for her own direction.
rationales.hedonism: Even when the party will "be fine," she says "there is nothing in it for me," describing pleasure as absent rather than pursued.
rationales.achievement: She measures herself against the past: "I used to coordinate events for two hundred guests... now I cannot order a cake on time," framing the moment as failed competence.
rationales.power: The day feels like something happening to her rather than something she controls, from being "pulled away" for two weeks to collapsing over a decision already made.
rationales.conformity: She still secures the expensive castle cake and keeps the birthday plan intact, meeting the expected standard for the party despite her distress.
rationales.benevolence: She says yes to the costly replacement cake "without even checking with Anil" because she "could not bear the thought" of disappointing her daughter.

rationales.self_direction: "I need more of that... moments that belong to me" marks a deliberate claim on time that is hers rather than the family's.
rationales.hedonism: The hidden "glass of wine" and "forty-five minutes of nothing" were "the best part of my week," clearly valuing simple pleasure and comfort.
rationales.benevolence: Her strongest feeling earlier in the evening is when Rhea grabs her face and says "best mummy," showing deep emotional investment in her daughter.

rationales.self_direction: "I agreed because what else would I say" shows her suppressing what she actually wanted to say and following someone else's decision.
rationales.security: With Anil's restructuring news, she immediately agrees to "hold off on any big expenses for a few months, just in case."
rationales.conformity: She falls in line with Anil's cautious plan and avoids challenging it, even though the nudge implies she had other thoughts.
rationales.benevolence: She spends three days "washing sheets, making khichdi, and checking his temperature" while Aarav is sick, centering close care.

rationales.self_direction: She thinks about writing the blog post but "the moment passed" once family needs intervened, leaving no protected space for her own project.
rationales.achievement: The blog "used to feel like proof I still had a brain" but now feels like "another thing I am failing at," explicitly framing it as underperformance.
rationales.benevolence: She spends the day taking the kids to the park, helping Rhea, and celebrating their milestones, with her own work pushed aside.

rationales.self_direction: She "actually finished" the paneer tikka post and published it at 11 PM, reclaiming a personal project in her own name.
rationales.hedonism: Making butter chicken from her grandmother's recipe and enjoying that "both kids asked for seconds" shows concrete investment in pleasure and comfort.
rationales.achievement: Publishing the post and saying it "felt good to hit that button" reflects satisfaction in completing and sharing competent work.
rationales.security: She renewed health insurance, raised coverage to "ten lakhs," and renewed accident cover before football classes, prioritizing protection.
rationales.tradition: Using "my grandmother's recipe" keeps a family practice alive through the meal.
rationales.benevolence: She increases the family's coverage and cooks a dinner everyone enjoys, putting close relatives' wellbeing first.

rationales.achievement: He spent "two weekends" gathering quotes and walked away after a "seven to two" vote, showing effective and recognized competence.
rationales.power: He says "you control the information, you set the direction" and ends the meeting feeling like he knows "how to move a room."

rationales.achievement: He redesigned the KPI template so it "made the data speak for itself," and the GM publicly called it "clean work."
rationales.power: What mattered to him was that the GM "still sees the difference" between him and Daniel's team, highlighting rank and standing.

rationales.self_direction: He says he "just let it happen" and sat there smiling instead of asserting authorship of his own idea.
rationales.power: Daniel presents "my regional consolidation idea" without credit, and Kenneth chooses silence while the status payoff goes elsewhere.
rationales.conformity: He says speaking up would have looked "petty," so he follows the room and protects appearances instead of disrupting it.

rationales.achievement: His team is "burning hours on manual reconciliation" and the migration is behind, so the outcome tied to his name is failure rather than progress.
rationales.power: He has "done everything I can do" yet still has to wait for IT to "circle back next week," leaving him without control over the timeline.

rationales.self_direction: He says "I could have pushed harder" but instead settles on "fine. I will support the pilot," subordinating his own judgment.
rationales.power: A system he has run for "eight years" is opened up to others' preferences, and the decision shifts to the GM and HR while he anticipates cleaning it up.
rationales.security: His argument rests on "predictable" overtime, known rotations, and the belief that "people need structure," all centered on stability.
rationales.conformity: He shakes Priscilla's hand and tells Michelle, "it is what the GM wants, so we execute," explicitly aligning with the formal chain.

rationales.self_direction: He disagrees internally throughout the workshop but keeps giving polished answers and says he is "too tired to fight these battles anymore."
rationales.power: A senior manager lets a younger facilitator define his experience as "vulnerability" and chooses not to contest the framing.
rationales.conformity: He keeps nodding because it is "not worth making a scene" and says "You pick your battles," prioritizing smooth compliance.

rationales.achievement: He watches Daniel get applause for "my idea" and says the role is starting to feel "like a frame around an empty picture," signaling lost accomplishment.
rationales.power: Others now present his ideas and get called "innovative," while he sits "clapping politely," stripped of influence and standing.
rationales.benevolence: He dismisses his wife's concern by thinking "What does she know" and belittling her business instead of receiving support from someone close.

rationales.self_direction: Even when classmates laugh, she "didn't say anything" and keeps replaying what she should have said, showing inhibited self-expression.
rationales.achievement: Professor Mansour asks "Where is the artist in this?" and the class laughs, turning months of careful work into a public blow to her competence.
rationales.conformity: She "sat there and took it" rather than confronting the room or breaking the critique script.
rationales.tradition: Her defense of the work is rooted in "my jiddo's hand over mine," treating the script as inherited practice rather than mere decoration.

rationales.self_direction: She wonders if she is holding on because letting go would feel impossible, suggesting the path is constrained by loyalty rather than freely chosen.
rationales.tradition: Sorting and preserving jiddo's practice sheets, and fearing that stopping would mean "forgetting him," ties her work directly to inherited continuity.
rationales.benevolence: She spends the morning helping Teta Hana with the papers and treasures the sheet her grandmother "put aside for me," emphasizing close-family care.

rationales.self_direction: She admits "I could do it" but the family image in her head keeps the option feeling unavailable, limiting her ability to choose her own direction.
rationales.conformity: She says the idea makes her stomach tight because Teta would not be able to read it, showing restraint to avoid upsetting family expectations.
rationales.tradition: What troubles her is taking the script apart in a way that would disconnect it from jiddo and make it unreadable to Teta, so she protects the inherited form.

rationales.self_direction: "I'm not afraid... I just don't think ripping something apart is the only way to prove you understand it" asserts her own artistic standard against others' framing.
She says she wants to choose "freely" but concludes "So I won't do it," giving up the path that part of her wants.
rationales.achievement: She rejects the route that could help "for the sake of a grade and a gallery slot," explicitly stepping away from external advancement.
rationales.conformity: She will not tell the family about fragmenting the script because she imagines "a room full of people uncomfortable" and acts to avoid that rupture.
rationales.tradition: She loves the family story about jiddo and refuses the collage direction partly because it would break with the calligraphic practice the family honors.
rationales.benevolence: Her choice is shaped by concern that her grandmother would go quiet "when she's hurt" and her mother would be distressed, prioritizing close relatives' feelings.

rationales.self_direction: She makes a second version where the letters "drift apart" and then keeps both sheets as "mine," experimenting on her own terms.
rationales.security: She hides the sheets because showing them might push her "somewhere I didn't agree to" or draw Teta's criticism, preserving safety and control.
rationales.tradition: The piece begins with writing "the bismillah in naskh the way jiddo taught me," and even the looser version stays legible and tied to his hand.

rationales.self_direction: "When I stopped chasing the next promotion, the next credential" shows she deliberately chose the life that fits her rather than the conventional advancement track.
rationales.hedonism: "The weight of the teacup" and "the specific shade of orange" are treated as "the whole point," making present-moment enjoyment the center of the entry.
rationales.achievement: "These aren't consolation prizes for not advancing" directly rejects promotion and credentials as the thing that matters.

rationales.hedonism: "No more lingering over coffee" and "Thursday evenings at the souk" are described as "the small things that make it bearable," showing how strongly she prioritizes everyday enjoyment and peace.
rationales.achievement: She dismisses Tariq's talk of "opportunities for growth" and says "martyrdom doesn't taste good," rejecting careerist striving when it costs her peace.
rationales.conformity: She says pushing back would get her labeled "difficult, uncooperative" and decides she will probably "go to the meetings," showing restraint to avoid disapproval.

rationales.hedonism: "Good olive oil makes a difference," the warm light, and the bakery's knafeh are the details she chooses to dwell on, turning a tedious day toward sensory enjoyment.

rationales.self_direction: "I've planned nothing. That's the plan" and "not knowing feels like freedom" frame unscheduled time as freedom on her own terms.
rationales.hedonism: The glowing street, warm sesame cookie, long baths, and "real rest" are all treated as the point of the break.

rationales.self_direction: "Even as a child I remember preferring slow afternoons" and "That person doesn't exist" show a stable commitment to her own way of living rather than the one others reward.
rationales.hedonism: "Even the coffee felt rushed. I was drinking it to finish it, not to enjoy it" shows the semester crowding out the pleasures she usually protects.
Bringing "the loose-leaf oolong" to school and saying those five minutes "change the texture of the entire day" shows deliberate investment in small pleasures during work.
rationales.benevolence: She turns the tea ritual into shared care with Hana, trading cups on Tuesdays and Thursdays and creating "a small island of niceness in the week."

rationales.self_direction: "I've built a life that works for me" and her answer that the conversation may just stop show her trying to live by her own standards rather than her family's script.
rationales.hedonism: She measures her path by getting home "in time to cook dinner and read before bed" and by "how it feels to live it," centering daily enjoyment over status.
rationales.achievement: She rejects "higher positions" and "a path that leads somewhere" because they look like waking up exhausted every day.
rationales.power: Her brother's version of success is "higher positions" and "more responsibility," and she explicitly refuses to treat that kind of status as desirable.

rationales.self_direction: "I wanted to" and "This is what I protect" make the meal an act done purely on her own terms, without needing anyone else's approval.
rationales.hedonism: She lingers over the smell, texture, warmth, and slow eating at the window, turning the whole afternoon into a pleasure ritual.

rationales.self_direction: She rejects the demand to "fix these problems" or "optimize my way out of it" and instead gives herself permission to cry and feel what she feels.
rationales.hedonism: The bath, wine, and "permission to feel bad without trying to optimize" show active care for comfort and immediate experience even on a bad week.

rationales.self_direction: "choosing, again, to make my own day good" and "Tonight is mine" show her taking ownership of how the day will feel instead of waiting for someone else to define it.
rationales.hedonism: Coffee, pastries, a good book, and "a body that's been cared for" are presented as her answer to "What do you want from life?"
He calls helping Amira "the right use of a Tuesday evening" and explicitly chooses it over "watching Netflix again," giving up an easy comfort option.
rationales.benevolence: He spends his evening helping Amira with a bug she'd been stuck on for two days, which is direct support for someone nearby.
rationales.universalism: Doing this work "at the nonprofit" because it felt like "the right use" of his evening points toward helping people beyond himself.

rationales.conformity: He says the problem was "the room" because "nobody else flinched," and that group atmosphere is exactly why he let it pass.
rationales.universalism: He notices the drivers have "zero protections" and still says nothing, then admits he probably won't bring it up tomorrow either.

rationales.hedonism: He says he "picked convenience" and chose the sixty-euro flight over the slower option, prioritizing ease and personal comfort in the moment.
rationales.security: The train would have been "three times the price" and eaten "half my weekend travel budget," so protecting his limited money clearly drove the choice.
rationales.universalism: He knowingly books the airline "flagged for its emissions record" and admits he chose the easier option anyway.

rationales.power: Because Sandra is "senior to me" and leading the roadmap review, he gives up influence in the moment and stays quiet instead of asserting himself.
rationales.conformity: He "went with the safe option" and changed the subject rather than risk social friction at lunch.
rationales.universalism: Sandra's comment was clearly exclusionary, but he "changed the subject" and left it unchallenged.

rationales.self_direction: He says Stefan shutting it down gave him "an excuse to stop" and admits he wants "someone else to fight this one," surrendering his own agency.
rationales.conformity: He doesn't want to "make waves" or make anyone uncomfortable, so he files the issue away instead of pushing further.
rationales.universalism: He knows the reporting band lets the company underreport, yet chooses the "easier path" and stops rather than pursuing the harm it hides.

rationales.conformity: Tomasz "laughed it off" and Lukas followed that lead, deciding not to say anything and returning to his Jira tickets.
rationales.universalism: He notices the joke about an Eastern European teammate and even sees that the laugh "didn't quite reach his eyes," but still lets the moment pass.

rationales.benevolence: He has "showed up consistently" for the Haddads, and Marwan's line that he is "part of the story now" shows a real supportive bond with that family.
rationales.universalism: Helping a Syrian family with paperwork and caring about their daughter doing better is concrete concern for people beyond his immediate circle.

rationales.self_direction: He stops "choosing silent" and says the comment bothered him "out loud" to someone with authority, even though he knows it costs him to do it.
rationales.conformity: Instead of smoothing things over again, he raises "something mildly uncomfortable" in a work conversation and refuses to let it pass.
rationales.universalism: He speaks up about the remark during Tomasz's onboarding because he wants the team to be more careful with that kind of exclusion.

rationales.security: With "Forty euros left until payday," he chooses the cheaper coffee and keeps pointing to money as the reason he takes the easier path.
rationales.universalism: He knows the fair-trade price means "someone down the supply chain is getting squeezed," but buys the store brand anyway and connects it to other times he stayed quiet.
Feeling "like a fraud" after Tomasz caught his eye shows he sees his own silence at work as a failure to act on what he thinks matters.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I had a valid point and I swallowed it whole" and calls it "exhausting pretending you don't have a voice," showing she suppresses her own judgment.
rationales.conformity: She keeps quiet because "you're new, you keep your head down" and follows the veteran teacher's lead instead of challenging the group.
She "adjusted the unit plans" and notes Amma was proud of her for "not making a fuss," emphasizing compliance over objection.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I had the words right there. I didn't say them. I never do," showing she withholds her own view even when she knows what she wants to say.
rationales.conformity: She repeats "I understand, uncle" and takes pride in not escalating because "that's how you're supposed to handle these things" and "don't give anyone a reason to complain about you."

rationales.self_direction: It was "automatic" to say yes to the family plan, and she says it "didn't even occur" to her to pause or choose for herself.
rationales.conformity: She agrees to attend before checking with anyone and dismisses the workshop because "Dadiji's plans are set," deferring to family expectations without question.
rationales.tradition: She prioritizes Rakshabandhan plans and says "You don't skip family things for a workshop. That's just how it is," grounding the choice in established family custom.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I know I'm right about Arjun and I still didn't say it," showing she abandons her own judgment when pressured by authority.
rationales.conformity: When "Mrs. Deshpande looked at me" she immediately switched to "yes ma'am, I'll send the note," yielding to the senior teacher's cue.
rationales.benevolence: She insists "He doesn't need punishment, he needs someone to be patient with him" and feels sick because she is "supposed to be looking out for him."

rationales.self_direction: She asks "Who am I to decide my approach is better?" and admits she is "so afraid of the gap" between what feels right and what she is told to do.
rationales.conformity: The thought of Mrs. Deshpande finding out makes her "stomach drop" because the senior teacher writes her probation review, so fear of disapproval still governs her choice.
rationales.benevolence: She wants to speak to Arjun's mother "gently" and quietly gives him "a spare" notebook, centering his needs rather than punishment.

rationales.self_direction: She "spoke with the mother directly" and chose her own approach instead of sending the note she had been expected to send.
rationales.benevolence: She checks in with Arjun's mother, hears that "it's been a hard few months," and promises "I'd keep an eye on him."

rationales.self_direction: She reflects that "I did something different" and "found a way to do what I thought was right," giving more weight to her own judgment than before.
She privately thinks Meera is "brave" but says nothing, and then retreats to "It's not my place," suppressing her own view.
rationales.conformity: She stays silent because "everyone was already piling on," choosing not to disrupt the family's consensus.

rationales.self_direction: She answers "no, of course not" to reassure Amma, and the response feels like "a small door closing," suggesting she shuts down her own options.
rationales.conformity: Amma's visible relief after her answer shows Priya is telling her mother what is expected rather than voicing uncertainty.

rationales.hedonism: She says "I haven't had an hour to myself in I don't know how long" and skips chai because even that feels like "more effort than it was worth," so enjoyment keeps being deferred.
rationales.conformity: She avoids staying out because "Amma would ask where I was" and chooses "the path of least resistance" instead of inviting friction at home.
rationales.benevolence: Even while exhausted, she is "still helping Bhabhi with the kids" because "that's what family does."

rationales.self_direction: She says "The grade is accurate" but also "I already know what I'm going to do" and plans to change it anyway, overriding her own judgment.
rationales.conformity: The principal's hinted request and the looming "probation review" push her toward "keep[ing] everyone comfortable" rather than holding her ground.

rationales.self_direction: He keeps presenting the courier work as his path - "things are going well with the courier gigs" - even though relatives still think he should "finish school."

rationales.security: He "picked up a double shift ... because rent's due" and works until he has "enough to cover rent plus a bit extra," prioritizing financial stability.
rationales.tradition: He says "I'll probably go" to Diane's seventieth and then clarifies "I'm not going to skip it," treating the family milestone as something he should honor.

rationales.self_direction: He "switched my Tuesday route" and built his own system for where to stage, showing independent control over how he works.
rationales.achievement: He optimizes the route to "grab three or four lunch deliveries" because "it adds up over the week," focusing on improved performance and results.
rationales.tradition: The call turns into Grand-mere repeating a family story about her father delivering ice blocks, and Marcus says "I don't mind hearing it again."
rationales.benevolence: He calls Grand-mere after his shift and gives her his time even while she is only "half-listening," which reflects steady care for a close family member.

rationales.self_direction: He says never having to ask permission to "take a day off or switch a route" is the part that "still feels right," highlighting how much he values choosing for himself.
rationales.security: "Some weeks are tight" and "the money doesn't feel right," but he still prefers this arrangement, accepting instability rather than prioritizing steadiness.
rationales.conformity: He says life under "somebody else's schedule" in business school meant "I couldn't breathe in that," showing active rejection of imposed structure.

rationales.self_direction: "I just can't be the one who drops everything every time" shows him asserting his own limits instead of letting family decide for him.
rationales.security: He says ten days off would cost him "real money I don't have a cushion for" and fears the app "buries you if you go inactive," so he chooses income stability.
rationales.conformity: He gets "sharp" with Maman and refuses the expected family response instead of smoothing things over.
rationales.tradition: Pushing back on coming home for Thanksgiving and helping Uncle Rene conflicts with the family duty tied to a holiday visit.
rationales.benevolence: He admits "Rene needs the help" and that "family should show up," but still chooses not to be the one who helps a close relative.

rationales.self_direction: Despite her mother-in-law's comment, she keeps doing the translation work and says "I don't need to justify this to anyone," showing she is choosing this path for herself rather than deferring to family expectations.
rationales.universalism: She spends late-night hours translating "asylum application or work permit or medical form" so strangers can understand life-critical paperwork and feel "less terrifying."

rationales.hedonism: She uses "two hours after the kids' bedtime" to redo the flyer instead of taking a free evening, putting responsibility ahead of rest or enjoyment.
rationales.benevolence: She takes on the meeting materials because "nobody else would" and worries about neighbors like "Mrs. Reyes" being left confused and embarrassed.
rationales.universalism: Rewriting the flyer in "Korean and English" so non-Korean families can understand the recycling rules shows active concern for inclusion across language barriers.
Seeing the cleanup pulls at her because she used to spend "whole Saturdays hauling plastic" so a river stretch was cleaner, showing enduring concern for the environment even while she cannot join.

rationales.hedonism: She turns down "yoga, self-care" and says "I don't want a break," rejecting personal comfort in favor of usefulness.
rationales.universalism: She wants to join the river cleanup and is haunted by "migrant workers" locked in a factory fire, showing that environmental and human harm feels personally urgent to her.
She takes a shift at the refugee resettlement office and carefully flags extra Burmese notes because they were "probably questions she was too afraid to ask out loud."

rationales.conformity: Missing the committee meeting feels like failure because she told Mrs. Park she'd present and "letting someone down ... just eats at me," showing a strong pull to meet obligations.
rationales.benevolence: She stays up "every two hours with wet towels and children's Tylenol" and insists "my kids come first," centering the immediate needs of her family.

rationales.self_direction: On rare time alone she "should have napped" but instead chose to draft her own letter to a representative, acting on conviction rather than what others wish she would do.
rationales.hedonism: She gives up her rare chance to nap or "just look at the sky," using those hours for emotionally heavy advocacy instead of rest or pleasure.
rationales.universalism: Her letter argues against making emergency medical care harder for undocumented workers and draws on cases from the resettlement office to defend people falling through the cracks.

rationales.self_direction: She emails the cleanup group and reorganizes Saturday mornings with her husband, deliberately reshaping her routine around what matters to her.
rationales.universalism: Sending the letter and joining the river cleanup rotation are concrete steps to help both vulnerable people and the local environment.
She spends her first cleanup hauling "three bags of plastic" from the riverbank, taking direct action for the local environment.

rationales.self_direction: She keeps taking mission-driven translation work despite her cousin pushing corporate jobs, deciding for herself that "some things matter more" than higher-paying status paths.
rationales.security: She accepts pay that was "below market rate" and explicitly prioritizes meaning over "what shows up in my bank account," undercutting financial stability.
rationales.universalism: She translates testimonies about "water access issues in South Asia" and connects them to her grandmother's village, showing concrete concern for basic welfare beyond herself.

rationales.self_direction: She says she came here to build "a different kind of life than the one that was mapped out for me in Mumbai," asserting a self-authored path over inherited expectations.
rationales.security: Even with a rent increase and being "already stretched thin," she keeps choosing the garden over extra shifts and resists securing only "my own small corner."
rationales.benevolence: She spends Saturdays helping Amira's daughters experience the garden, and "watching those girls discover where food actually comes from felt like enough today."
rationales.universalism: She frames the choice as refusing to "stop caring about the bigger picture," and her work with a Syrian family serves people beyond her immediate household.

rationales.self_direction: She resists turning a meaningful encounter into networking because she left Mumbai to escape "every relationship being measured by what it can do for you."
rationales.universalism: The conversation centers on "plastic waste in the ocean" and community outreach translation, and she feels encouraged that other people also care about the wider world.
News about garment workers in Bangladesh "ruined my whole day," and she says she is "grieving for people you've never met" because "we're all connected."

rationales.tradition: She treats her grandmother's saying that "the hands should be busy while the mind rests" as real guidance and even calls her "wiser than all my searching."
rationales.benevolence: She spends the afternoon planting cucumbers with Mr. Chen and focuses on a small act that will feed local children.
rationales.universalism: Working the garden is her grounded response to "the big problems," and she values a quiet, cooperative act that nourishes people beyond herself.

rationales.self_direction: Despite rent worries and doubts about "living foolishly," she ends with "This is the life I chose," reaffirming her chosen way of living.
rationales.benevolence: Amira's message about her family eating tomatoes from the garden shows that Priya's teaching directly supported one family's dinner and her daughters' learning.
rationales.universalism: She situates the photo against "displacement, the inequality, the destruction of the planet" and still values small acts that help others and the wider world.

rationales.achievement: They "mapped out the first unit already" and wanted to "set this up right from the start," showing deliberate effort to do the job well.
rationales.benevolence: They spent their own money on worksheets because otherwise "the kids lose momentum," centering the students' needs over their own convenience.

rationales.self_direction: They turned down the job with "full benefits" because "the literacy program here is the whole reason I came," sticking with a path they chose for themselves.
rationales.security: They may "wish I'd just taken the safe thing" but still plan not to call back, choosing against a more stable option.
rationales.benevolence: "I keep thinking about Mateo and the other kids who just started trusting me" shows the decision was driven by care for the children relying on them.

rationales.self_direction: "I still can't make myself call her back" shows they are holding to their own chosen direction even after replaying the decision all night.
rationales.security: They acknowledge the other salary would have covered "student loans and left room to breathe" yet continue refusing the safer financial path.

rationales.self_direction: They deleted the county job emails because picturing "a curriculum someone else wrote" was something "in me just won't do," rejecting a more controlled path.
rationales.security: With "possible mid-year budget cuts" and safer jobs offering "steady pay" and a "pension," deleting those options goes against stability.

rationales.benevolence: They call Mateo reading to his little brother "the whole point," showing the day mattered because of what it did for a student's home life.

rationales.self_direction: They describe the good exhaustion as coming from having "actually built" something, unlike "following someone else's plan all day."
rationales.achievement: "Two kids moved from level B to level D in three weeks" and they reorganized the baskets so each child can find the right book, emphasizing concrete progress and competence.
rationales.benevolence: They stayed late so "every kid can find a book at their level," putting students' learning needs at the center.

rationales.self_direction: "Money isn't the only thing that matters" and being "tired of everyone acting like it should be" shows they are choosing by their own priorities rather than others' standards.
rationales.security: After calculating an extra "nine hundred a month," they still dismiss money as the deciding factor, moving away from the safer financial option.

rationales.self_direction: "I don't want to build my life around avoiding the worst-case scenario" and "I want to build it around something I actually chose" is a direct assertion of self-authored choice.
rationales.security: They admit "the safer path is right there" with better benefits and a pension, but refuse it anyway.
