rationales.benevolence: "I chose the kids" shows the decision is anchored in care for the students rather than personal ease.

rationales.self_direction: "I keep choosing this instead" and "right now I'm staying" shows a continued commitment to the path they personally want.
rationales.security: Even after weeks of worry and with the "safer move" easy to list on paper, they continue rejecting the more stable option.
They say supporting the motion would put long-standing client ties at risk "financially" and "personally," so preserving those stable relationships drives the choice.
rationales.conformity: They "didn't say anything" in the meeting because "you don't blow that up in a board meeting," prioritizing smooth relations over open conflict.
rationales.universalism: Despite recent photos of workers without proper protective gear, they stayed silent and let the motion fail rather than back broader protection for vulnerable people.

rationales.security: They funded the delayed pump installation immediately even though their wife said to wait until "cash flow is better," choosing action over caution.
rationales.benevolence: The son's call mattered enough that they acted right away, and "I told him I was proud" shows support for someone close to them shaped the decision.
rationales.universalism: They transferred money to cover a "clean-water pump installation" and said "some things shouldn't wait," prioritizing help for people beyond their immediate circle.

rationales.conformity: They could have asked the hard questions but stayed quiet because raising it would mean "delaying the whole partnership review," going along with the existing course.
rationales.universalism: They suspected the solvent was unsafe, "could smell it from the parking lot," and still chose not to ask about possible harm to workers.

rationales.benevolence: They frame the business as what funds "my children's projects" and other giving, showing responsibility toward people in their orbit.
rationales.universalism: "The ability to actually do something about the problems I see" and funding "the foundation" and "scholarships" shows a broad, outward-looking sense of responsibility.

rationales.conformity: Saying "the agenda is always packed and nobody wants to hear philosophy from the recycling guy" shows reluctance to disrupt the room's expectations.
rationales.universalism: They connect the hawker uncle's lease worries to "small people squeezed by systems that don't see them," treating a wider social pattern as something that deserves action.

rationales.conformity: They "didn't push back" and "changed the subject," choosing table harmony over challenging the group.
rationales.universalism: They let comments dismissing migrant worker housing pass without objection, even though the issue "sat with me on the drive home," leaving broader harm unchallenged.
They chose the cheaper route even though the company "cuts corners on packaging for rural deliveries" and someone may face "a damaged pump at the other end."

rationales.achievement: He highlights that he "exceeded expectations" and immediately starts calculating the director track, showing that reaching one milestone only sharpens the push for the next one.

rationales.hedonism: He "didn't sleep much" while covering extra work and shipping the project, pushing present comfort aside for the job.
rationales.achievement: He took on two sick teammates' workload, hit every deadline, and notes that the "Director noticed," showing strong drive to perform and be seen doing it.
"Excellent work that nobody sees might as well not exist" and his fixation on being a "strong candidate" show intense concern with outperforming a peer and getting recognized.
rationales.power: He is preoccupied with "high-profile projects that get executive attention" and whether visibility will decide the promotion, showing focus on position inside the hierarchy.

rationales.hedonism: Being in the office until "9pm every day" and skipping holiday travel shows he is giving up rest and enjoyment for work pressure.
rationales.achievement: He treats the delayed promotion decision as "more time to prove myself" and stays put because Ahmad might be "making moves," keeping advancement at the center of his choices.
rationales.security: He says leaving feels like "a risk I can't take," showing that threat avoidance is driving the decision to stay in Dubai.
rationales.benevolence: He misses his sister's engagement and dismisses it as "just one event," putting work ahead of an important family moment.

rationales.security: He explains that his visa is tied to employment and that losing the job would mean "30 days to find another or leave," so his thinking is dominated by threat and instability.
rationales.benevolence: Layla wanted him there "in person" and Mama "barely looked at the camera," underscoring that work choices have pulled him away from close family.

rationales.hedonism: Standing under fireworks and feeling "nothing" while avoiding anything that might "slow you down" shows present enjoyment has been pushed aside.
rationales.achievement: He says "Get the promotion, obviously" and describes living with his "head down, eyes on the goal," keeping advancement as the default objective.
rationales.security: Calling it "survival mode" and saying he is surviving "the uncertainty" and the feeling that "nothing is really mine" shows a strong need for steadiness.

rationales.self_direction: "I realized I was building a life I wouldn't want to live" and booked a non-refundable ticket, deliberately choosing a life he can stand behind instead of drifting on work demands.
rationales.achievement: He accepts the feedback about "strategic visibility" and signs up for the all-hands presentation despite discomfort to position himself for the next level.
rationales.benevolence: Booking a non-refundable trip for Mama's birthday and saying the promotion "can't be the only thing that matters" shows renewed priority for a close family tie.

rationales.achievement: He took the visible presentation opportunity, notes that it "went well," and pays close attention to the positive feedback afterward.

rationales.hedonism: He chooses a nice dinner and says "Tonight, I'm going to let myself feel this," making room to enjoy the win instead of racing past it.
rationales.achievement: "Two years of work" and "all those late nights, the stress, the sacrifices" are framed as paying off in the promotion.
rationales.power: The new title, 15% raise, and "a team of twelve direct reports instead of five" show movement into greater authority and status.

rationales.self_direction: He spends the presentation sketching his own one-act-play unit and keeps imagining a very different job, showing a pull toward doing work on his own terms.
rationales.stimulation: He is "crawling out of my skin" in repetitive PD and lights up when remembering the "electric" mock trial and the wilderness guide posting.
rationales.conformity: He resents the pacing guide, wants to throw the binder, and describes his best teaching as something he had to "sneak in," showing open friction with the prescribed rules.

rationales.stimulation: Teaching "the same intro lesson five periods in a row" leaves him on "autopilot," showing routine and repetition draining him rather than energizing him.
He misses the version of himself who woke up "not knowing what the day would look like," treating uncertainty as what felt most alive and right.
rationales.security: He says he traded "the unknown for the predictable" and is no longer sure that was the right choice, pushing against stability rather than embracing it.

rationales.self_direction: He "threw out" the lesson plan mid-class and taught the way he thought the room needed, rather than staying inside the script.
rationales.stimulation: The "messy and loud" walking debate and the feeling that something "catches fire" show him actively seeking energetic, unpredictable work.
rationales.conformity: He contrasts what worked with "the binder" and the observation cycle, and says he would have to "fight a system every single day" to teach this way.

rationales.stimulation: His mind keeps going to the six-month wilderness guide contract, and five-year plans make him feel like he "can't breathe," showing a strong pull toward a less predictable life.
rationales.hedonism: He chooses the camping trip over staying home to grade and describes the weekend through bouldering, the cold river, and music around the fire.
rationales.security: Thinking about breaking his lease and recoiling from mapped-out future plans pushes against stability and settled commitments.

rationales.self_direction: He is drawn to a "student-directed, no textbook" class and is willing to question whether "this whole career might not be the right container," showing a strong need to choose a path that fits him.
rationales.stimulation: The appeal of the "innovation lab" is that it breaks from textbook routine and creates something more open-ended and alive than his other periods.
rationales.conformity: He immediately chafes at the committee, extra meetings, and four more periods of "binder teaching," showing resistance to institutional structure.

rationales.tradition: Seeing the old Fenix warehouse brings back going there with Mam when it was "a working warehouse," and he dwells on how "the bones are there but the life has changed."
He rejects the idea that you can recreate his grandmother's house with "an app controlling all the lights," insisting that warmth came from lived family practice, not a modern imitation.

rationales.conformity: He accepts being fifteen minutes late and getting "a warning from the app" because he prioritized the visit over the platform's timetable.
rationales.tradition: The Zeeland dialect, his grandfather surviving the flood, and the line about "solid ground" show him locating meaning in inherited family history.
rationales.benevolence: He stops to drink coffee with a lonely regular customer, listens to her talk about her late husband, and says it felt like "the most honest thing I'd done all week."

rationales.security: He took "a full day of bookings" because he "needed the money," letting financial necessity drive the decision.
rationales.tradition: Missing Koningsdag at Uncle Willem's, with "Opa's flag on the pole like always," means stepping away from a familiar family ritual he believes he should have kept.
rationales.benevolence: He hates that his niece had to ask where he was and says "I should have been there," showing he put paid work ahead of being with close family.

rationales.security: He says the clock, radio, and everything being "where it's been for thirty years" let his body finally rest in a predictable environment.
rationales.tradition: He drives to Zeeland for coffee, cake, and stories about Dad, then says the house has "the shape of a day I recognise from childhood."
rationales.benevolence: He makes time to be at Mam's house and spend the afternoon with her instead of staying in the delivery grind.

rationales.security: He envies that his father "knew where he'd be every day for the next ten years" and says his own life feels like "scattered dots," showing a strong pull toward predictable structure.
rationales.tradition: He measures his life against his father's "shop, the house, the family under one roof" and keeps returning to rituals handed down over time.
rationales.benevolence: He says those routines mattered because "the whole family moved through them together" and that a ritual needs someone else to care whether he keeps it.

rationales.tradition: When Bram asks him to say grace, he does it and hears that "Oma would have liked it," making the meal a continuation of an older family practice.
rationales.benevolence: He shows up for Bram's birthday dinner and honors his son's request even though the family setup could have been awkward.

rationales.power: There is "no negotiation," "no union rep to call," and only "a feedback form that goes nowhere," leaving him with almost no leverage over the terms of his work.
rationales.security: He objects to lower base pay because it replaces a "steady amount" with competition for surge slots, and he underscores that "this is how I pay rent."
rationales.tradition: He judges the change against his father's handshake code, treating the older standard of keeping your word as the morally solid way to work.

rationales.security: He says that unchanging ground is what keeps him from feeling "completely adrift," showing how much he relies on enduring anchors for steadiness.
rationales.tradition: He drives to the old church, sits in the family pew, and says what steadies him is "Family. The language. The church" and Mam's Sunday table.
rationales.benevolence: What he names as solid ground is relational: his mother, Bram asking him to say grace, and the sense that something meaningful was passed along.

rationales.security: "I kept thinking about whether Amma took her evening medication yesterday" and "I just worry" centers protecting a parent's health and keeping things under control.
rationales.benevolence: He is preoccupied with Amma's medication and quietly supports Ananya at dinner by "just sat there passing the rice" when she looked drained.

rationales.stimulation: He rejects the fintech offer and bristles at being seen as "too afraid to try something new," showing he is choosing against novelty rather than pursuing it.
rationales.security: He defends staying because "my PF is solid" and "Amma and Appa are right here," explicitly favoring financial and family stability.

rationales.self_direction: He thought "we should get another opinion" but "didn't push it," setting aside his own judgment in favor of others' authority.
rationales.security: Wanting a second opinion before another surgery shows caution around a high-stakes medical decision and a wish to reduce risk.
rationales.benevolence: His explanation is that he is "scared of Appa going through all that pain again," which centers protecting his father from suffering.

rationales.tradition: He dwells on "Sunday puja," "the smell of camphor," and his grandfather explaining the epics, treating those inherited rituals as something important to preserve.
rationales.benevolence: What bothers him is that Vikram "doesn't have that" shared family experience, showing concern for his children's connection to the family bond.

rationales.stimulation: He turns away from the startup offer after reading about layoffs and shaky equity, choosing against the uncertainty of "two rounds of layoffs in eighteen months."
rationales.security: He weighs "college fees" and rising "medical bills" against unstable equity and weak healthcare, clearly prioritizing predictability and protection.
rationales.benevolence: He frames the choice around caring for "a daughter whose college fees start in two years" and parents with rising medical bills, then spends the evening helping Ananya with physics.

rationales.tradition: Making "Paati's rasam recipe" immediately brings him back to "her kitchen in Mylapore" and childhood summers, showing attachment to family practices handed down across generations.

rationales.security: He praises the old path because it led to "something stable," and his worry about film is that he does not know what it will lead to.
rationales.tradition: He anchors the moment in family lineage: "My father was an engineer. His father was a civil servant. There was a path."
rationales.benevolence: Even while worried, he focuses on taking care of loved ones and says he does not want Vikram "to stop talking to me about it."

rationales.security: He calls film "brutal and unpredictable" and insists that if Vikram is serious there should be "a proper path," showing a strong preference for structure and reduced risk.
rationales.tradition: He brings Amma and Paati into the conversation and still tries to fit Vikram's choice into "actual structured" programs and "a proper path," favoring an established route over a fully improvised one.
rationales.benevolence: He tells Vikram "I'd look into some programs with him," which is concrete support rather than dismissal.

rationales.self_direction: He highlights "my framework" and the months of building credibility through his own sharper questions and seminar interventions, emphasizing an independently shaped path.
rationales.achievement: He has "spent months building that credibility" and lingers over his name on the memo, treating visible excellence and recognition as important outcomes.
rationales.power: He relishes "when the room orients toward you" and being the person others defer to, which is about influence and standing at the front.
rationales.benevolence: When a newer student asked if he could review her proposal, he "said yes of course," offering help to someone junior to him.

rationales.power: What stings is being treated like "someone you go around rather than talk to," showing how much control over the process and others' deference matters to him.
rationales.conformity: He objects that if Huda wanted a different format she should have raised it "in the planning meeting" instead of through back channels, emphasizing proper procedure and expected conduct.
rationales.benevolence: He dismisses Huda's concern and focuses on blame and seniority, saying "I've been running events longer than she's been in the program" rather than showing any care for her perspective.

rationales.self_direction: He wants the paper to remain proof that he can produce something "on my own," and sees co-authorship as taking away that independence.
rationales.achievement: He calls the paper his "ticket" to conferences and CV distinction, and says losing sole authorship weakens the signal of what he accomplished.
rationales.power: He says the paper should show he "belong at the front of a room," making status and professional standing part of what is at stake.
rationales.conformity: He recoils from sharing credit for the sake of "departmental cohesion," treating the collaborative norm as a burden rather than something to uphold.
rationales.benevolence: He frames co-authorship mainly as his work being "diluted" and "redistribution," with no interest in colleagues' benefit or shared success.
rationales.universalism: He rejects the broader collective good behind "departmental cohesion" and reduces the decision to what it does for his own signal and career.

rationales.achievement: He says there is nothing wrong with "wanting recognition for what I built" and is physically bothered by a conference program where his name is no longer alone.
rationales.power: The imagined loss feels like "someone move your chair at a table you set," which shows how strongly he values keeping his place and authority.
rationales.tradition: He recalls his father's long civil-service career only to reject that model, saying he decided early he would not let his work disappear the way his father's did.
rationales.benevolence: Even when Faisal speaks gently, he interprets it through the lens of losing his personal stamp, keeping the focus on himself over family or collaborators.

rationales.stimulation: He welcomes Monterrey as "finally something different on the calendar" and says the office routine felt like "wearing a shirt that's too tight."
rationales.security: His restlessness with the same commute and desire for change shows comfort and predictability are not what he is trying to preserve.

rationales.stimulation: The empty warehouse feels like "possibility" and "the first page of a notebook," showing how energized he is by new terrain and unfinished beginnings.
rationales.hedonism: He lingers on the pleasures of the trip, from "carnitas" to the mountains from the hotel balcony, treating the sensory experience itself as part of the reward.
rationales.benevolence: Calling Lucía and hearing she might join the next trip "made the whole day feel complete," showing how much the close relationship matters in the experience.

rationales.stimulation: He is "already counting days until the next Monterrey trip," with the ordinary desk view serving mostly as something to escape.
rationales.security: The problem is not danger but sameness; the familiar office scene immediately leaves him wanting out rather than settled.

rationales.stimulation: Tomás's fixed ten-year plan leaves him feeling "incomprehension," and he describes his own life as multiple cities and quitting when told to specialize.
rationales.security: He reacts to predictable routines, stable plans, and even the city itself as "gray," which shows stability feels stifling rather than reassuring.
rationales.tradition: He cannot relate to Tomás repeating the same resort "his parents always went to," treating inherited patterns as alien rather than grounding.

rationales.stimulation: He admits, "I always think about what's next," making ongoing movement and the next challenge central even while he stays to finish the job.
rationales.achievement: He pushes back by pointing to his "track record of getting things done" and the fact that he has "never left a project half-finished," grounding his response in competence and completion.

rationales.stimulation: He frames the night as proof that "variety doesn't always require a boarding pass," showing he is actively chasing novelty even close to home.
rationales.hedonism: Going dancing at a new salsa club and staying until "1am" is a straightforward choice for present enjoyment and pleasure.
rationales.benevolence: He chooses to share the night with Lucía, making the pleasurable outing a way of investing in that close relationship.

rationales.security: He says the old house was "just a building" and contrasts himself with relatives who treat places like "anchors," showing little need for stable attachment points.
rationales.tradition: His indifference to the family house sale and claim that memories are "not in the walls" rejects the symbolic pull of family continuity and inherited place.

rationales.stimulation: He says the construction phase is "always my favorite part" and admits he gets "restless again" once things start running smoothly, showing he is drawn to novelty more than maintenance.
rationales.security: He explicitly identifies smooth operation as the point where he becomes restless, so stability itself is something he pushes against rather than seeks.

rationales.stimulation: What drains him is a room focused only on "risk, risk, risk" and "mitigation," while he longs for "new routes" and "experiments."
rationales.hedonism: He treats the canyon weekend as "rescuing" him and "balance," making immediate enjoyment a needed counterweight rather than an afterthought.
rationales.security: The heavy emphasis on redundancies and contingencies leaves him "deflated," showing that safety-first thinking feels deadening rather than reassuring.
rationales.benevolence: He says "Lucía is balance" and counts on the weekend with her as an emotional counterweight, showing how central that close bond is to staying grounded.

rationales.stimulation: His retirement fantasy is "exploratory travel" — learning a language, living somewhere for a few months, then moving on — which is a direct embrace of novelty and change.
rationales.hedonism: The day centers on simple pleasures like the canyon view, the picnic, and "those little marzipan candies I like," with no attempt to defer enjoyment.
rationales.benevolence: Lucía's offer to come with him "felt like something," showing the imagined future matters partly because it is shared with someone close.

rationales.self_direction: He describes his career as "a series of sprints toward interesting things" and resists pressure to "settle down," framing the path in self-authored terms rather than others' expectations.
rationales.stimulation: He keeps going because each project feels "genuinely different," and says being "still curious about what comes next" is what matters.
rationales.security: The idea of doing one thing for twenty-five years brings "panic" and "suffocation," showing durable stability feels threatening rather than comforting.

rationales.power: Khalid spoke over him using numbers Tariq had gathered, and Tariq 'just sat there,' leaving him publicly cut out of influence over his own work.
He says 'someone else holds the cards' and that being unable to move the ultrasound request forward leaves him feeling stuck and powerless.
rationales.benevolence: He admits he 'snapped at Faisal over homework' even though 'it had nothing to do with him,' showing harm to someone close because of displaced frustration.

rationales.achievement: He successfully reorganized the shift crisis and was told it was 'the calmest crisis she'd been through,' showing effective, recognized competence.
rationales.power: He pulled everyone together, set the frame for solving the staffing gap, and the ward ran well because he 'kept it steady' without having to force anyone.
He gave Amira 'full responsibility' and reflects that there are things he may not need to 'hold onto so tightly,' which means loosening his grip on control.
rationales.benevolence: He trusted Amira with the audit and praised her work 'in front of the team,' actively supporting a colleague's growth and recognition.

rationales.self_direction: He drafted his own protocol from patterns he had been tracking for months and openly pushed back when told to use the regional template instead.
rationales.power: Despite making the case, he is told 'the decision was made' and ends up implementing a protocol he does not believe in, with no real say over the outcome.
He corrected being introduced as only 'one of the senior nurses' and fixates on title and standing, showing strong concern for status and recognition.
He is talked over in the meeting, then 'sat there for the rest of the meeting barely saying a word,' emphasizing how little influence he feels he has in the room.
rationales.conformity: He says he is afraid of being seen as 'the angry nurse' and that if a doctor talks over you 'that's just how it is,' so he restrains himself to avoid becoming the problem.

rationales.achievement: He sought out a nursing leadership conference, handled his own shift coverage, and followed up on the form, showing active investment in professional advancement.
rationales.power: Even after doing the preparation, he can only wait on his department head's sign-off and says there is 'no reason for this to take a week' except that it is not his priority.

rationales.achievement: He spent 'two evenings' preparing a clear, documented staffing case with patient numbers, acuity trends, and incident reports, showing strong performance focus.
rationales.power: He concludes the real decisions happen through 'relationships, politics' and conversations he is 'not part of,' highlighting exclusion from influence.

rationales.achievement: He says 'I don't think I can stop preparing. That's who I am,' showing a continued commitment to doing the work thoroughly even after earlier disappointment.

rationales.power: She has led the committee for six years, but when Meena aunty questions her she 'stayed quiet' while no one defended her, leaving her authority publicly undercut.
rationales.conformity: She says defending herself would have looked exactly like what she was accused of, so she swallows the insult rather than risk social disapproval.

rationales.benevolence: She brings 'three containers of biryani' to a neighbor after surgery and says 'showing up when people need you' is what it is all about.
She spent 'an hour consoling' Priya after the birthday exclusion and, when asked, insists her focus is 'Priya, obviously.'

rationales.self_direction: She says 'I said yes before even thinking about it' and wanted to say 'let me check with Ravi first,' showing that she set aside her own choice.
rationales.conformity: 'What else could I say?' and 'the math is simple' frame the decision as obedience to expected family roles rather than personal preference.
rationales.benevolence: She agrees to host the in-laws for six weeks and immediately starts planning room changes and low-sodium meals around Appa's needs.

rationales.achievement: She lists concrete work like managing finances, sewing a recital costume until midnight, and proofreading seven essays, emphasizing sustained competence and effort.
rationales.benevolence: Her examples center on taking care of family members' needs, from finding Ravi's passport to supporting Priya's recital and Vikram's applications.

rationales.self_direction: She 'volunteered to step back' from leading Navratri planning, making an active choice about her own role instead of automatically keeping the responsibility.
rationales.power: Stepping away from vendor contracts and volunteer schedules means giving up the directing role she usually holds in committee work.

rationales.self_direction: She takes fifteen minutes alone on the balcony 'doing nothing,' carving out time for herself instead of staying absorbed in household demands.
rationales.hedonism: The quiet chai break with 'just me and the crows' is a small but deliberate moment of rest and enjoyment.

rationales.benevolence: She is proud that Vikram is 'kind' and 'notices people,' and she links that to years of family members showing up for one another.

rationales.conformity: She had already finished cleaning for the visit and immediately notices that 'Amma looked at the sheets,' showing how closely she tracks whether she is meeting household expectations for guests and elders.

rationales.self_direction: She says the kitchen is 'the one domain where I should have some say' yet still says nothing, showing her own preferences being overridden in her own home.
rationales.conformity: She tells herself 'she is seventy-two and she is a guest,' so she suppresses complaint in order to preserve expected respect and hospitality.
rationales.benevolence: Even while angry, she 'made everyone's favorite dinner anyway,' continuing to care for the household instead of acting on her frustration.

rationales.power: She insists 'someone has to keep track of things' and keeps reminding Priya because she feels responsible for keeping the household under control.
rationales.benevolence: Her pressure comes from concern that Priya will 'coast' and fail, and she frames letting that happen as something a mother should not do.

rationales.self_direction: She finally says 'this is my house and my family,' asserting her own judgment after two weeks of constant suggestions and corrections.
rationales.conformity: Rather than swallowing the comments to keep peace with an elder, she pushes back and accepts Ravi seeing her as 'too harsh.'
rationales.benevolence: In the response, she says she wants her children to remember that 'I showed up' and 'fed people,' grounding her efforts in care for close family.

rationales.self_direction: He is seriously considering switching labs again because the new project is 'so much more interesting,' prioritizing his own curiosity over the path already set by his committee.
rationales.stimulation: He lights up at 'VR and neural mapping,' 'fieldwork in Costa Rica,' and the chance to be in a rainforest instead of routine sequencing, showing a strong pull toward novelty and challenge.
rationales.security: He is tempted to 'uproot everything again' for a project he admits he does not 'even fully know,' favoring uncertainty over a stable setup.
rationales.conformity: He pushes against the expectation to stay put, bristling at 'what will your committee think?' and questioning how anyone can just do one thing.

rationales.stimulation: His attention keeps jumping to 'flights to New Zealand,' 'pottery classes,' and learning to surf, and he says his brain 'doesn't want to stay in one place.'
rationales.achievement: When Dr. Sharma asked about his thesis timeline, he had 'literally nothing' to say and cannot stay on his analysis, which undercuts sustained progress.
rationales.security: He has no thesis direction and fears his mother will learn he 'might not actually finish it,' showing instability around a supposedly secure path.
rationales.conformity: He knows he is not meeting academic and family expectations, admitting Amma will be 'so disappointed' and that he cannot do what everyone else seems able to do.
rationales.benevolence: By hiding that he 'hasn't figured anything out' while his mother tells everyone about the PhD, he is failing to protect a close relationship from avoidable hurt.

rationales.self_direction: He does not delete the message and keeps the 'little door' open even though 'everyone' would say to stay in the PhD, showing willingness to author his own path.
rationales.stimulation: A Singapore startup, a rooftop garden, a young team, and the idea of more 'velocity' all clearly energize him far more than routine paper reading.
rationales.achievement: He spends an hour on company videos 'instead of reading papers,' pulling attention away from the work he is supposed to advance.
rationales.security: The opportunity is a 'random job' that could mean leaving his PhD midstream, so he is actively entertaining a less predictable future.

rationales.self_direction: He chooses to do the interview and keep moving through the process even though leaving the lab he just transferred into would be hard to justify.
rationales.stimulation: He is energized by a role building 'something new' and by being valued for the 'weird' project he built for fun, which fits his attraction to novelty.
rationales.conformity: He keeps the interview secret because he knows people in the program will think he is 'not serious,' showing he is moving against the expectations around him.

rationales.stimulation: The second interview has him so charged up that his 'heart is doing weird things,' showing how much this new possibility is driving him.
rationales.achievement: He says he 'definitely bombed' the committee meeting because he could not focus, which directly undercuts competent performance.

rationales.self_direction: He is seriously considering a move where he would build a data pipeline from scratch in Singapore rather than stay in the PhD, weighing a path that is clearly his own.
rationales.stimulation: The offer centers on relocation, a new country, and a role starting from scratch, all of which keep his attention far more than lab work.
rationales.security: He describes the choice as a 'LinkedIn message away from blowing up my life again' and notes having 'no savings,' showing how destabilizing it could be.
rationales.tradition: Taking the startup offer would break from the established PhD route his family has been celebrating, and he knows it will be hard to explain to them.
rationales.benevolence: He lies to Amma that research is 'going great' and calls himself a 'terrible son,' signaling that his choices are straining his obligations to close family.

rationales.self_direction: He frames the move as 'run the experiment' to find out what fits instead of letting the current track decide for him.
rationales.stimulation: What keeps pulling him is the chance to know startup life and Singapore firsthand rather than stay with 'pure hypothesis.'
rationales.security: He knows that if he leaves and it fails, 'I can't just come back,' so he is actively weighing a much less protected path.
rationales.benevolence: He turns down Aditya's dinner invitation, says he has been doing that 'a lot lately,' and calls himself a 'terrible friend' and 'terrible son.'

rationales.self_direction: He makes the decision himself, writing 'Decided to accept the offer. Sent the email,' which is a direct act of choosing his own path.
rationales.stimulation: Accepting a January move to Singapore for a startup role is a clear step toward something new and unpredictable.
rationales.security: By leaving the PhD for an overseas startup, he is giving up a more established track for a riskier one.

rationales.self_direction: Even after expecting anger, he follows through and stays with the choice that feels like he has been 'searching for something,' rather than returning to the old path.
rationales.stimulation: He describes a 'fizzy feeling' like when 'something NEW' is coming, showing how strongly novelty is driving the decision.
rationales.tradition: The conversation with his parents turns on 'so no PhD?' and he continues anyway, stepping away from the expected academic path.
rationales.benevolence: He handles the news directly with Dr. Sharma, his parents, and Aditya, and the entry stays closely attuned to those relationships rather than treating the move as purely individual.

rationales.self_direction: He is still moving forward with the relocation even after finding the old promise to 'definitely finish my PhD,' choosing the life he now wants over the one he once pledged to follow.
rationales.stimulation: The fact that he is in the middle of packing for Singapore keeps the entry oriented around a major new chapter rather than staying put.
rationales.tradition: Leaving behind the old all-caps promise to 'definitely finish my PhD' shows a break from the established path he once took as a given.

rationales.self_direction: He says he has interrogated the decision 'from every angle' and still needs to move, showing an active choice rather than passive drift.
rationales.stimulation: He explicitly values that Singapore will be 'something different,' which is the main appeal he keeps returning to.
rationales.security: He rejects staying for 'safety' and chooses a path whose outcome he cannot know in advance.

rationales.hedonism: He turns his phone off, cooks shakshuka 'from scratch,' eats slowly with coffee, and says 'I needed it,' clearly prioritizing rest and sensory enjoyment.
Even with good wine and music, he keeps checking Slack and finally opens the laptop, saying he felt 'relieved' to stop pretending he was relaxing.
rationales.achievement: He keeps doing the math on whether he 'finish[ed] enough' and works another two hours, showing strong pressure to meet performance standards.
rationales.benevolence: He explains that if he is not working, 'someone on my team might be stuck,' so he is prioritizing his team's needs over his evening.

rationales.hedonism: After a ten-hour day he wants to run a bath, but instead goes straight back to prep and says he could not even argue he 'deserved an hour off.'
rationales.achievement: He keeps working after exhaustion to prepare for 'tomorrow's review,' putting performance ahead of recovery.

rationales.hedonism: He uses the forty-five-minute gap to get a cortado, sit at the window, and enjoy that 'small and quiet but nice' pause without a laptop or phone.
He goes to a restaurant he had wanted to try, but spends 'half the meal thinking about the quarterly review deck' and leaves feeling he was not really there.

rationales.conformity: She describes conferences as a 'careful dance' of saying 'all the right things' so nobody complains, and ends satisfied that 'nobody left upset.'

rationales.self_direction: She worries that being 'the person everyone expects me to be' may mean giving up the more approachable teacher she wants to be.
rationales.conformity: She 'followed protocol exactly' and did everything 'by the book' even though it cost her the student's trust.

rationales.tradition: She treats her mother's suggestions about 'that nice man from church' as an ongoing family script, saying 'it's just what mothers do' rather than rejecting it outright.

rationales.self_direction: She admits she may skip the ceramics class because of her parents' reaction and says she has 'started to forget what I want when no one's watching.'
rationales.conformity: She is known as the one who 'doesn't argue in meetings,' plans to smile politely at Teacher Lin, and avoids the class because she does not want to upset her parents.
rationales.tradition: She frames the conflict in family terms, saying 'that's not how my family works' and 'we show up for each other,' which keeps inherited expectations in charge.

rationales.self_direction: She picks up the registration form but still may not sign up, and she cannot even raise the topic with her mother, showing how little room she gives her own wants.
rationales.security: She talks herself toward the existing arrangement by emphasizing a 'stable job,' 'parents who care,' 'a routine that works,' and the cost of the class.
rationales.conformity: Her toast goes exactly as 'it was supposed to be,' she responds politely to Teacher Lin, and she centers Sunday dinners and family expectations over the hobby.

rationales.self_direction: He says he built the company to "do things my way," making personal choice the core frame for this situation.
rationales.power: He "held back" and let Anna present instead of changing the deck just to leave his "fingerprints," which means giving up control.
rationales.benevolence: He calls Anna's deck "solid work" and lets her present it, backing a colleague's success instead of centering himself.

rationales.self_direction: He dismisses the association's advice in favor of "actual decisions that affect actual outcomes," showing a strong preference to act on his own judgment.
rationales.conformity: He openly rejects the meeting's "standard recommendations for standard companies" and then "left early," breaking the expected script.

rationales.self_direction: He keeps coming back to not being able to "stomach the idea of someone else making decisions about my work again" and values knowing "I chose it."
rationales.security: He contrasts Marcus's offer with "stability, predictability, a clear path to retirement" and still leans away from it, choosing uncertainty over safety.

rationales.conformity: He says his first instinct was to "push back hard" but he "didn't" and let Erik handle the diplomacy, restraining himself to keep things smooth.
rationales.tradition: When Emilia mentions the "Lucia dinner tomorrow," he says yes and joins the holiday observance instead of skipping it.
rationales.benevolence: He frames the choice as keeping the "client's happy, team's intact," prioritizing relationship maintenance over venting his frustration.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects Marcus's offer because what he wants is "to keep making choices" and to face problems that are "problems I chose."
rationales.security: He declines the safer offer and even says Sara "wanted more certainty than I could give her," showing he keeps choosing a less secure path.
rationales.benevolence: He closes the office by handing out "bonuses" and is moved by Anna's tears and Erik's toast, showing real care for his team.

rationales.achievement: The postdoc presents his framework with "my slides" and "my structure," yet he "said nothing," letting his own work go uncredited.
rationales.power: He watches someone else claim "something I've been working on" and does not challenge it, surrendering status and control in the room.
He says the best conversations are the ones where "nobody's positioning themselves" or measuring publications and funding, explicitly preferring life away from status competition.

rationales.self_direction: He thinks his original framing is stronger but still restructures the chapter because "you have to write for the audience that matters," yielding to external demands.
rationales.hedonism: He spends the evening with "eyes burning," a messy flat, and "bread and cheese" after not cooking for three days, clearly sacrificing comfort.
rationales.achievement: He keeps reorganizing the chapter around reviewer expectations and pushes through exhaustion to strengthen the journal submission.
He is willing to adjust citations and play the journal game because it could get him "a better review at the European Journal of Political Economy."
rationales.power: He admits "you learn not to give away the full picture" when spots are limited, using information strategically in a competitive hierarchy.
He says he cannot become "another doctoral student waiting for permission" and is already thinking about what kind of "leverage" is needed to make things happen.
rationales.universalism: He prepared a proposal to give "doctoral candidates more presenting time," pushing for a fairer distribution of opportunity beyond his own immediate gain.

rationales.self_direction: When the committee stalls him, his response is to "find another route" because "standing still isn't really something I do well."
rationales.achievement: He assembled the application with an updated CV, polished statement, and letter, then immediately started looking for ways to keep the opportunity alive.

rationales.power: He openly defends being the one who runs meetings, reads everything first, and "know[s] how to organise people or push things forward."
rationales.conformity: He refuses to shrink himself to match "expectations of what a doctoral student should be" and is comfortable "tak[ing] up more space" than expected.

rationales.self_direction: He has a "clear view" on the right cases but ends up with "Her cases it is," unable to follow his own judgment.
rationales.power: Lena is "firm" and Bjorn's silence means "she won," leaving him to absorb the consequences without influence over the decision.

rationales.universalism: His hesitation centers on "cutting corners on something that goes to people who cannot complain if it breaks," showing concern for vulnerable end users over convenience.

rationales.security: The Dubai salary could cover "both girls' tuition in one year" and relieve family strain, but he still keeps choosing the less stable path.
rationales.universalism: He closes the job listing while thinking about why "clean water matters" and the families in Azraq, keeping the mission above an easier, better-paid role.
The work is framed around getting filters out, and Abu Faris's story that "the kids stopped getting sick last winter" shows the broader human impact driving the effort.

rationales.conformity: He keeps the Bekaa idea to himself because he has "learned not to pitch things until I have run the numbers," submitting to the board's expectations instead of speaking freely.

rationales.universalism: He is consumed by the fact that families now have to buy "trucked water at five times the cost" and starts calculating how many households are affected.

rationales.conformity: He says "yes to all of it" - logos, photos, and executive visits - because the funding requires going along with the sponsor's script.
rationales.universalism: Even while taking the deal, he keeps centering that "Fifty units is fifty families with clean water" and rejects treating camp residents as "props."

rationales.security: His daughter asks why they do not live in a bigger house, and he admits the uncertainty of whether the work will outlast "the next funding cycle," yet he stays with it.
rationales.universalism: He explains the business in terms of filters, camps, and how water-borne illness keeps kids out of school, showing the wider welfare aim at the center of his choices.
Even in a grim overtime shift, the work is about finishing the Zarqa batch - "Fifty units, all sealed and loaded" - for people who need the filters.

rationales.power: He dismisses Samir's promotion by saying "Samir's title is a line on a business card" and rejects measuring success by "Cars, titles" and schools.
rationales.universalism: What feels "real" to him is that a mother reported her son's eczema cleared after the filter, showing he values concrete social impact over status.

rationales.hedonism: He ate 'lunch ... at my desk' until the soup was 'lukewarm' and cut short balcony time to confirm maintenance, repeatedly letting work override simple enjoyment.
rationales.benevolence: He approved the supervisor's December leave because 'he hasn't taken time in months,' prioritizing a team member's wellbeing over staffing convenience.

rationales.hedonism: He 'just wanted to sit and drink tea' but let thoughts about covering shifts make it so 'the tea doesn't taste like anything anymore,' turning rest into self-denial.
rationales.achievement: He couldn't let himself sit still without feeling 'I wasn't being productive,' showing work output was the standard he kept applying even late at night.

rationales.hedonism: Even the small pleasure of tea got abandoned: he 'never actually finished the tea' and found it 'cold on the railing,' suggesting enjoyment was again pushed aside.
He deliberately stayed in the car to eat 'knafeh ... still warm' and notes he 'didn't check my phone once,' fully protecting a moment of enjoyment.
He was 'genuinely tired' and considered leaving early, but stayed, answered emails 'that could have waited,' and ate dinner 'standing in the kitchen' instead of resting.
rationales.conformity: He kept working because of 'how that would look, leaving at three when my team is there until five,' letting role expectations override his own needs.
rationales.benevolence: He treats his own rest as secondary because 'if I rest and something goes wrong, it's my fault' and 'if they rest, I'm the one who covered,' absorbing the burden for his team.

rationales.hedonism: At a gathering with 'good food and music' he spent the whole time thinking about Monday's deadline and 'left early,' missing the enjoyment he had anticipated.
rationales.achievement: A 'procurement deadline on Monday' dominated his attention so completely that it pulled him out of a family event, showing work demands took priority over leisure.

rationales.self_direction: He recoils from the brand brief as 'their story, not mine,' showing a clear pull toward making work on his own terms rather than executing someone else's agenda.
rationales.security: He says he 'should probably say yes because I need the money' but is still pulled toward refusing it, showing willingness to sacrifice stability for integrity.
rationales.universalism: The Syrian girl's sketchbook and not knowing if her grandmother is alive reshape the whole day for him, and he keeps questioning whether his art should do more for people beyond himself.

rationales.self_direction: He says no to Pieter and feels 'RELIEVED,' then starts imagining work that tells 'THEIR story instead of a brand's story,' choosing his own artistic direction.
rationales.stimulation: He wants something 'that moved,' says the murals were 'starting to feel like cages,' and is actively drawn to a new medium that breathes and changes.
rationales.achievement: He'd 'rather be broke and making something real than comfortable and decorating,' explicitly rejecting conventional career success in favor of meaning.
rationales.security: He knows there is 'No money in it probably' and still prefers that path, choosing uncertainty over financial comfort.
rationales.conformity: He brushes off Pieter's disappointment with 'Whatever. I can't be responsible for everyone's disappointment,' refusing to organize his choices around others' approval.
rationales.universalism: He spends the day making art with refugee kids and wants a collaborative piece that tells 'their story,' centering people beyond his immediate circle.

rationales.self_direction: He takes the 'very safe' hotel lobby job and says the honest faces series 'can always wait,' suppressing the work he actually wants to make.
rationales.conformity: He keeps the real series hidden because he can already hear relatives criticizing it, and says he'd need 'someone to tell me it's okay,' showing how strongly social approval governs the choice.

rationales.self_direction: He hides a real relationship and gives 'all the correct answers' at lunch, choosing concealment over speaking for himself.
rationales.conformity: He has become 'very efficient at predicting which battles aren't worth starting,' carefully managing family expectations to keep the room smooth.

rationales.self_direction: He admits the faces series is 'the best work I've ever done' but still keeps it unseen, letting avoidance override the art he actually believes in.
rationales.stimulation: He turns down a 'boundary-pushing' Beirut show and stays with 'technically proficient and completely forgettable' commercial work instead of challenge or novelty.
rationales.conformity: He says every option involves 'disappointing someone or drawing attention,' so he keeps choosing the safer, more socially acceptable path.
He says 'Of course I'll be there' and imagines wearing 'the right shirt' and saying 'the right things,' showing active alignment with family expectations and social scripts.
He smiles through the questions 'because that's what you do' and feels relieved when his aunt calls him 'a good boy,' showing how approval still organizes his behavior.
He refuses the Beirut show because he'd 'rather keep things smooth' and wants to avoid 'the shift in how people look at you when you stop being predictable,' prioritizing social ease over expression.

rationales.self_direction: He 'almost posted' the faces series, then imagines family reactions and 'covered everything back up,' pulling back from his own work at the last second.
rationales.conformity: He says the 'approval, the ease, the feeling of fitting in' are what he is afraid to lose, showing how strongly belonging still dictates his choices.

rationales.self_direction: He adds the tiny figures that feel true to him and then 'painted over them before anyone could see,' again erasing his own artistic impulse.
rationales.conformity: He still calls the faces series 'nothing, just experiments' and says showing it to family depends on finding someone who won't make him feel 'wrong,' showing continued deference to family judgment.

rationales.self_direction: Hearing the client say it was 'exactly what he envisioned' feels 'depressing' because the finished work fully serves someone else's vision rather than his own.
rationales.conformity: The same line shows he successfully matched the client's expectations, even though doing so left him feeling flattened.

rationales.self_direction: He finally showed his sister the faces pieces and says he may be 'getting tired of hiding the work,' a concrete step toward backing his own artistic voice.
rationales.conformity: Leaving the work uncovered and sharing it despite expecting the usual 'concern' or warning about Baba shows him loosening the habit of keeping others comfortable first.

rationales.achievement: Stayed past six finishing the mid-term analysis spreadsheets and tracked reading-club gains, showing sustained effort to improve outcomes.
rationales.benevolence: She says she "can't separate myself from the outcome" and takes Ravi's possible failure personally, showing deep care for specific students.
rationales.universalism: She worries students will end up in "the same cycle their parents were stuck in," framing the work as breaking a wider pattern of disadvantage.

rationales.achievement: "Fourth out of nine" feels unacceptable to her, and she lists mapping weak areas, differentiated worksheets, and Saturdays as evidence she should be nearer the top.

rationales.hedonism: She "spent two hours building supplementary inference exercises tonight instead of eating dinner," choosing more work over immediate comfort.
rationales.achievement: She came home, analyzed the question-type breakdown, and built new exercises right away, showing a relentless drive to improve results.
rationales.universalism: She argues the test is really measuring "privilege" and language mismatch, focusing on structural unfairness beyond her own ranking.

rationales.achievement: "Six months of readings from twelve sites" shows disciplined evidence-gathering to build a case instead of just venting frustration.
rationales.universalism: She is trying to force action on contaminated school water and is ready to go "to the press" because children are drinking it every day.

rationales.achievement: Her planned day off turns into three stacks of school and NGO work, and she rates her own effort a five while still scrutinizing the results.
rationales.benevolence: She has not been home in seven months, missed her cousin's engagement, and says the "missing feels like something I can't afford right now," putting work ahead of family presence.

rationales.self_direction: She rejects the suggestion to "let the NGO stuff go" and sets her own limit of fieldwork "every other Saturday," acting on her own judgment instead of others' pressure.
rationales.universalism: She refuses to pause the water-readings work because otherwise "the block office gets to keep ignoring it," staying focused on a public-health problem bigger than herself.

rationales.hedonism: She slept "maybe four hours" and worked until 2 AM, sacrificing rest to redo the module.
rationales.achievement: She rewrote multiple passages overnight and immediately tested them with students, showing intense commitment to improving performance.
rationales.universalism: She replaces "summer camps and snow days" with familiar contexts because the standard material assumes a different childhood and leaves her students at a disadvantage.

rationales.achievement: The revised module moves the pass rate from "38% to 61%," and she carefully documents the evidence while also being flagged for district recognition.
rationales.universalism: She keeps pushing both the water case and the argument that the standardized test is biased, using data to challenge systems that harm students.

rationales.achievement: She says she "genuinely cannot take three days off right now" because she has to prep students for boards, putting the work first.
rationales.tradition: Skipping Pongal because of school prep puts a major family and cultural observance in direct conflict with her current choices.
rationales.benevolence: She hears her mother's disappointed silence and still cannot come home, so the cost lands on a close relationship.

rationales.hedonism: She says "the water report matters more than my comfort" and her students' futures matter more than her sleep schedule, explicitly putting wellbeing behind the work.
rationales.achievement: She submits an "Emerging Educator" portfolio and includes a data-backed argument about gains, showing continued focus on performance and results.
rationales.benevolence: She missed Pongal, has not called her grandmother in three weeks, and ate dinner at her desk all week, letting close relationships absorb the cost.
rationales.universalism: She spent six months of volunteer effort getting the school water issue acted on and frames her work as caring about things "bigger than your own life."

rationales.security: He immediately runs the numbers on the new pay structure and calculates he would need "four extra hours a day" to protect his income.
He abandons the routine that "already works," pays an unnecessary deposit, and creates confusion that costs time and gas, undermining his stable setup.
He deletes the extra app, goes back to the two he knows, logs earnings, and plans affordable brake maintenance, restoring a predictable system.

rationales.achievement: He fixates on reaching the "thirty deliveries by Friday" streak bonus and keeps pushing toward that measurable target.
rationales.security: He knowingly rides with brakes "grinding metal on metal" and delays the repair despite remembering how expensive that mistake was before.
He finally pays for the brake repair and values that "the bike stops clean now," prioritizing reliable and safe equipment.
He refuses to risk "two months of savings" on Andres's food truck and is clear that "the money stays where it is."
Three missing deliveries mean "maybe sixty mil gone," and he immediately files a support ticket because lost income threatens his stability.
He abandons the routes he knows for a shortcut, gets a one-star rating, and knows that one unnecessary risk can reduce future orders and income.
He moves the 130 mil bonus to savings immediately and tracks whether May will cover June's bike insurance without touching that cushion.
He pays bike insurance before it is due, keeps savings at their highest since December, and plans the plumbing repair "one thing at a time."

rationales.tradition: "exactly how Nani used to make it" and planning to drape Amma's sari for Diwali show deliberate preservation of family recipes and rituals across generations.
rationales.benevolence: She cooks for Amma's birthday and says helping with the sari is "our thing" because her mother's hands shake, showing active care for a close family member.

rationales.hedonism: "Double shift again" and not remembering "the last time I sat down for a proper meal" show repeated self-denial and pushing pleasure aside.
rationales.security: She keeps taking extra shifts because of "medical bills," a struggling shop, and "Meera's wedding fund," prioritizing financial stability over comfort.
rationales.tradition: "This is what daughters do" frames her decisions as fulfilling an inherited family duty rather than choosing freely.
rationales.benevolence: She works exhausting hours to support her parents, brother, and sister, and feels pained about missing Appa's call, showing strong care for close family.

rationales.self_direction: "Meera chose it. That matters more than a wedding date" clearly endorses her daughter's right to choose her own path over social pressure.
rationales.conformity: She "just smiled and nodded because that is what you do," restraining disagreement to keep the interaction smooth.
Skipping puja because she did not want "everyone asking about the family" pulls away from an expected communal ritual and its social norms.
rationales.tradition: "Skipped the temple this morning" and avoiding the puja despite guilt show an active break from a valued religious routine.

rationales.benevolence: She uses her break to sit with Mrs. Okonkwo, hold her hand, and bring chai, giving personal comfort beyond her required duties.
rationales.universalism: "We are not so different ... both far from home" shows empathy that reaches beyond her own family or group to a broader human connection.

rationales.security: Her pain around Meera moving to Toronto and longing for family to stay within "calling distance" show a strong pull toward stable, nearby relationships.
rationales.tradition: She measures the choice against how "all the women in our family stayed close," showing attachment to inherited family patterns and continuity.
Teaching the "recipe from my mother's side" and retelling stories about Nani are explicit acts of passing down family customs.
rationales.benevolence: She spends two hours teaching Meera before she goes and answers all her questions about Nani, investing time and care in a close relationship.

rationales.tradition: "Three generations passing the same batter between their hands" frames the recipe as a family inheritance being deliberately carried forward.
rationales.benevolence: Sending the recipe to her mother and sharing it onward to Meera maintains a close, caring bond across the family.

rationales.stimulation: "Book a flight somewhere, anywhere" because "the walls are closing in" shows a strong need for novelty and escape from routine.
rationales.security: Summer break at home feels intolerable rather than comforting, so stable downtime is experienced as stifling instead of reassuring.

rationales.self_direction: "Nothing to run, nothing to decide" bothered him, and saying yes immediately reflects a pull toward acting with autonomy and control over his work.
rationales.stimulation: He says he has been "rotting at home" and that sitting around "feels like drowning," showing a strong need for activity and change.
rationales.power: The word "lead" hooked him instantly, and he wanted "something with my name on it," showing attraction to authority and visible ownership.

rationales.achievement: "Being good at something would be enough" and wanting initiative to be rewarded show a strong focus on competence and earned recognition.
rationales.power: He resents being unable to "actually lead" a committee he was asked to run, showing frustration when authority is constrained.
rationales.conformity: He bristles at being told "you're still new here" and resists the expectation that he should quietly defer to senior colleagues.
rationales.tradition: His complaint about promotions going by seniority and capability mattering less than "being old" rejects age-based hierarchy and established order.

rationales.self_direction: He still insists "The outdoor assembly IS a better idea," showing confidence in his own judgment even after rethinking his approach.
rationales.stimulation: "I move fast because slow feels unbearable" and "Sometimes I just need to move to think" show a clear need for motion and intensity.
rationales.conformity: He recognizes others "needed to feel heard" and reaches out to Ms. Liu after the meeting, adapting his behavior to preserve cooperation.
rationales.benevolence: Messaging Ms. Liu to get boba and work through the logistics together shows active care for the relationship, not just winning the argument.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I should have said something" but "froze," showing she acted against her own judgment instead of asserting it.
rationales.power: Watching Amna present "my slides" and then staying silent left her without control over her own work or credit.
rationales.conformity: She withheld a public challenge because it would "look petty," restraining herself to fit the meeting's social norms.

rationales.power: The hardest part is "having no way to speed it up," showing frustration at being unable to influence a decision that affects her work.
rationales.conformity: She avoids pushing harder because it would make her look "desperate" or like she was "hovering," so she restrains herself to protect appearances.

rationales.self_direction: She believed the new schedule fixed a real problem but still "backed down," acting against her own judgment under public pressure.
rationales.power: Having Farah challenge her loudly on the floor and then revisiting the rollout undercuts her authority to make the call.
rationales.conformity: She chose the reasonable, non-confrontational response because fighting publicly "would have made me look worse," prioritizing social restraint.

rationales.achievement: Even after a "Long day" and Eid shopping, she comes home to finish weekly reports and review tomorrow's brief, showing disciplined follow-through.
rationales.tradition: "Eid shopping with Ammi" ties the day to an established religious and family practice.

rationales.self_direction: "The ability to move fast without asking permission" is what she says she is protecting, making autonomy the clearest signal in the entry.
rationales.power: Each new gatekeeper feels like "ground lost," and having to go through Bilal's assistant reduces her control over how work gets done.

rationales.achievement: She answers "every single" client question without hesitation and wins the buyer's confidence by the end, showing strong competence under scrutiny.
rationales.power: Being introduced as "one of our operations team" stings because she sees herself as the person who runs the floor, showing a strong need for recognized standing.

rationales.achievement: "I've already earned this" centers her sense that her competence and work should already merit recognition.
rationales.power: She is upset that others do not "see it the way I do," showing a strong need for acknowledged standing rather than quiet patience.

rationales.achievement: "Secured better terms on the denim lot" and getting confirmation that the scheduling format is working reflect concrete performance success.
She emphasizes the "three weeks mapping the entire cycle" and wants the proposal recognized for its full scope, showing investment in demonstrated competence.
rationales.power: "Loop in procurement" bothers her because she will "lose sole ownership," making loss of control the central conflict.
Being treated like she is just "keeping busy" undermines the status she wants for her role, and she cannot fully correct the room.
rationales.conformity: She "smiled and ate my biryani" instead of lecturing her relatives, restraining herself to keep the social event smooth.

rationales.hedonism: He "slept until 10," made "a proper French press coffee," and enjoyed perfect weather with no lesson plans, explicitly treating simple pleasure and comfort as "enough."

rationales.self_direction: Choosing a cabin with "no cell service for five days" shows a deliberate break from constant availability and outside demands.
rationales.hedonism: "Best decision we've made in months" frames the unplugged cabin trip as a major source of enjoyment and relief.
rationales.benevolence: Spending "the week at a cabin in Wisconsin with Jenna" prioritizes shared time with a close partner.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects the department head's pressure, saying "I'm not doing it this year" and choosing to ignore the email to protect his own summer.
rationales.hedonism: He defends "protect[ing] their own time" instead of reworking curriculum in July, prioritizing rest and enjoyment over extra work.
rationales.conformity: Calling the sessions "'optional'... because we all know how that goes" and refusing to participate pushes against the school's expected compliance.

rationales.stimulation: He is drawn to "cooking something complicated" and describes the demanding four-hour process as "the point," showing enjoyment of immersive challenge rather than easy convenience.
rationales.hedonism: He says "the four hours weren't a cost - they were the point" and savors "just saffron and timing," treating the sensory experience itself as the reward.

rationales.conformity: She interrupted a meeting's testing agenda to argue for different material, then noticed she felt like she had "spoken out of turn," showing willingness to break the expected line.
rationales.universalism: She wanted content on "different cultures" because students' families came from "Syria or Turkey or Eritrea," reflecting concern for inclusion beyond the dominant curriculum.
Her distress centers on "microplastics found in baby formula" and on systems that "are supposed to protect people" but do not, showing concern for children's welfare at a broad social level.

rationales.benevolence: She spent time helping Amira with reading homework and celebrated that "she finished a whole chapter book for the first time," focusing on one child's growth.
rationales.universalism: Volunteering "at the community center after school" reflects care for people beyond her immediate obligations, not just her own workload.

rationales.conformity: She "just nodded and ate" and later says "So I stay quiet" to avoid upsetting her dad, restraining herself to preserve harmony.
rationales.benevolence: She keeps silent mainly to protect "my dad" and their relationship, prioritizing a close tie over airing her full view.
rationales.universalism: She is explicitly concerned that "the river matters" and that "people who fish in it" and "kids who swim in it matter," showing concern beyond her family.

She frames her "worrying about the world" as "paying attention," listens to podcasts about "ocean acidification," and goes out of her way to buy organic, all pointing to sustained concern for environmental harm.
Sorting donated coats and repeating Fatima's line that "you help because the person in front of you is cold" reflects direct concern for vulnerable people in the wider community.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I stopped waiting for permission" and "just did it myself, ten minutes, no approval needed," showing independent action inside the system.
rationales.universalism: The questions she opens up, like "why some people do not have houses" and "if fish can feel pain," show active concern with social and ethical issues larger than herself.

rationales.conformity: She openly challenged a colleague at lunch and says she does not want to be "the person who stays quiet when it matters," showing readiness to disrupt smooth social consensus.
rationales.universalism: She insists climate content belongs in school because "the climate is not political, it is physical reality," grounding her stance in broader environmental concern.

rationales.self_direction: He sticks with "the tracking sheets I designed" and says "my system" works while the manual does not, showing confidence in his own judgment over imposed procedure.
rationales.hedonism: He stayed late and took on extra Christmas-Eve paperwork himself, sacrificing comfort and time off to keep operations running.
rationales.achievement: He insists "the numbers are right because I checked them" and stays late to ensure the reports are correct, emphasizing competence and standards.
rationales.conformity: Using his old sheets "instead of the new corporate software" and ignoring the manual resists the expected company process.

rationales.self_direction: He filters top-down directives for himself, keeping only the parts that "actually apply to us" and letting "the rest fade."
rationales.security: "Keep doing what works" and his focus on processes that reliably function show a strong preference for stability over imposed change.
rationales.conformity: He outwardly attends the alignment meetings but intentionally adopts only "about thirty percent" and discards the rest, resisting full compliance.
rationales.tradition: He embraces methods others call "old-fashioned," saying the whiteboard stays because "old-fashioned works," which favors continuity with established ways.

rationales.self_direction: He independently audits the new software, gathers local data, and says "If their software improves that number, I will use it. If not..." rather than deferring automatically.
rationales.achievement: He pulls "data from the last two quarters" and anchors the case in a "94% on-time delivery rate," focusing on measurable performance.
rationales.conformity: Instead of simply accepting the February mandate, he and Sergio plan to document "everything that does not work" and challenge it with specifics.
rationales.benevolence: He collaborates with Sergio and helps younger workers understand that "experience teaches you the exceptions," showing attention to colleagues' practical needs.

rationales.self_direction: He spent extra time building a case from data and rejects a decision made for "uniformity" over what actually works, showing strong resistance to outside control.
rationales.achievement: He and Sergio "stayed late preparing the presentation" and centered it on efficiency and results, signaling commitment to competent performance.
rationales.conformity: He is frustrated that leadership wants "standardization regardless of the data," which directly conflicts with his push against blank uniformity.

rationales.self_direction: He manually overrides the software because it ignores local realities and says "I know what works here," privileging on-the-ground judgment over handed-down rules.
rationales.conformity: His complaint that "they want compliance, not solutions" shows active resistance to simply following the imposed system.
rationales.benevolence: When "the younger staff kept asking me how to work around the glitches," he became the person helping them adapt, showing practical support for his team.

rationales.self_direction: Said driving for Grab turned permanent because he was "scared to go back" to performance reviews and promotions, choosing the road over office hierarchy.
rationales.stimulation: Said riding "beats sitting in an air-conditioned office" and reflected on needing "the motion, the doing," showing attraction to movement over routine.
rationales.achievement: Said he was "scared I'd become someone who measures his worth in promotions again," explicitly rejecting recognition-based success.

rationales.self_direction: Told his brother-in-law "not everyone needs a business card to feel important," asserting his own standard instead of accepting the family's status script.
rationales.power: Dismissed the need for a "business card" to feel important, directly rejecting status markers and superiority games.

rationales.benevolence: He spends Saturdays teaching Ramesh English and felt "stupidly proud" when he said "bureaucracy" correctly, showing sustained care for someone close enough to know personally.
rationales.universalism: He reflects on migrant workers, donors, wealthy customers, and the demolition of Geylang shophouses, insisting "someone should notice what we're losing."

rationales.security: Being "soaked through three times" while the app crashed and surge bonuses vanished highlights how exposed and unstable this work is, even to him.

rationales.achievement: Worked a "twelve-hour shift" and explicitly noted he "hit my weekly target," showing effort toward a measurable standard.
rationales.benevolence: Said dinner with Priya felt like "us again" and that these moments of being present with her are "what I'm working for," centering the relationship over himself.

rationales.stimulation: Delivering "thirty identical gift baskets" until "my back was screaming" turns the day into deadening repetition rather than novelty or challenge.

rationales.security: He keeps returning to "the uncertainty, the lack of a plan" and says "having no plan is its own kind of trap," showing a pull toward stability and assurance.

rationales.self_direction: Said "some of us just refuse to let corporations define what success looks like," insisting on his own criteria instead of institutional ones.
rationales.achievement: The complaint about gig workers "lacking ambition" is rejected outright, showing active resistance to conventional success and striving metrics.

rationales.self_direction: He wonders whether this life is just "afraid of commitment dressed up as philosophy" and says he "walked away from choices," framing his path as avoidance rather than self-authorship.
rationales.security: Priya's fears about injury, regulation changes, and retirement land with him, and he starts admiring the courage of a more dependable life "day after day."
rationales.benevolence: He cannot argue once he sees Priya is "scared" and ends up dwelling on ordinary scenes of families showing up for each other.
rationales.universalism: He contrasts his chosen instability with "the men at the shelter" who have "no choice," recognizing the broader inequality in their situations.

rationales.conformity: He told himself "it's a party, don't make it weird," prioritizing social smoothness over confrontation.
rationales.universalism: He "let this man talk about them like they were a problem" even though he knows the asylum-seeker families by name and circumstance.
He knowingly bought the plastic-heavy sandwich because he was hungry and made the "lazy choice," despite understanding the environmental harm and fearing he's becoming someone who only talks.

rationales.conformity: He "pretended I was busy with the coffee machine" instead of disrupting the room after the joke, choosing social ease.
rationales.universalism: Staying silent while a colleague mocked "a Somali student's accent" leaves prejudice against an outsider unchallenged.

rationales.hedonism: He ordered Foodora twice because he "couldn't face making pasta," letting immediate comfort and relief from exhaustion drive the choice.
rationales.universalism: He knowingly used chains that "treat their delivery riders badly" so he would not have to cook, putting convenience over concern for those workers.

rationales.achievement: He seriously weighs the liaison role because it would be "good for my CV" and admits he liked "being chosen."
rationales.universalism: Considering work with Vattenfall despite naming its lignite history and harm to "indigenous Sami land rights" pulls against his stated concern for broader people and impacts.
